posterior reconstruction of a given contribution to the correlation function (or g̊Asubscript̊𝑔𝐴mathringgA) and the “data” superscript denotes the numerical data. The full correlation between the numerical data and posteriors are used in these reconstruction. The 2pt + sc is the sum of the first two terms and the 3pt is the sum of all excited states. The FH(tsep,τc=1)A3/g̊AA3(trm sep,tauc=1)/mathringgA keys correspond to the same breakdown of various excited state contributions after they are passed through Eq. . From these plots, there are several observations and conclusions one can make: 1. There is a significant cancellation between the scattering excited states and two-point excited states; 2. The transition excited states are the dominant source of excited states and they are relatively suppressed in FHA3​(tsep,τc)subscriptFHsubscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sepsubscript𝜏𝑐rm FHA3(trm sep,tauc) as compared to RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2); 3. The total excited state contamination at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm is ≈8%absentpercent8approx 8% for RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2) and ≈1%absentpercent1approx 1% for FHA3​(tsep,τc)subscriptFHsubscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sepsubscript𝜏𝑐rm FHA3(trm sep,tauc). In Eq. we see, from the proportionality of the nthsuperscript𝑛thnrm th excited state to gn​nΓ−g00Γsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝑛𝑛Γsuperscriptsubscript𝑔00ΓgnnGamma-g00Gamma, an explicit cancellation between the scattering (3pt sc) and two-point excited state (2pt es) contamination. For this cancellation to be significant, as observed in the posterior determination of the two classes of excited states, one explanation is that gn​nΓ≈g00Γsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝑛𝑛Γsuperscriptsubscript𝑔00ΓgnnGammaapprox g00Gamma for all n𝑛n. For the vector current, we know that in infinite volume, the vector operator measures the iso-vector charge of the system, which in the isospin limit, is exactly equal to g00V4=1superscriptsubscript𝑔00subscript𝑉41g00V4=1 (up to renormalization). It has further been demonstrated that in the forward scattering limit, the finite volume interaction amplitudes conspire with the finite volume Lellouch-Lüscher factors to ensure that gn​nV4=g00V4superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑛𝑛subscript𝑉4superscriptsubscript𝑔00subscript𝑉4gnnV4=g00V4 for two-particle statescitebib.bib103. This result, while not surprising, demonstrates charge conservation in finite volume by allowing one to relate n𝑛n-point Greens functions to (n−1)𝑛1(n-1)-point Greens functions through the Ward-Takahashi equations. In the case of the axial-vector matrix element, it is plausible that in the forward scattering limit, the corrections to such a relation arising from the partially-conserved nature of the axial-vector current would also vanish, such that in this limit, the Lellouch-Lüscher factors are similarly removed. For example, for the nucleon, we know the induced pseudo-scalar form factor, which would provide a correction to the Ward-Takahashi equations, vanishes in the forward limit. We leave a detailed study of this question to future work. In the left panel of Fig. we see that the scattering excited state contribution (the upper (red) band/data) and the contribution from the excited states coming from the two-point function (the middle (purple) band/data) are roughly equal and opposite in sign. The hatched curve, which is mostly white with gray hash lines, is the sum of these two contributions and it lies between them. We observe that the sum of these two sources of excited state contributions decay to a 1% correction (the horizontal dashed (red) lines) at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm. In contrast, the transition (3pt tr) excited states depicted by the lowest (blue) band/data do not decay to the 1% level until tsep≈2.2subscript𝑡sep2.2trm sepapprox 2.2fm. In the right panel of Fig. we show the same excited state breakdown for the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function. The sign of the scattering and two-point excited state contributions both change compared to RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2). This sign change can be understood from Eq. . It is also interesting to note that the magnitude of the transition excited state contributions at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm go from ≈7.5%absentpercent7.5approx 7.5% for RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2) to ≈2.5%absentpercent2.5approx 2.5% for FH(tsep,τc=1)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=1). Finally, for RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2), the sum of the scattering and two-point excited state contributions is the same sign as the sum of transition excited states, while for FH(tsep,τc=1)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=1) it is the opposite sign at intermediate and large tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep. Thus, the total excited state contamination decays to the 1% level for the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm while this does not happen until tsep>2subscript𝑡sep2trm sep>2fm for RA3​(tsep,τ=tsep/2)subscript𝑅subscript𝐴3subscript𝑡sep𝜏subscript𝑡sep2RA3(trm sep,tau=trm sep/2). There is no proof that this fortunate cancellation must happen. However, an examination of our results in Ref. citebib.bib31 shows a consistent picture that the excited state contamination of the FH(tsep,τc=none)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=rm none) correlation function decays to the 1% level by roughly 1 fm over a broad range of pion masses, 130≲mπ≲400less-than-or-similar-to130subscript𝑚𝜋less-than-or-similar-to400130lesssim mpilesssim 400MeV.
content_sections.III Lattice Calculation III.4 Comparis: In this section, we perform the correlator analysis on late time separation data, tsep=[10,12,14]subscript𝑡sep101214trm sep=[10,12,14], which is directly comparable to the results in Refs. citebib.bib27, bib.bib34 that were performed on the same ensembles, and otherwise mimicking the more common strategy used for example in Refs. citebib.bib18, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib37. The results are obtained with a simultaneous fit to the two- and three-point functions using the same priors as our main fit, given in App. . We also demonstrate the sensitivity of the posterior distributions of the ground state parameters to changes in the input priors as in App. . Because we have only three values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep, we use a minimal number of excited states: with one excited state, there is only one degree of freedom in describing the tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep dependence of RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau), after which, the analysis relies upon the priors for the excited state matrix elements. With the quark smearing we have used we find that we must apply a relatively aggressive truncation on the current insertion time in order for the model to describe the numerical data, restricting the analysis to the “center” of the current insertion time τ𝜏tau. We illustrate the region of τ𝜏tau used for each tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep in Fig. and depict the stability of the ground state axial matrix element in Fig. . The optimal result is chosen as the fit with the largest amount of numerical data while maintaining a good fit quality (cfr. the lower panel of Fig. ). We refer to the optimal choice of data included for this 2-state model fit as τinc=τincoptsubscript𝜏incsuperscriptsubscript𝜏incopttaurm inc=taurm incrm opt, but we also show results for the ground state posteriors when one more (τincopt+1superscriptsubscript𝜏incopt1taurm incrm opt+1) or one less (τincopt−1superscriptsubscript𝜏incopt1taurm incrm opt-1) value of τ𝜏tau is included in the fit. While not depicted, the ground state posteriors are also stable under variation of the choice of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintrm seprm min in the two-point function. We observe that for this specific dataset, the late-tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep result is in ≈2​σabsent2𝜎approx 2sigma tension with the result from the full analysis that includes more values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep, and is clearly stable over a variety of data truncations and model variations. This is indicative that late-tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep data can be subject to correlated fluctuations which are difficult to identify without having results at many values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep. The same systematic effect can be seen in a simpler case using a fit to the two-point correlation function. In Fig. , we show the result of the ground state energy extracted from the two-point correlation function under varying tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintrm seprm min. We highlight the best fit ground-state energy, which is supported by a robust plateau in the dimension of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintrm seprm min with high Q𝑄Q-value. However, we see that at late time beyond 1fm, a second plateau develops approximately two standard deviations above our best fit. This second plateau cannot be physical and simply reached later in time due to latent excited state contamination, as the 2-pt correlator is positive definite and therefore required to approach the ground state from above. This second “stable” plateau is also the logical result for a one-state fit. Analogous to the three-point analysis, if the model fails to describe excited-state contaminations, the model must extract ground-state parameters at late time. These values are then sensitive to uncontrolled statistical fluctuations. Also note that we observe the fluctuation in the effective mass occurs approximately 0.2fm later than the matrix elements, see the top panel of Fig. in App. . Similar to the three-point analysis, in the absence of a more holistic view as granted by analyzing more source-sink separation data, there is no measure on the size of how large the potential underestimation of errors are. Without using more than three values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep, it is not possible to identify if one is susceptible to such a fluctuation, and therefore, it is not possible to fully quantify the uncertainty on the posterior distribution of the ground state parameters.
content_sections.III Lattice Calculation III.5 Compariso: In this final section, we compare our result with that from Ref. citebib.bib31 on the same a09m310 ensemble. Those results were obtained using the Feynman-Hellmann method as described in Ref. citebib.bib48, which is the same as Eq. with the sum over current insertion time running over the full time extent, including contributions from contact operators at τ=[0,tsep]𝜏0subscript𝑡septau=[0,trm sep] and from out-of-time region, τtsep𝜏subscript𝑡septau>trm sep. We applied a two-state model and a frequentist analysis on the data from the two-point and Feynman-Hellmann correlation functions. In Fig. we show the extracted value of g̊Asubscript̊𝑔𝐴mathringgA with three different fully-correlated analysis strategies: 23s : Fit to C2​(tsep)subscript𝐶2subscript𝑡sepC2(trm sep), RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau) and FH(tsep,τc=1)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc=1); 2s : Fit to C2​(tsep)subscript𝐶2subscript𝑡sepC2(trm sep) and FH(tsep,τc=1)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc=1); 23 : Fit to C2​(tsep)subscript𝐶2subscript𝑡sepC2(trm sep) and RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau). The horizontal gray band is the result from Ref. citebib.bib31. These results show the consistency between the four computational and analysis strategies. Even though the FH(tsep,τc)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc) correlation functions are constructed from the RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau) correlation functions, they are subject to a different pattern of excited state contamination (cfr. Sec. ). Therefore it is useful to include them in the fully-correlated analysis. The combined fit yields a robust and consistent fitting strategy and precise extractions of hadronic matrix elements at zero-momentum transfer. In Fig. , we present the posterior correlation function on top of the numerical data for both the results in Ref. citebib.bib31 as well as from the FH(tsep,τc=1)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=1) dataset in the present work. Using the bootstrap results, the correlated ratio of gAsubscript𝑔𝐴gA determined previously and now is gA​Changetal. gA​[present​work]=1.009​,subscript𝑔𝐴Changetal. subscript𝑔𝐴delimited-presentwork1.00931fracgAtextcite[cite]@@bibrefAuthors Phrase1YearPhrase2Chang:2018uxx@@citephrase(@@citephrase)gA[rm presentwork]=1.009,, showing the statistical consistency of the ground state matrix element extracted from these two different methods on the same ensemble. The FH(tsep,τc=none)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=rm none) dataset from Ref. citebib.bib31 is observed to have significantly less excited state contamination at early tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep as compared to FH(tsep,τc=1)A3A3(trm sep,tauc=1). This is what enabled a two-state analysis in Ref. citebib.bib31. The combined fit (23s) in the present work enables a determination of significantly more excited state parameters through the precise early-tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep data. Moreover, as shown in this section, a confident extraction of the ground state posterior distributions is attained: as depicted in the figure, both strategies yield precise and consistent extractions of the large-tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep extrapolation of the results. The former result is more economical for obtaining a precise value of g̊Asubscript̊𝑔𝐴mathringgA, while the method in this work also enables a determination of non-zero momentum transfer results which can be used to determine the form factors.
content_sections.IV Observations and Conclusions: In this work, we have computed the three-point correlation functions that are used to determine the nucleon axial charge for 13 values of the source-sink separation time in the range tsep≈0.17−1.22subscript𝑡sep0.171.22trm sepapprox 0.17-1.22fm on an ensemble with a≈0.09𝑎0.09aapprox 0.09fm and mπ≈310subscript𝑚𝜋310mpiapprox 310MeV. This large numerical dataset (Fig. ) has enabled us to robustly determine the ground state mass and matrix elements with a fully-quantified systematic uncertainty arising from excited states. Important to this study is the use of a fully-correlated global minimization of the two-point and respective three-point correlations functions. We were further able to quantify the excited state contribution to the correlation function with greater detail than has been previously achieved in the literature. We were able to demonstrate that the ground state parameters are stable against the model of excited states (Eq. and Fig. ), the number of excited states used in the analysis, and truncations of either small tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep or large tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep results as well as through the use of only even or odd values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep results (Fig. ). A re-writing of the spectral decomposition of the three-point correlation functions revealed a prospective cancellation between two classes of excited states arising from the n𝑛n-to-n𝑛n scattering states and those arising from the two-point correlation function used to construct RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau), Eq. (there is an exact cancellation for the vector matrix element for which gn​nV4=g00V4=1superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑛𝑛subscript𝑉4superscriptsubscript𝑔00subscript𝑉41gnnV4=g00V4=1 up to renormalization). For our calculation, this cancellation seems to materialize for gAsubscript𝑔𝐴gA (Fig. ) leaving the dominant excited state contributions to be the n𝑛n-to-m𝑚m transition terms. In the standard fixed source-sink separation method, the excited state contributions do not decay down to the 1% contamination level until tsep≳2greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑡sep2trm sepgtrsim 2fm while in the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function, Eq. , they decay to the 1% level at tsep≈1subscript𝑡sep1trm sepapprox 1fm. We further observed that the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function constructed with a sum over the entire time extent of the latticecitebib.bib48 (FH(tsep,τc=none)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc=rm none)), rather than just between the source and the sink (FH(tsep,τc=1)ΓGamma(trm sep,tauc=1)), leads to an even further suppression of excited state contamination (Fig. ). This stronger cancellation of excited states enabled us to compute gAsubscript𝑔𝐴gA with a ≈1%absentpercent1approx 1% uncertainty by utilizing early-time datacitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, which demonstrated this higher suppression of excited states over a broad range of pion masses, 130≲mπ≲400less-than-or-similar-to130subscript𝑚𝜋less-than-or-similar-to400130lesssim mpilesssim 400MeV. By utilizing a large number of early to mid-time data, one is able to detect if the late-time data is subject to a correlated fluctuation which might otherwise bias the determination of the ground state matrix elements (Figs. and ). With only three values of tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep at late time, it is not possible to perform such a data truncation study that could identify this issue. We have found that including the Feynman-Hellmann correlation functions in a global fully correlated analysis, even though they are constructed from the three-point functions as in Eq. and are highly correlated with RΓ​(tsep,τ)subscript𝑅Γsubscript𝑡sep𝜏RGamma(trm sep,tau), improves the stability and precision of the extracted ground state parameters (Fig. ). This improvement arises because the excited states present themselves differently in the two sets of matrix-element correlation functions. Even though the Feynman-Hellmann correlation function has greater noise than the standard three-point correlator at equal tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep and statistics (compare, for example, tsep=1subscript𝑡sep1trm sep=1fm. in Fig. ), the more rapid decay of excited states demonstrated in Sec. means that this strategy mitigates the exponential degradation of the signal-to-noise of the nucleon’s two- and three-point correlation functions as tsepsubscript𝑡septrm sep is increased, which requires exponentially more computational resources to control the stochastic precision. The strategy we present in this work offers a more economical method of obtaining the ground state matrix elements than that which is more commonly advocated for in the literature, which is to use high-statistics calculations at tsep≈1−2subscript𝑡sep12trm sepapprox 1-2fm or larger. In future work, we will investigate the same strategy for non-zero momentum transfer correlation functions which are used to determine the nucleon form factors. Data availability: The computations were performed utilizing LaLiBecitebib.bib104 which utilizes the Chroma software suitecitebib.bib92 with QUDA solverscitebib.bib105, bib.bib106 and HDF5citebib.bib107 for I/Ocitebib.bib108. They were efficiently managed with METAQcitebib.bib109, bib.bib110 and status of tasks logged with EspressoDBcitebib.bib111. The final extrapolation analysis utilized gvarcitebib.bib112 and lsqfitcitebib.bib113. The analysis and data for this work can be found at . Acknowledgements. We thank O. Bär, R. Briceño, R. Gupta, M. Hansen and A. Jackura for enlightening conversations and correspondence. We thank B. Hörz for help cross-checking the three-point code in LaLiBe and generating some of the results. We thank the MILC Collaboration for use of their HISQ gauge ensembles. sorted_papers.title:
$\Delta I = 3/2$ and $\Delta I = 1/2$ channels of $K\to\pi\pi$ decay at
  the physical point with periodic boundary conditions
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice calculation of the $K\to\pi\pi$ matrix elements and
amplitudes with both the $\Delta I = 3/2$ and 1/2 channels and $\varepsilon'$,
the measure of direct $CP$ violation. We use periodic boundary conditions
(PBC), where the correct kinematics of $K\to\pi\pi$ can be achieved via an
excited two-pion final state. To overcome the difficulty associated with the
extraction of excited states, our previous work \cite{Bai:2015nea,RBC:2020kdj}
successfully employed G-parity boundary conditions, where pions are forced to
have non-zero momentum enabling the $I=0$ two-pion ground state to express the
on-shell kinematics of the $K\to\pi\pi$ decay. Here instead we overcome the
problem using the variational method which allows us to resolve the two-pion
spectrum and matrix elements up to the relevant energy where the decay
amplitude is on-shell.
  In this paper we report an exploratory calculation of $K\to\pi\pi$ decay
amplitudes and $\varepsilon'$ using PBC on a coarser lattice size of
$24^3\times64$ with inverse lattice spacing $a^{-1}=1.023$ GeV and the physical
pion and kaon masses. The results are promising enough to motivate us to
continue our measurements on finer lattice ensembles in order to improve the
precision in the near future.

sections.100: Standard Model Prediction for Direct CP Violation in K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋K\to\pi\pi Decay.
sections.101: π𝜋\pi-π𝜋\pi scattering, QED, and finite-volume quantization.
sections.102: Weak transition matrix elements from finite volume correlation functions.
sections.103: Two particle states on a torus and their relation to the scattering matrix.

qualitative: The variational method is employed to resolve the two-pion spectrum and matrix elements up to the on-shell energy for K→ππ decay, enabling the use of periodic boundary conditions (PBC) without requiring G-parity boundary conditions.

Excited-state contamination is controlled by using the variational method, which allows for the extraction of the ground state and relevant excited states in the two-pion system, thereby isolating the physical on-shell decay amplitude.

Plateau fits are used in the analysis of correlation functions, with both single-state and two-state fits applied to assess the stability and consistency of the extracted matrix elements and to estimate systematic uncertainties from excited-state contributions.

The use of PBC with the variational method avoids the need for non-zero pion momentum enforced by G-parity conditions, while still achieving correct on-shell kinematics for the decay via excited two-pion states.

The success of the variational method in isolating the relevant energy region supports the validity of the plateau behavior in the effective mass and matrix element plots, indicating suppression of excited-state contamination.
quantitative: Lattice volume: $24^3 \times 64$
Inverse lattice spacing: $a^{-1} = 1.023$ GeV
Physical pion and kaon masses are used in the simulation
The analysis includes both single-state and two-state fits to correlation functions to control excited-state contamination
The variational method is applied to extract matrix elements up to the relevant on-shell energy for $K\to\pi\pi$ decay

content_sections.I Introduction: K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi decay is an ideal probe to search for new physics beyond the Standard Model (SM) as it contains charge-parity (C​P𝐶𝑃CP)-violating decay processes. The amount of C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation in the SM is believed to be too small to explain the dominance of matter over antimatter in the current universe. Particularly ε′superscript𝜀′varepsilonprime, the measure of direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation in K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi decay, is very sensitive to potential new sources of C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation. The experimental measurements were performed by the NA48citebib.bib3 and KTeVcitebib.bib4, bib.bib5 collaborations and their average is quoted as Re(ε′/ε)exp=16.6​(2.3)×10−4subscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀exp16.62.3superscript104(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm exp=16.6(2.3)times 10-4citebib.bib6, where ε𝜀varepsilon is the measure of indirect C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation |ε|=2.228​×10−3𝜀2.22811superscript103|varepsilon|=2.228times 10-3. Since the decay processes receive considerable nonperturbative QCD effects, lattice QCD should play a key role in giving a SM prediction of ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon and, in fact, the RBC and UKQCD collaborations have achieved the first ab initio SM prediction of this quantitycitebib.bib1, bib.bib2. Our most recent resultcitebib.bib2 is Re(ε′/ε)2020=21.7​(2.6)​(6.2)​(5.0)×10−4subscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀202021.72.66.25.0superscript104(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)2020=21.7(2.6)(6.2)(5.0)times 10-4citebib.bib2. The first two errors are statistical and systematic, respectively, but excluding the systematic error due to electromagnetic and isospin-breaking corrections, which is listed as the third error. While consistent with the experimental result, the relatively large uncertainty motivates further refinement of the calculation especially since the current error in the lattice calculation is a lot bigger than in the two decades old experimental determination. In the isospin limit ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon is determined by ε′ε=i​ω​ei​(δ2−δ0)2​ε​[Im​(A2)Re​(A2)−Im​(A0)Re​(A0)],superscript𝜀′𝜀i𝜔superscripteisubscript𝛿2subscript𝛿02𝜀delimited-Imsubscript𝐴2Resubscript𝐴2Imsubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴0fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilon=fracrmiomegarmermi(delta2-delta0)sqrt2varepsilonleft[fracmboxIm(A2)mboxRe(A2)-fracmboxIm(A0)mboxRe(A0)right], where δIsubscript𝛿𝐼deltaI is the S𝑆S-wave two-pion scattering phase shift with isospin I𝐼I at the energy of the kaon mass, and AI=⟨(π​π)I|HW|K0⟩subscript𝐴𝐼quantum-operator-productsubscript𝜋𝜋𝐼subscript𝐻𝑊superscript𝐾0AI=(pipi)I|HW|K0 is the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi decay amplitude defined with the weak Hamiltonian HWsubscript𝐻𝑊HW, kaon initial state |K0⟩ketsuperscript𝐾0|K0 and two-pion final state ⟨(π​π)I|brasubscript𝜋𝜋𝐼(pipi)I| with the definite isospin I𝐼I. The normalization of these states is clarified and the relations between the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes and decay rates are explicitly given in Appendix. In Eq. we also define ω=Re​(A2)/Re​(A0)𝜔Resubscript𝐴2Resubscript𝐴0omega=rm Re(A2)/rm Re(A0). We employ the three-flavor effective weak Hamiltonian with the formcitebib.bib7, bib.bib8 HW=GF2​Vu​s∗​Vu​d​∑i[zi​(μ)+τ​yi​(μ)]​Qi​(μ),subscript𝐻𝑊subscript𝐺𝐹2superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑subscript𝑖delimited-subscript𝑧𝑖𝜇𝜏subscript𝑦𝑖𝜇subscript𝑄𝑖𝜇HW=fracGFsqrt2Vus*Vudsumi[zi(mu)+tau yi(mu)]Qi(mu), where we define the Fermi constant GFsubscript𝐺𝐹GF, Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix elements Vq′​qsubscript𝑉superscript𝑞′𝑞Vqprimeq connecting up-type (q′superscript𝑞′qprime) and down-type (q𝑞q) quarks, their ratio τ=−Vt​s∗​Vt​d/Vu​s∗​Vu​d𝜏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑡𝑠subscript𝑉𝑡𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑tau=-Vts*Vtd/Vus*Vud, the Wilson coefficients zi​(μ)subscript𝑧𝑖𝜇zi(mu) and yi​(μ)subscript𝑦𝑖𝜇yi(mu) that encompass the effects of heavy particle fields that are integrated out, and the effective four-quark operators Qi​(μ)subscript𝑄𝑖𝜇Qi(mu) renormalized in the same scheme and scale, μ𝜇mu, as the Wilson coefficients. The effective operators Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Qi relevant for K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi have the strangeness-changing nature, Δ​S=1Δ𝑆1Delta S=1, and the dominant contributions are associated with the four-quark electroweak operators given in Eqs. –. The matrix elements ⟨(π​π)I|Qi|K0⟩quantum-operator-productsubscript𝜋𝜋𝐼subscript𝑄𝑖superscript𝐾0(pipi)I|Qi|K0 depend on nonperturbative QCD properties in the low-energy regime. Lattice QCD is the only known approach to computing non-perturbative physics that is systematically-improvable, i. e. formulae-sequence𝑖𝑒i. e. for which all of the systematic errors can be quantified and improved with sufficient computational effort. One long-standing obstacle in this subject, originally described in Ref. citebib.bib9, is that it is not straightforward to extract the unique energy-conserving on-shell matrix elements from Euclidean correlation functions at large time separations. In fact, in a periodic box and in the rest frame, the ground two-pion state in the rest frame comprises two pions at rest and has an energy near twice the pion mass, 2​mπ2subscript𝑚𝜋2mpi, which is not equal to the initial kaon state energy mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK. While using periodic boundary conditions (PBC) and extracting signals of the ground states in the rest frame is the most common approach to lattice calculations, the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements obtained in this way do not correspond to the energy-conserving on-shell matrix elements. In early attempts to compute K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi on the lattice, chiral perturbation theory (ChPT) was utilized to relate the on-shell K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements to some other matrix elements with unphysical kinematics that were accessible to numerical calculations at that timecitebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16. More recently, lattice calculations of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements with (nearly) on-shell kinematics have employed a different approach. The Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 process with the I=2𝐼2I=2 two-pion final state was computed with physical pion and kaon masses at a finite lattice spacingcitebib.bib17, bib.bib18 and in the continuum limitcitebib.bib19. In these works, the matrix elements of K+→π+​π+→superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋K+topi+pi+, which are related to A2subscript𝐴2A2 by the Wigner-Eckart theorem and isospin symmetry, were computed by imposing anti-periodic boundary conditions (APBC) for spatial directions on the down quark field. This results in the π+superscript𝜋pi+ states also satisfying APBC in those directions, and therefore having momenta discretized in odd-integer multiples of π/L𝜋𝐿pi/L, where L𝐿L is the lattice size. As a result, by tuning L𝐿L, the energy of the π+​π+superscript𝜋superscript𝜋pi+pi+ ground state matching the kaon mass mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK was realized. Calculation of the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 process with the I=0𝐼0I=0 two-pion final state is much more complicated due to the presence of many more diagrams, including noisy, disconnected contributions, possible mixing of the quark bilinear operators with the effective four-quark operators causing a power divergence, and so on. In addition, the APBC procedure is not applicable for this isospin channel because it only makes π+superscript𝜋pi+ and π−superscript𝜋pi- anti-periodic but π0superscript𝜋0pi0, which is also essential for the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel, is still periodic. Importantly, it also breaks isospin. For Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2, this is circumvented because π+​π+superscript𝜋superscript𝜋pi+pi+ cannot mix with other two-pion states due to charge conservation. For Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 there is no way to avoid this issue. For our previous calculations of the I=0 decay, we resolved this difficulty through the use of G-parity boundary conditions (GPBC)citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, which employ a combined charge-conjugation and isospin rotation to induce APBC on both charged and neutral pion states, while preserving the isospin symmetry. With this approach we were successfully able to calculate the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 process and ε′superscript𝜀′varepsilonprime. Besides the works by the RBC and UKQCD collaborations summarized above, a moving frame was employed to realize the physical kinematics with the ground two-pion final state by Ishizuka et al. citebib.bib22. They reported the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 and 3/2323/2 channels of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes near on-shell and Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) computed with Wilson fermions at unphysical pion and kaon masses. They utilized C​P​S𝐶𝑃𝑆CPS symmetrycitebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, which is the symmetry under C​P𝐶𝑃CP transformation followed by an interchange of the strange quark (S𝑆S) with the down quark, to ensure the absence of four-quark operator mixing with wrong chirality even with the Wilson fermion action. In this work, we aim to avoid the complexities of manipulating the boundary conditions and of moving frames, by employing PBC on an appropriately sized lattice such that the on-shell decay can be obtained via an excited two-pion state with energy Eπ​π≈mKsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋subscript𝑚𝐾Epipiapprox mK. We extract signals from an excited two-pion state with the energy near the kaon mass, Eπ​π≈mKsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋subscript𝑚𝐾Epipiapprox mK using the generalized eigenvalue problem (GEVP) methodcitebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, which provides a convenient method for isolating the contributions of individual low-lying states to Euclidean correlation functions. The GEVP method has been used for several calculations of matrix elements, for example for nucleon structurecitebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, B𝐵B physicscitebib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, light-meson radiative transitionscitebib.bib40, bib.bib41 and form factorscitebib.bib42. We can utilize this method not only for removing excited-state contamination from the ground-state signal but also for extracting signals from low-lying excited states. The latter is an important goal of this work and we have reported our successful extractions of a few excited two-pion states in the companion two-pion scattering papercitebib.bib43. The method also enables us to extract these signals at relatively short Euclidean times, where the signals of excited states could be still resolved, with the truncated excited-state contamination under control. Our previous GPBC workcitebib.bib2 also employed multiple two-pion interpolation operators to remove the excited-state contamination using multi-state fits. In our first GPBC calculation of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipicitebib.bib1, we only used a single π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like operator, a product of two single-pion operators projected to I=0𝐼0I=0. In Ref. citebib.bib2 we additionally introduced an iso-singlet scalar bilinear operator, which we call a σ𝜎sigma-like operator, and another π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like operator with the same total momentum but different constituent pion momenta. We then observed a significant change in the two-pion phase shift, K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements and ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon relative to our earlier calculationcitebib.bib1, which we attribute to excited-state contamination that was not formerly resolvable from the rapidly-growing statistical noise when measured with a single operator and lower statistics. The σ𝜎sigma-like operator in particular was shown to play a significant role in removing the excited-state contamination. For the three-operator basis employed in Ref. citebib.bib44, this fit-based approach was found to be equivalent in its resolution as the GEVP, but, unlike GEVP, also offered the flexibility to use a different number of states than operators to describe the two-pion correlation function in the region in which the excited-state contamination cannot be resolved from the noise. This ultimately proved important to obtain a result with minimal excited-state contamination. In our companion papercitebib.bib43 we describe a ”re-basing” strategy that also allows the GEVP approach to consider a lower number of state than operators. We believe that the GEVP will offer improved stability over a fit-based approach for larger numbers of operators and states, for which the covariance matrix may become ill conditioned. While computing ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon with a different setup is itself interesting as there have been very few lattice results despite its phenomenological importance, we expect some further benefits of using PBC for K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi calculation. To discuss this we here remark on two major systematic errors on ε′superscript𝜀′varepsilonprime estimated in our previous GPBC workcitebib.bib2: the finite lattice spacing error, and electromagnetic and isospin-breaking corrections. The first resulted from computing ε′superscript𝜀′varepsilonprime on a single, rather coarse lattice spacing of 1.38 GeV. Work is underway to repeat the GPBC calculation on two finer ensembles in order to take the continuum limit and remove this errorcitebib.bib45, bib.bib46. The necessity of generating ensembles for the specific purpose of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi calculation is requires an extra computational cost. With PBC, on the other hand, we have already generated finer ensemblescitebib.bib47, bib.bib48 with domain wall fermions at physical masses up to the inverse lattice spacing of 2.69GeV, which have been used for other various projects. We can use these ensembles for K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi calculation once the approach in the present work is found to be feasible. One potential obstacle to using these ensembles is that, since these ensembles were generated without tuning the volume for K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi calculation, physical kinematics cannot be precisely achieved with a two-pion state allowed in the given volume. Even though electromagnetic and isospin-breaking corrections are typically of order O​(1%)𝑂percent1O(1%), they are significant for ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon because of the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 rule. The Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 process is enhanced relative to Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 (Re​(A0)/Re​(A2)≈22.5Resubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴222.5rm Re(A0)/rm Re(A2)approx 22.5) because Re(A2subscript𝐴2A2) is suppressed by a factor of 10 due to non-perturbative dynamicscitebib.bib2 and a further factor of two from higher-energy kinematics. This results in a strong suppression of ε′superscript𝜀′varepsilonprime through the coefficient ω=Re​(A2)/Re​(A0)𝜔Resubscript𝐴2Resubscript𝐴0omega=mboxRe(A2)/mboxRe(A0) in Eq. and a corresponding 20×20times enhancement in the relative size of electromagnetic and isospin-breaking effects. These effects were estimated to be O​(20%)𝑂percent20O(20%) based on ChPT and the large-Ncsubscript𝑁𝑐Nc expansion of QCDcitebib.bib49, and while a direct prediction from Lattice QCD would certainly be useful, it is still inaccessible due to lack of a full formalism. This requires an extension of the formalism given by Lüschercitebib.bib50 and Lellouch-Lüschercitebib.bib51 that deals with two-hadron systems in finite volumes. While there are related studies on-goingcitebib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, we expect PBC is more suitable than GPBC to accomplish it because GPBC mixes the up and down quarks violating charge conservation. In this paper we present our first PBC results for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 and Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channels of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes and Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) on a 243×64superscript24364243times 64 ensemble with 2+1212+1-flavors of domain wall fermions with physical pion and kaon masses and an inverse lattice spacing of a−1=1.023​superscript𝑎11.0232a-1=1.023GeV. We employ the all-to-all (A2A) propagator methodcitebib.bib55 with 2,000 low modes for the light quark and spin-color-time diluted random noise for both the light and strange quarks. The all-mode averaging (AMA) techniquecitebib.bib56, bib.bib57 is also employed to accelerate the sampling. With the A2A procedure, AMA is implemented by reducing the number of configurations for exact calculations rather than the number of propagator sourcescitebib.bib43. A preparatory study of two-pion scattering with the same setup and GEVP was recently reportedcitebib.bib43. For two-pion interpolation operators we employ four π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like operators, products of two single pion operators, with different relative momenta for both the I=2𝐼2I=2 and I=0𝐼0I=0 channels and additionally one σ𝜎sigma-like scalar quark-bilinear operator for the I=0𝐼0I=0 channel. We employ a single kaon operator expecting the contamination from kaon excited states is less significant. We apply the Lellouch-Lüscher methodcitebib.bib51 to relate the two-pion state in the finite box with that in infinite volume. We use the RI-SMOM renormalization procedurecitebib.bib58 with step scalingcitebib.bib59 up to the renormalization scale of 4GeV. In general these details are similar to the previous GPBC calculationcitebib.bib2. We employ the same Möbius domain wall formalism and Iwasaki+DSDR gauge action as the GPBC calculation of the I=0𝐼0I=0 matrix element, and our physical volume is the same to within a percent. Besides the lattice spacing, the choice of boundary condition and two-pion operators, and the inclusion of the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 matrix element, the calculation is largely the same as the GPBC measurement, differing only in minor details such as the number of low-eigenmodes, the modifications to the A2A approach required in the G-parity case to treat the explicit flavor structure, and the use of cost-reduction techniques such as AMA and zMöbius (c. f. formulae-sequence𝑐𝑓c. f. , below). However, the differing finite-volume effects resulting from the change in boundary conditions result in an I=0𝐼0I=0 two-pion energy that does not as closely match the kaon energy, requiring an interpolation to on-shell kinematics. We use the lattice results for the matrix elements with the ground and first-excited two-pion final states for the interpolation and estimate the corresponding systematic error. For the convenience of the reader we summarize the primary results of this work in Table. We estimate various systematic errors. The systematic error due to electromagnetic and isospin-breaking corrections is listed for Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) as the third error. We inherit most of the systematic errors from Ref. citebib.bib2 with a few exceptions, which need a new estimation for the setup in the present work and are discussed in Section. Table also shows the corresponding experimental values for comparison, except Im(AI)subscript𝐴𝐼(AI), which are not accessible from experiments. The paper is organized as follows. Section briefly explains the lattice ensemble of gauge fields used in this study and measurement details. Section gives results for two-point functions including the kaon mass, a brief summary of the two-pion spectrum study in Ref. citebib.bib43, and the corresponding Lellouch-Lüscher factors. In Section we present the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi three-point functions and the results for the matrix elements obtained by combining the three-point correlation functions with the results given in Section. Section is devoted to the operator renormalization procedure and results. In Section we present the remainder of analysis to obtain the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes and Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon), including the interpolation of the renormalized matrix elements to physical kinematics. Section concludes the present work and discusses future prospects.
content_sections.II Lattice ensemble and overall measurem: In this paper we present our lattice calculation carried out on a 243×64superscript24364243times 64 lattice ensemble. We employ Iwasaki ++ DSDR (dislocation suppressing determinant ratio) gauge actioncitebib.bib60 and 2+1212+1-flavor Möbius domain wall fermionscitebib.bib61, bib.bib62 with the extent for the fifth direction Ls=24subscript𝐿𝑠24Ls=24, the Möbius scale b+c=4𝑏𝑐4b+c=4, b−c=1𝑏𝑐1b-c=1 and the domain-wall height M5=1.8subscript𝑀51.8M5=1.8. We choose β=1.633𝛽1.633beta=1.633, which corresponds to the inverse lattice spacing a−1=1.023​superscript𝑎11.0232a-1=1.023GeV and hence spatial extent L=4.639​𝐿4.6399L=4.639fm and time extent LT=12.34​subscript𝐿𝑇12.342LT=12.34fm. We tune the input light quark mass to a​ml=0.00107𝑎subscript𝑚𝑙0.00107aml=0.00107 and the strange quark mass to a​ms=0.0850𝑎subscript𝑚𝑠0.0850ams=0.0850 so that the pion and kaon masses have (nearly) their physical values. The pion mass is a​mπ=0.13944​𝑎subscript𝑚𝜋0.1394417ampi=0.13944 and the kaon mass is, as quoted in the following subsection, a​mK=0.50189​𝑎subscript𝑚𝐾0.5018936amK=0.50189. See Ref. citebib.bib63 for more details on the ensemble. We use the all-to-all (A2A) quark propagator method citebib.bib55, which combines exact low-mode solutions with a stochastic approximation to the high-mode contribution, with low-mode deflation for acceleration of the conjugate gradient (CG) inversions. We calculate 2,000 low modes for the light quarks via the local coherent Lanczos algorithmcitebib.bib64 with the zMöbius actioncitebib.bib65, bib.bib66 with Ls=12subscript𝐿𝑠12Ls=12, while the strange quark propagators are calculated without low-modes. The high(all)-mode contributions to light (strange) quark propagators are computed with one random source for each spin, color, and time-slice (spin-color-time dilution). This requires 64×12=768641276864times 12=768 CG inversions per configuration for each of the light and strange quarks. Since different random sources are generated for different configurations, We perform measurements on 258 configurations, which are separated by 10 or 20 molecular dynamics time units, with which we do not see apparent autocorrelation in the correlation functions discussed below. We implement the all-mode averaging (AMA) technique citebib.bib56, bib.bib57 to save computational time. Since the A2A prescription as used in this calculation naturally requires sampling on all time slices, we perform an exact calculation on a small subset of configurations with the same A2A contraction strategy as for the approximate (sloppy) calculations and combine the results using the superjackknife approach. A detailed description was given in Ref. citebib.bib43. In this work we have performed the exact calculations on 14 configurations. For the approximate part, the CG is stopped at 400 iterations and the light quark propagators are calculated with the zMöbius fermion actioncitebib.bib65, bib.bib66, which well approximates quantities obtained with the Möbius action and tighter CG stopping residual, but with smaller Ls=12subscript𝐿𝑠12Ls=12 and hence with smaller cost. The χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 fits presented in the paper are all performed with a fixed covariance matrix for all (super)jackknife samples and the errors are estimated by the standard jackknife method.

content_sections.III Two-point functions III.2 Two-pion c: In this subsection we summarize the results for the two-pion correlators and energies obtained in our companion paper, Ref. citebib.bib43. To construct two-pion operators, we first define single pion operators with specific momenta, (0,0,0)000(0,0,0), (0,0,2​πL)002𝜋𝐿(0,0,frac2piL), (0,2​πL,2​πL)02𝜋𝐿2𝜋𝐿(0,frac2piL,frac2piL), (2​πL,2​πL,2​πL)2𝜋𝐿2𝜋𝐿2𝜋𝐿(frac2piL,frac2piL,frac2piL) and permutations with respect to cubic symmetry. As for the kaon, Coulomb gauge fixed hydrogen-like wave functions are used for single pion interpolating operators but with radius r=1.5​a𝑟1.5𝑎r=1.5a, which well matches with the smearing radius for single pion operators used in the GPBC calculation in physical unitscitebib.bib2. Next two-pion operators are formed by multiplying two single-pion operators together with opposite momenta so that the total momentum is zero. Since the SO symmetry of angular momentum is broken down to a discrete symmetry in a finite box, we use the simplest irreducible representation that couples to the S𝑆S-wave state, the A1 representation. In addition we perform isospin projection of the two-pion operators to the I=2𝐼2I=2 and I=0𝐼0I=0 representations. We separate the two single-pion operators by 3 time-slices in order to reduce the vacuum state contribution to the I=0𝐼0I=0 channelcitebib.bib1, bib.bib2. The same separation in physical units were employed in the GPBC calculationcitebib.bib2. For the I=0𝐼0I=0 channel, we also introduce a σ𝜎sigma-like operator, an iso-singlet scalar bilinear of the up and down quarks since it was previously found to play an important role in controlling contamination from excited states citebib.bib67, bib.bib2. The σ𝜎sigma-like operator is also smeared in the same way as the single pion operators. Thus, we employ four two-pion operators for the I=2𝐼2I=2 channel and five for the I=0𝐼0I=0 channel. In order to distinguish the σ𝜎sigma-like operator from the other two-pion operators we refer to the latter as π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like operators. It is also convenient to denote them as π​π​𝜋𝜋000pipi, π​π​𝜋𝜋001pipi, π​π​𝜋𝜋011pipi, π​π​𝜋𝜋111pipi and σ𝜎sigma, where the three-digit number in the parentheses represents the three-dimensional momentum, in units of 2​π/L2𝜋𝐿2pi/L, of a single pion interpolation operator. The single pion and σ𝜎sigma operators, which are dimension-3, are summed over three-dimensional space to project onto a certain momentum and are therefore dimensionless. We compute the following two-point correlation functions Ca​b2​p​t,I​(t)=⟨OaI​(t)​ObI​(−Δb)†⟩−⟨OaI⟩​⟨ObI†⟩,superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑎𝑏2pt𝐼𝑡delimited-⟨⟩superscriptsubscript𝑂𝑎𝐼𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑂𝑏𝐼superscriptsubscriptΔ𝑏†delimited-⟨⟩superscriptsubscript𝑂𝑎𝐼delimited-⟨⟩superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑂𝑏𝐼†Cabrm 2pt,I(t)=left OaI(t)ObI(-Deltab)daggerright-left OaIrightleftObIdaggerright, where OaIsuperscriptsubscript𝑂𝑎𝐼OaI is a two-pion operator labeled by the operator index a(=1,2,…,N)a(=1,2,ldots,N) and isospin I𝐼I. ΔbsubscriptΔ𝑏Deltab is 0 when b𝑏b corresponds to the σ𝜎sigma operator and otherwise 3​a3𝑎3a so that the time variable t𝑡t indicates the separation between the “inner”, or nearer, pions or σ𝜎sigma operator of the source and sink. The second term on the RHS corresponds to the vacuum subtraction relevant for the I=0𝐼0I=0 channel. In what follows we omit the superscript I𝐼I for simplicity. These two-pion correlation functions behave as Ca​b2​p​t​(t)superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑎𝑏2pt𝑡displaystyle Cabrm 2pt(t) =∑nAn,a​An,b∗​e−En​t+thermal effects,absentsubscript𝑛subscript𝐴𝑛𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑛𝑏superscriptesubscript𝐸𝑛𝑡thermal effectsdisplaystyle=sumnAn,aAn,b*rme-Ent+mboxthermal effects, where Ensubscript𝐸𝑛En (n=0,1,…𝑛01…n=0,1,ldots) is the energy of the n𝑛n-th two-pion state of the corresponding isospin channel and An,a=⟨0|Oa|π​π​(En)⟩subscript𝐴𝑛𝑎quantum-operator-product0subscript𝑂𝑎𝜋𝜋subscript𝐸𝑛An,a= 0|Oa|pipi(En). Here the thermal effect is significant, and its leading contribution is an around-the-world propagation effect of the ground-state pion in the time direction which is independent of t𝑡t. In order to eliminate this contribution we perform a subtraction and obtain the following behavior: Ca​b2​p​t​(t,δt)superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑎𝑏2pt𝑡subscript𝛿𝑡displaystyle Cabrm 2pt(t,deltat) =Ca​b2​p​t​(t)−Ca​b2​p​t​(t+δt)absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐶𝑎𝑏2pt𝑡superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑎𝑏2pt𝑡subscript𝛿𝑡displaystyle=Cabrm 2pt(t)-Cabrm 2pt(t+deltat) =∑n(1−e−En​δt)​An,a​An,b∗​e−En​t+…absentsubscript𝑛1superscriptesubscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝐴𝑛𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑛𝑏superscriptesubscript𝐸𝑛𝑡…displaystyle=sumnleft(1-rme-Endeltatright)An,aAn,b*rme-Ent+ldots with an arbitrary time shift δtsubscript𝛿𝑡deltat. The ellipsis represents time-dependent thermal effects, which are invisible in the results shown below and in Ref. citebib.bib43 with the given statistical precision and therefore neglected in this work. While this subtraction cancels the vacuum subtraction term in Eq. , we find a minor statistical improvement by continuing to apply the vacuum subtraction in a time-dependent manner, i. e. formulae-sequence𝑖𝑒i. e. by replacing the second term on the right hand side of Eq. with ⟨Oa​(t)⟩​⟨Ob​(−Δb)†⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑂𝑎𝑡delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑂𝑏superscriptsubscriptΔ𝑏†left Oa(t)rightleft Ob(-Deltab)daggerright, where the vacuum expectation values are taken at a specific time slice indicated in the parentheses. The time-translation average is taken after the modified vacuum subtraction. To decompose the correlation functions into contributions from individual states we employ the variational method citebib.bib26, where we solve the Generalized Eigenvalue Problem (GEVP) for a given N×N𝑁𝑁Ntimes N correlator matrix, C2​p​t​(t,δt)superscript𝐶2pt𝑡subscript𝛿𝑡Crm 2pt(t,deltat), C2​p​t​(t,δt)​Vn​(t,t0,δt)=λn​(t,t0,δt)​C2​p​t​(t0,δt)​Vn​(t,t0,δt). superscript𝐶2pt𝑡subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝑉𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡superscript𝐶2ptsubscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝑉𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡Crm 2pt(t,deltat)Vn(t,t0,deltat)=lambdan(t,t0,deltat)Crm 2pt(t0,deltat)Vn(t,t0,deltat). From the original operators and the eigenvectors Vn,a​(t,t0,δt)subscript𝑉𝑛𝑎𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡Vn,a(t,t0,deltat) we can construct an operator, 𝒪n(t,t0,δt)=∑aVn,a​(t,t0,δt)​Oa,superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝑎subscript𝑉𝑛𝑎𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝑂𝑎cal On(t,t0,deltat)=sumaVn,a(t,t0,deltat)Oa, which couples well to the state labeled by n𝑛n but not with any of the other N𝑁N low-lying states. We can thus calculate the state-specific correlation functions: Cn2​p​t​(t,δt,t′,t0)superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑛2pt𝑡subscript𝛿𝑡superscript𝑡′subscript𝑡0displaystyle Cnrm 2pt(t,deltat,tprime,t0) =Vn​(t′,t0,δt)†​C​(t,δt)​Vn​(t′,t0,δt)absentsubscript𝑉𝑛superscriptsuperscript𝑡′subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡†𝐶𝑡subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝑉𝑛superscript𝑡′subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡displaystyle=Vn(tprime,t0,deltat)daggerC(t,deltat)Vn(tprime,t0,deltat) =(1−e−En​δt)​Bn(t′,t0,δt)​Bn(t′,t0,δt)∗​e−En​t+…,absent1superscriptesubscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝛿𝑡superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑛superscript𝑡′subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐵𝑛superscript𝑡′subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡superscriptesubscript𝐸𝑛𝑡…displaystyle=left(1-rme-Endeltatright)Bn(tprime,t0,deltat)Bn(tprime,t0,deltat)*rme-Ent+ldots, where the state index n𝑛n is not summed over, and we define Bn(t,t0,δt)=⟨0|𝒪n(t,t0,δt)|π​π​(En)⟩. superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡quantum-operator-product0superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡𝜋𝜋subscript𝐸𝑛Bn(t,t0,deltat)=left 0big|cal On(t,t0,deltat)big|pipi(En)right. The ellipsis in Eq. represents minor contributions from higher states with energies larger than EN−1subscript𝐸𝑁1EN-1 and the remaining thermal effects. The (generalized) eigenvalues λn​(t,t0,δt)subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡lambdan(t,t0,deltat) obtained by solving the GEVP provide the effective two-pion energies Eneff​(t,t0,δt)=ln⁡λn​(t,t0,δt)λn​(t+a,t0,δt),subscriptsuperscript𝐸eff𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡subscript𝜆𝑛𝑡𝑎subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡Erm effn(t,t0,deltat)=lnfraclambdan(t,t0,deltat)lambdan(t+a,t0,deltat), which should be independent of t,t0𝑡subscript𝑡0t,t0 and δtsubscript𝛿𝑡deltat at sufficiently large time separations (t𝑡t) where the higher-state contamination is invisible. Ref. citebib.bib27 demonstrated that the higher-state contamination in the energy of the n𝑛n-th state defined by is O​(e−(EN+1−En)​t)𝑂superscriptesubscript𝐸𝑁1subscript𝐸𝑛𝑡O(rme-(EN+1-En)t) in the region t0≥t/2subscript𝑡0𝑡2t0geq t/2. In this work we choose t0=t−asubscript𝑡0𝑡𝑎t0=t-a, which satisfies the inequality for t0≥asubscript𝑡0𝑎t0geq a. In Ref. citebib.bib43 we tuned the value of δtsubscript𝛿𝑡deltat and found the statistical precision can be optimized by choosing δt=5​asubscript𝛿𝑡5𝑎deltat=5a for I=2𝐼2I=2 and δt=8​asubscript𝛿𝑡8𝑎deltat=8a for I=0𝐼0I=0, with which we quote the results throughout this paper. In practice, there is a significant correlation between two-pion correlation functions with and without interactions between two pions. We utilize this correlation to improve the statistical and systematic precision of the two-pion energies. We compute the difference between the fully-interacting and non-interacting two-pion energies Δ​Eneff​(t,t0,δt)=Eneff​(t,t0,δt)−En0,eff​(t,t0,δt),Δsubscriptsuperscript𝐸eff𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡subscriptsuperscript𝐸eff𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑛0eff𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡Delta Erm effn(t,t0,deltat)=Erm effn(t,t0,deltat)-Enrm 0,eff(t,t0,deltat), where the non-interacting two-pion effective energy En0,eff​(t,t0,δt)superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑛0eff𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡Enrm 0,eff(t,t0,deltat) is calculated by the same procedure as the interacting one but using non-interacting two-pion correlators, i. e. ,formulae-sequence𝑖𝑒i. e. , a product of two single-pion correlators, ensemble-averaged separately, with pion operators placed at the same time slices as the ones for the interacting two-pion correlators. Then we add back the non-interacting two-pion energy obtained by using the continuum dispersion relation to obtain the improved effective energy, Eneff⁣′​(t,t0,δt)=Δ​Eneff​(t,t0,δt)+2​mπ2+4​n​π2L2. superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑛eff′𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡Δsubscriptsuperscript𝐸eff𝑛𝑡subscript𝑡0subscript𝛿𝑡2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋24𝑛superscript𝜋2superscript𝐿2Enrm eff,prime(t,t0,deltat)=Delta Erm effn(t,t0,deltat)+2sqrtmpi2+frac4npi2L2. Figure shows the improved effective two-pion energies for the I=2𝐼2I=2 and I=0𝐼0I=0 channels. We omit the I=0𝐼0I=0 fourth excited state energy because it is unresolved. The figure indicates that we can well extract the signal from the four lowest states of the I=2𝐼2I=2 channel and three lowest ones of the I=0𝐼0I=0 channel. We perform constant fits to each two-pion effective energy considering the correlation among the data points at different time slices. The results are summarized in Tables and for the I=2𝐼2I=2 and I=0𝐼0I=0 channels, respectively. In the I=2𝐼2I=2 channel, the first-excited state energy is closest to the kaon mass, a​mK=0.50189​𝑎subscript𝑚𝐾0.5018936amK=0.50189, but is 21% larger, hence a somewhat sizeable interpolation will be required to obtain physical kinematics as we will discuss in Section. For the I=0𝐼0I=0 channel the difference is only 5%, requiring a much more modest interpolation to be performed. As noted in the beginning of this subsection more detailed and sophisticated discussion on two-pion correlators and energies was presented in Ref. citebib.bib43.
content_sections.III Two-point functions III.3 Lellouch-L: Because of the interaction between two pions in a finite box, the normalization for the two-pion states is not the same as in the infinite-volume limit. We need to correctly normalize the two-pion states in order to obtain the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes in infinite volume. The prescription to determine the normalization factor F𝐹F, the Lellouch-Lüscher (LL) factor, was provided in Ref. citebib.bib51. With a given two-pion energy Eπ​πsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋Epipi in a finite box, the normalization factor can be determined as F2=4​π​mK​Eπ​π2k3​(k​d​δ0d​k+q​d​ϕd​q),superscript𝐹24𝜋subscript𝑚𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋2superscript𝑘3𝑘dsubscript𝛿0d𝑘𝑞ditalic-ϕd𝑞F2=frac4pi mKEpipi2k3left(kfracrmddelta0rmdk+qfracrmdphirmdqright), where k=(Eπ​π2)2−mπ2,q=L​k2​πformulae-sequence𝑘superscriptsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋22superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2𝑞𝐿𝑘2𝜋k=sqrtleft(fracEpipi2right)2-mpi2, q=fracLk2pi and δ0subscript𝛿0delta0 is the two-pion phase shift and ϕitalic-ϕphi is a known function, tan⁡ϕ​(q)=−π3/2​qZ00​(1;q2)italic-ϕ𝑞superscript𝜋32𝑞subscript𝑍001superscript𝑞2tanphi(q)=-fracpi3/2qZ00(1;q2) for periodic boundary conditions defined with the Lüscher zeta function, Z00​(s;q2)=14​π​∑n→(|n→|2−q2)−s. subscript𝑍00𝑠superscript𝑞214𝜋subscript→𝑛superscriptsuperscript→𝑛2superscript𝑞2𝑠Z00(s;q2)=frac1sqrt4pisumvecnleft(|vecn|2-q2right)-s. Eq. is valid for Re​(s)≥3/2Re𝑠32rm Re(s)geq 3/2. For Re​(s)<3/2Re𝑠32rm Re(s)<3/2 its analytic continuation is used. We employ the efficient numerical implementation given in Ref. citebib.bib68 to evaluate it at s=1𝑠1s=1. In this work, the phase shift term, the first term in the parentheses on the right hand side of Eq. , is calculated via dispersion theory based on the Roy equation citebib.bib69 with inputs from chiral perturbation theory (ChPT) and experimental data, which is consistent with results from lattice QCD using the Lüscher finite volume methodcitebib.bib26, both near the two-pion threshold citebib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73 and at larger two-pion energiescitebib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib44, bib.bib43, bib.bib76. In practice, we use Eqs. (17.1)–(17.3) of Ref. citebib.bib77 with the pion mass on our lattice ensemble to compute the phase shift. The formula is valid up to the exponentially suppressed correctionscitebib.bib78, O​(e−mπ​L)𝑂superscriptesubscript𝑚𝜋𝐿O(rme-mpiL), in the elastic region, 2​mπ<Eπ​π<4​mπ2subscript𝑚𝜋subscript𝐸𝜋𝜋4subscript𝑚𝜋2mpi<Epipi<4mpi in the case of the rest frame. In the inelastic region above the four-pion threshold, Eπ​π≥4​mπsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋4subscript𝑚𝜋Epipigeq 4mpi, and there are extra states that are not accounted for by the Lüscher prescription. The systematic effect slightly above the inelastic threshold is expected to be small since the effects of four-pion states appear at next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) in ChPTcitebib.bib74. In addition, some pion scattering studies have found that the systematic effects appear to be sub-statistical slightly above the inelastic thresholdcitebib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib43, bib.bib76. Since the LL factor is derived using the Lüscher formula for the phase shift, the systematic error on the LL factor slightly above the inelastic threshold is expected to be small. In this section, we calculate the LL factor up to the second excited state with an energy slightly above 800MeV and 700MeV for I=2𝐼2I=2 and I=0𝐼0I=0, respectively. For calculations of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes in Section we only use the ground and first-excited two-pion states for the interpolation to obtain the physical kinematics. Out of these states, only the I=2𝐼2I=2 first excited state is above (but close to) the threshold. Since the I=0𝐼0I=0 two-pion ground state energy in the rest frame is smaller than 2​mπ2subscript𝑚𝜋2mpi due to the attractive interaction in this channel, the corresponding value of k2superscript𝑘2k2 is negative, and the formulae above needs to be analytically continued. While this kind of analytic continuation for I=0𝐼0I=0 was implicitly performed for the calculation of the scattering length by a number of works as emphasized in Ref. citebib.bib71, here we give the formulae for the LL factor below the two-pion threshold. where the sums over q𝑞q run for all the active quarks: up, down and strange in three-flavor theory. The sum over the Lorentz index μ𝜇mu is implicitly taken for each operator. The color indices are explicitly shown by α𝛼alpha and β𝛽beta, while the spin indices are omitted as they are always contracted in the trivial manner. The electric charge of a quark q𝑞q is expressed by eqsubscript𝑒𝑞eq for the electroweak penguin operators Q7−10subscript𝑄710Q7mathchar 45relax 10. Here, the current-current operators, Q1subscript𝑄1Q1 and Q2subscript𝑄2Q2, dominate the physics of the real parts of the amplitudes; the QCD penguin operators, Q3−6subscript𝑄36Q3mathchar 45relax 6, dominate that of Im(A0)subscript𝐴0(A0); and the electroweak penguin operators, Q7−10subscript𝑄710Q7mathchar 45relax 10, that of Im(A2)subscript𝐴2(A2). Note, while the lattice calculation does not include electromagnetic effects, we do include the effective operators resulting from short-distance photonic propagation due to the significant role they play in the I=2𝐼2I=2 channel decay. As is well known, a lattice calculation preserves all dimension-4 Fierz relations, while these are broken in dimensional regularization approaches to perturbation theory for which the dimension-dependence of γ5subscript𝛾5gamma5 breaks certain Fierz relations leading. Fierz symmetry gives rise to three relations among the four-quark operators. We therefore use them to reduce the operator basis to seven operators, Qj′j=1,2,3,5,6,7,8subscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑄𝑗′𝑗1235678Qjprimej=1,2,3,5,6,7,8 defined in citebib.bib13, in our lattice calculation, which we call the chiral basis, as well as the ten-operator basis above. The linear independence of the chiral basis is convenient to renormalize the four-quark operators as it requires only a minimum number of independent renormalization conditions, and the inverse renormalization matrix needed for step scaling is well defined. It should also be noted that each operator in the chiral basis transforms as a specific representation of SU​L×SU​RSUsubscript3𝐿SUsubscript3𝑅rm SULtimesrm SUR chiral symmetry so that the mixing among the operators is minimal, while the current-current (Q1,2subscript𝑄12Q1,2) and electroweak penguin (Q7−10subscript𝑄710Q7mathchar 45relax 10) operators are composed of multiple representations. This property is convenient especially when fermions with good chiral symmetry such as domain wall fermions are employed. The basis enlargement from the chiral basis to the ten-operator basis after renormalization only has nontriviality in the perturbative matching from a nonperturbative scheme to MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS, which was well discussed in Ref. citebib.bib79 and is taken into account in Section. Alternative definitions of Q1subscript𝑄1Q1 and Q2subscript𝑄2Q2 can be formed by applying the Fierz identitiescitebib.bib13, bib.bib79, Q1=s¯α​γμ​(1−γ5)​dα⋅u¯β​γμ​(1−γ5)​uβsubscript𝑄1⋅subscript¯𝑠𝛼subscript𝛾𝜇1subscript𝛾5subscript𝑑𝛼subscript¯𝑢𝛽subscript𝛾𝜇1subscript𝛾5subscript𝑢𝛽widetildeQ1=barsalphagammamu(1-gamma5)dalphacdotbarubetagammamu(1-gamma5)ubeta and Q2=s¯α​γμ​(1−γ5)​uβ⋅u¯β​γμ​(1−γ5)​dαsubscript𝑄2⋅subscript¯𝑠𝛼subscript𝛾𝜇1subscript𝛾5subscript𝑢𝛽subscript¯𝑢𝛽subscript𝛾𝜇1subscript𝛾5subscript𝑑𝛼widetildeQ2=barsalphagammamu(1-gamma5)ubetacdotbarubetagammamu(1-gamma5)dalpha. These definitions give rise to identical numerical results on the lattice and simplify the structure of the contractions by making all four-quark operators with an odd (even) index have a color-diagonal (color-mixed) structure. However, the Wilson coefficients in the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme depend on the definitions of Q1subscript𝑄1Q1 and Q2subscript𝑄2Q2 since dimensional regularization is employed. We use the definitions in Eqs. and for computing the Wilson coefficients based on the formulae given in Refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib8. While these operators are all relevant for the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel, the QCD penguin operators Q3−6subscript𝑄36Q3mathchar 45relax 6 and four operators Q2,3,5,6′superscriptsubscript𝑄2356′Q2,3,5,6prime in the chiral basis are purely in the (8,1)81(8,1) representation and do not contribute to the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel. As a result the number of independent operators is three for this channel. Our earlier works on the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel citebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19 employed a three-operator basis that is purely made of Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 operators, Q(27,1)subscript𝑄271Q(27,1), Q(8,8)subscript𝑄88Q(8,8) and Q(8,8)​mixsubscript𝑄88mixQ(8,8)rm mix. In this work we employ the ten operators in Eqs. – and the chiral basis of the seven operators for both the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 and Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channels to apply the same numerical analysis, although the matrix elements of the QCD penguin operators are always zero for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel.

Again the isospin index I𝐼I is suppressed for simplicity and we discuss the three-point function in general for both the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 and 1/2121/2 channels corresponding the I=2𝐼2I=2 and 0 two-pion operators on the right hand side, respectively. The subscript a𝑎a labels a two-pion operator, including the quark bilinear σ𝜎sigma. As mentioned in the previous section the source and sink operators are dimensionless as each bilinear of these operators is summed over three-dimensional space. The dimension-6 operator Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Qi is also summed over three-dimensional space for the measurements and thus these correlation functions are dimension-3. The time indices t1subscript𝑡1t1 and t2subscript𝑡2t2 stand for the time separations between the four-quark and kaon operators, and between the two-pion and four-quark operators, respectively. We calculate the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi three-point functions with several time separations between the kaon source and two-pion sink operators. We choose (t1+t2)/a=6,7,9,10,11,13subscript𝑡1subscript𝑡2𝑎679101113(t1+t2)/a=6,7,9,10,11,13 in this work. Counting the parity of the two-pion and kaon operators one can recognize that the three-point functions are contributed by the parity-odd part of the four-quark operators. The parity-even part of the four-quark operators only increases statistical error on the three-point functions and is therefore excluded from the measurements. The Wick contractions for these correlation functions yield four classes of diagram, which are summarized in Figure for the case where Oasubscript𝑂𝑎Oa is a π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like operator and Figure for the case where Oasubscript𝑂𝑎Oa is a σ𝜎sigma-like operator. The Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel contains contributions from only type1 diagrams, whereas the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel receives contributions from all the diagrams. While the contraction formulae with a π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like sink operator for PBC were given in Ref. citebib.bib80, the formulae with a σ𝜎sigma-like operator were not presented. We summarize the contraction formulae for both K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi three-point functions including those with a σ𝜎sigma-like operator in Appendix. These correlation functions are averaged over all time translations with the A2A quark propagators. Figure shows the results for α2subscript𝛼2alpha2 and α6subscript𝛼6alpha6. The stable plateau seen up to t1=12​asubscript𝑡112𝑎t1=12a indicates that the thermal effect, which contributes to the numerator of Eq. in the form ∼⟨π|Qi​(t1)​OK​†|π⟩​e−mπ​(LT−t1)similar-toabsentquantum-operator-product𝜋subscript𝑄𝑖subscript𝑡1superscript𝑂𝐾superscript0†𝜋superscriptesubscript𝑚𝜋subscript𝐿𝑇subscript𝑡1simleftpibig|Qi(t1)OKdaggerbig|pirightrme-mpi(LT-t1), is not significant. While the values of αisubscript𝛼𝑖alphai on the plateau correspond to the subtraction condition ⟨0|Qi|K⟩=0quantum-operator-product0subscript𝑄𝑖𝐾0 0|Qi|K=0, the other data points also remove the power divergence from the quark loop of type3 and type4 diagrams but lead to different values of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements with energy-non-conserving kinematics. In this work, the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 matrix elements are determined from the region of large enough t1subscript𝑡1t1 where ⟨0|Qi|K⟩=0quantum-operator-product0subscript𝑄𝑖𝐾0 0|Qi|K=0 is satisfied. To implement this subtraction for the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi three-point functions we need to calculate additional diagrams, which are summarized in Figures and for π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like and σ𝜎sigma-like sink operators, respectively. The contraction formulae for the correlation functions are also summarized in Appendix. While the A2A quark propagator method allows us to take the average of three-point functions over all space-time translations, the cost of performing contractions on every space-time translation is comparable to, or even larger than, the cost of generating the A2A quark propagators. However the diagrams that require a large fraction of the contraction cost are type1 and type2, which are fully connected and can be calculated precisely with relatively fewer measurements. In our previous GPBC calculationcitebib.bib2 we calculated type1 and type2 diagrams for every translation of eight time-slices and type3 and type4 diagrams every time-slice with a full three-dimensional volume average for all diagrams. We observed that the type4 diagram still dominated the statistical error while the cost for type1 and type2 diagrams was significant. In this work we reduce the number of measurements of type1 and type2 diagrams in the spatial directions as well as for the time direction by calculating these diagrams on a uniform grid of 83superscript8383 sites, for eight time translations for the four-quark operator, while type3 and type4 diagrams are calculated for all space-time translations. Figure shows the breakdown of the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi three-point functions into contributions from each diagram at t1+t2=10​asubscript𝑡1subscript𝑡210𝑎t1+t2=10a. We show the results with Q2subscript𝑄2Q2 and Q6subscript𝑄6Q6 operators, which provide the dominant contribution to Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0) and Im(A0subscript𝐴0A0), respectively, and with the π​π​𝜋𝜋000pipi, π​π​𝜋𝜋001pipi and σ𝜎sigma operators, which strongly couple with either the ground or first-excited two-pion state. While the type4 diagrams, which are disconnected, are expected to dominate the statistical error, that is not always the case at small time separations t2≤3​asubscript𝑡23𝑎t2leq 3a between the two-pion and four-quark operators, where the errors on the type2 diagrams are comparable or even larger that on type4. In addition to the intrinsic signal restoration of disconnected diagrams at short times, this should also be because of the reduction in the number of measurements for the type1 and type2 diagrams. Note that we do not extract the matrix elements at such short time separations, where the contamination from higher excited states is still significant. At larger time separations, t2>3​asubscript𝑡23𝑎t2>3a, on the other hand, the type4 diagram dominates the statistical error. This indicates that the cost reduction with less number of measurements of type1 and type2 diagrams does not significantly impact the total statistical precision of the correlation functions at time separations where the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements are extracted.

The difference between Mn,1subscript𝑀𝑛1Mn,1 and Mn,2subscript𝑀𝑛2Mn,2 is caused by the slight violation of the Fierz symmetry due to the use of the stochastic A2A method, which approximates the quark propagators in the given gauge configuration. Since three-point functions before and after Fierz transformation are calculated by taking the spin and color contractions quite differently, the difference between the exact and approximated quark propagators can cause the difference between the three-point functions and hence the corresponding matrix elements. The quark propagator becomes exact in the large-hit or large-ensemble limit. The upper panels indicate that, while the results for the matrix elements with the two-pion ground state are mostly independent of the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like operators with a non-zero relative momentum, adding the σ𝜎sigma-like operator makes a significant impact up to t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a for M0,2subscript𝑀02M0,2 and t2min=3​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min3𝑎t2rm min=3a for M0,6subscript𝑀06M0,6. The benefit of including the σ𝜎sigma-like operator is quite apparent in M0,6subscript𝑀06M0,6, which shows a plateau beginning at t2min=2​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min2𝑎t2rm min=2a with the σ𝜎sigma-like operator, whereas the values differ by more than 50% in this low-time region when the σ𝜎sigma-like operator is excluded. A similar trend is observed for the matrix elements with the first-excited two-pion state shown on the lower panels. The only difference in the trend between the ground- and first-excited two-pion final states is observed for “N=2𝑁2N=2 w σ𝜎sigma,” the results with the two operators π​π​𝜋𝜋000pipi and σ𝜎sigma, where the matrix elements with the first-excited state are too noisy to be in the lower panels. This may result from insufficient coupling between the first excited state and these two operators. We observe that the precision is significantly improved by including the π​π​𝜋𝜋001pipi operator. The π​π​𝜋𝜋001pipi operator is the most naïve operator for creating a two-pion state with an energy close to the kaon mass. It plays a similar role to the ‘π​π​𝜋𝜋111pipi’ operator in the GPBC work, which comprised two single-pion operators with back-to-back momenta of π/L𝜋𝐿pi/L in each spatial direction. The ‘π​π​𝜋𝜋111pipi’ operator was the only two-pion operator included in the original GPBC work, but was shown in Ref. citebib.bib2 to be insufficient to reliably isolate the required two-pion state. Our PBC results appear to verify this behavior. The lower panels indicate that, while the σ𝜎sigma-like operator plays an important role in removing excited-state contamination, including the π​π​𝜋𝜋001pipi operator is also important to obtain a well-resolved signal for the first excited state. The fit result for M1,6subscript𝑀16M1,6, which provides the dominant contribution to Im​(A0)Imsubscript𝐴0rm Im(A0) and therefore plays a crucial role in determining ε′superscript𝜀′varepsilonprime, appears to depend on t2minsuperscriptsubscript𝑡2mint2rm min still around our choice of t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a as seen in Figure. The consistency between “N=4𝑁4N=4 w σ𝜎sigma” and “N=5𝑁5N=5 w σ𝜎sigma” seen in Figure may not necessarily rule out the possibility of further excited-state contamination because the fourth excited state that can in principle be extracted by including the fifth operator, π​π​𝜋𝜋111pipi, is unresolved in our data and adding this operator might not play a role in isolating excited state effects. It is therefore valuable to demonstrate there is no evidence of further excited-state contamination for t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a.
