A similar trend in the t2minsubscriptsuperscript𝑡min2trm min2-dependence of the fit result for the matrix element of Q6subscript𝑄6Q6 was observed at roughly equal values of t2minsuperscriptsubscript𝑡2mint2rm min in physical units in our earlier work with GPBCcitebib.bib2, where we concluded, after a serious investigation of systematic errors, it is not due to contamination from excited two-pion states but a simple statistical effects. Table summarizes the fit results for the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements Mn,isubscript𝑀𝑛𝑖Mn,i with n=0,1,2𝑛012n=0,1,2 and i=1𝑖1i=1–10. Compared with the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel most of the fits are stable and χ2/d. o. f. formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜒2𝑑𝑜𝑓chi2/d. o. f. is fairly small despite the wider fit ranges. It is interesting to observe the accuracy of Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 matrix elements for the ground, first-excited and second-excited states. For the ground state, which corresponds to two pions at rest, the matrix elements appear the most precise among the three. For the first-excite state, which is the closest among the three to the physical kinematics, the matrix elements are well determined. For the second-excited state, the matrix elements are also somewhat resolved for several four-quark operators. These observations suggest that the states have been successfully disentangled by the GEVP. It is thus remarkable that we have succeeded in extracting the matrix elements with excited two-pion final states with well-resolved signals.
content_sections.V Renormalization of four-quark operator: Renormalization of composite operators is an essential step for the lattice calculation of weak processes to remove unphysical divergences, as is the case in general for quantum field theory, before we can remove the regulator, i. e. formulae-sequence𝑖𝑒i. e. , take the continuum limit a→0→𝑎0ato 0. While the lattice operators are usually renormalized in a non-perturbative scheme, the corresponding Wilson coefficients are computed in the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS schemecitebib.bib7, bib.bib8. In order to appropriately construct the weak Hamiltonian and obtain the corresponding decay amplitudes, we need to convert the nonperturbatively renormalized matrix elements to the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme. As mentioned in Section we use the chiral basis of the seven four-quark operators Qi′superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′Qiprimecitebib.bib13, which has two advantages in nonperturbative renormalization (NPR). First, the linear independence of operators minimizes the number of renormalization conditions and gives well-defined inverse renormalization matrices, which are useful for step scalingcitebib.bib59. Second, each operator in this basis transforms as a specific representation of SU​L×SU​RSUsubscript3𝐿SUsubscript3𝑅rm SULtimesrm SUR chiral symmetry, while the operators Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Qi in the ten-operator basis in general combine multiple representations. Out of the seven operators, one transforms as the (27,1) representation, four as (8,1) and two as (8,8). The operators in the same representations still mix with each other but not with the other operators. As a result, the corresponding renormalization matrices are block diagonal. We employ a common momentum-space procedure using the regularization independent symmetric momentum (RI-SMOM) schemes, in which renormalization conditions are imposed nonperturbatively. Since the perturbative matching between the RI-SMOM and MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS schemes is available to the one-loop levelcitebib.bib79, RI-SMOM gives convenient intermediate schemes to obtain the matrix elements in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. We also perform step scaling as we previously observed a significant, three times improvement in the estimated systematic errors associated with the truncation of the perturbative matching to the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme when raising the renormalization scale from 1.53GeV to 4GeV through the step-scaling procedurecitebib.bib2. This procedure is more important for the present calculation due to the much coarser, 1.0GeV inverse lattice spacing versus 1.4GeV in the previous calculation, restricting the range of usable energy scales even further. Since the RI-SMOM renormalization conditions do not obey the equations of motion and require gauge fixing, we need to take into account the mixing with extra operators that vanish by the equations of motion or are gauge variant. In this work we take the mixing with only quark bilinears into account since the mixing with irrelevant operators with higher dimensions such as G1=s¯​γν​(1−γ5)​Dμ​Gμ​ν​dsubscript𝐺1¯𝑠subscript𝛾𝜈1subscript𝛾5subscript𝐷𝜇subscript𝐺𝜇𝜈𝑑G1=barsgammanu(1-gamma5)DmuGmunud, where Dμsubscript𝐷𝜇Dmu is a covariant-derivative operator and Gμ​νsubscript𝐺𝜇𝜈Gmunu the gluon field strength and the space-time indices μ𝜇mu and ν𝜈nu are summed over, were found to be less significant than other systematic errorscitebib.bib2. In this section we give a summary for the renormalization procedure and then show the results for the renormalization and step-scaling matrices. The more detailed procedure is presented in Refs. citebib.bib81, bib.bib2.

Because of mixing with lower dimensional operators the four-quark operators are power divergent, and this needs to be subtracted before a multiplicative renormalization condition to remove the remaining logarithmic divergence can be imposed. We consider three bilinear operators S1=s¯​γ5​dsubscript𝑆1¯𝑠subscript𝛾5𝑑S1=barsgamma5d, S2=s¯​/​D→​γ5​dsubscript𝑆2¯𝑠→/𝐷subscript𝛾5𝑑S2=barsoverrightarrowhbox to0.0pthbox to8.55695pthfil/hfilhssDgamma5d and S3=s¯​/​D←​γ5​dsubscript𝑆3¯𝑠←/𝐷subscript𝛾5𝑑S3=barsoverleftarrowhbox to0.0pthbox to8.55695pthfil/hfilhssDgamma5d, where the arrow indicates the direction of the discrete covariant derivative /​D/𝐷hbox to0.0pthbox to8.55695pthfil/hfilhssD. The subtracted four-quark operators are written as Qi′⁣sub,lat=Qi′⁣lat+∑j∈𝒞bi,j​Sjlat,superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′sublatsuperscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′latsubscript𝑗𝒞subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑗latQiprimerm,sub,lat=Qiprimerm,lat+sumjincal Cbi,jSjrm lat, where we have added the superscript ‘lat’ to indicate bare operators and the sum over j𝑗j runs over the set 𝒞=1,2,3,5,6,7,8𝒞1235678cal C=1,2,3,5,6,7,8. The subtraction coefficients bi,jsubscript𝑏𝑖𝑗bi,j are determined by the condition P2,jβ​α​(p1,p2)​⟨s​(−p1)​d¯​(p2)​Qi′⁣sub,lat​(q)⟩ampα​β=0,subscriptsuperscript𝑃𝛽𝛼2𝑗subscript𝑝1subscript𝑝2subscriptsuperscriptdelimited-⟨⟩𝑠subscript𝑝1¯𝑑subscript𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′sublat𝑞𝛼𝛽amp0Pbetaalpha2,j(p1,p2)left s(-p1)bard(p2)Qiprimerm,sub,lat(q)rightalphabetarm amp=0, where the indices α𝛼alpha and β𝛽beta are combined spin and color indices and the projection operators P2,jsubscript𝑃2𝑗P2,j are given in Section7.2.6 of Ref. citebib.bib81. We use these momenta for the multiplicative renormalization condition as well, and they determine the renormalization scale μ𝜇mu. In the case of RI-SMOM schemes they satisfy p12=p22=q2≡μ2. superscriptsubscript𝑝12superscriptsubscript𝑝22superscript𝑞2superscript𝜇2p12=p22=q2equivmu2. Emsubscript𝐸𝑚Em denotes a product of external quark fields, E1,2,4,5subscript𝐸1245displaystyle E1,2,4,5 =s​(−p1)​d¯​(p2)​u​(−p1)​u¯​(p2),absent𝑠subscript𝑝1¯𝑑subscript𝑝2𝑢subscript𝑝1¯𝑢subscript𝑝2displaystyle=s(-p1)bard(p2)u(-p1)baru(p2), E3,6,7subscript𝐸367displaystyle E3,6,7 =s​(−p1)​d¯​(p2)​∑q=u,d,sq​(−p1)​q¯​(p2). absent𝑠subscript𝑝1¯𝑑subscript𝑝2subscript𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠𝑞subscript𝑝1¯𝑞subscript𝑝2displaystyle=s(-p1)bard(p2)sumq=u,d,sq(-p1)barq(p2). We consider two different schemes by employing different projection operators, which have the following spin structure: γμscheme:P4,m∼±γμ⊗γ5​γμ−γ5​γμ⊗γμ,q/scheme:P4,m∼±q/⊗γ5q/−γ5q/⊗q/,beginarrayrlmboxgammamu scheme:P4,msimpmgammamuotimesgamma5gammamu-gamma5gammamuotimesgammamu, mboxhbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/ scheme:P4,msimpmhbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/otimesgamma5hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/-gamma5hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/otimeshbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,endarray for the renormalization procedure with the parity-odd part of the four-quark operators. The sign ±plus-or-minuspm and color structure depend on m𝑚m. The explicit forms of these projection operators are given in Section3.3.2 of Ref. citebib.bib82. To calculate Zq​(μ)subscript𝑍𝑞𝜇Zq(mu), we first compute ZA/Zqsubscript𝑍𝐴subscript𝑍𝑞ZA/Zq from the RI-SMOM renormalization condition for the local axial current. We can thus obtain Zq​(μ)subscript𝑍𝑞𝜇Zq(mu) using ZAsubscript𝑍𝐴ZA computed from the ratio of the pion-to-vacuum matrix elements of the local and conserved currents. While we can also use the SMOM renormalization ZVsubscript𝑍𝑉ZV of the vector current to determine Zqsubscript𝑍𝑞Zq, we use ZA=0.73457​subscript𝑍𝐴0.7345711ZA=0.73457citebib.bib63 in this work because of an advantage in the statistical precision. Here we can consider the γμsubscript𝛾𝜇gammamu and q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/ schemes for determination of Zqsubscript𝑍𝑞Zq and Zi​jRIsuperscriptsubscript𝑍𝑖𝑗RIZijrm RI. We define the SMOM(A,B)𝐴𝐵(A,B) scheme as the scheme that employs the A𝐴A scheme for the projectors P4subscript𝑃4P4 and the B𝐵B scheme for the projector to determine Zqsubscript𝑍𝑞Zq. While there are four combinations, we only consider the SMOM(γμ,γμ)subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇(gammamu,gammamu) and SMOM(q/,q/(hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) schemes because we have observed that the nonperturbative running in these schemes is better described by perturbation theory than in the other two schemescitebib.bib83. On the 243superscript243243 ensemble, we calculate the vertex functions at the SU symmetric valence quark mass a​mq=0.01𝑎subscript𝑚𝑞0.01amq=0.01. While renormalization schemes are usually defined in the chiral limit, the vertex functions were found to depend on the quark mass very little for SMOM Δ​F=2Δ𝐹2Delta F=2 operatorscite. Therefore we calculate NPR at the single finite quark mass. We choose two renormalization scales μ=μ1≈1.3𝜇subscript𝜇11.3mu=mu1approx 1.3GeV and μ=μ2≈1.5𝜇subscript𝜇21.5mu=mu2approx 1.5GeV. Table shows a summary of these scales and the corresponding renormalization factors Zqsubscript𝑍𝑞Zq for quark fields in the γμsubscript𝛾𝜇gammamu and q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/ schemes. The higher scale μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 corresponds to the intermediate scale chosen in Ref. citebib.bib2 in physical units. Table shows the 7×7777times 7 renormalization matrix for the SMOM(q/,q/)(hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) scheme at μ=μ1𝜇subscript𝜇1mu=mu1. While the sea light and strange quark masses on the 243superscript243243 ensemble are 0.0017 and 0.085 in lattice units, respectively, we set the valence quark mass to 0.01 for both. While the renormalization matrix should in principle be extrapolated to the chiral limit, we neglect the systematic error due to these finite masses because the renormalization scales are relatively large and the mass effect is expected to be small. The results with different pairs of the scheme and scale are given in Table in Appendix.

Since our lattice ensemble is coarse, the renormalization window problem needs to be addressed; the renormalization scales μ1subscript𝜇1mu1 and μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 used in the present work are not high enough to perform a precise perturbative matching but renormalizing at higher scales may lead to significant discretization errors. To bypass this problem we employ the step-scaling techniquecitebib.bib59 using a finer 323×64superscript32364323times 64 lattice ensemble which we call the 32Ifine ensemble. Details are given in Ref. citebib.bib47, but we note that the inverse lattice spacing and pion mass on this ensemble are 3.148​3.148173.148GeV and 371MeV, respectively. We choose the valence quark mass a​mq=0.0047𝑎subscript𝑚𝑞0.0047amq=0.0047, which is the same as the sea light quark mass. As noted above we expect the systematic error due to the finite mass is small since it was found to be the case for the Δ​F=2Δ𝐹2Delta F=2 four-quark operators in the SMOM schemescite. On this fine lattice we calculate the step-scaling matrix ΣRI​(μ′,μ)=ZRI←lat​(μ′)​ZRI←lat​(μ)−1,superscriptΣRIsuperscript𝜇′𝜇superscript𝑍←RIlatsuperscript𝜇′superscript𝑍←RIlatsuperscript𝜇1Sigmarm RI(muprime,mu)=Zrm RIleftarrow lat(muprime)Zrm RIleftarrow lat(mu)-1, where the indices for the matrices are suppressed, and we suppose μ′>μsuperscript𝜇′𝜇muprime>mu and obtain the four-quark operators renormalized at a higher scale, Qi′⁣RI​(μ′)=∑j∈𝒞Σi​jRI​(μ′,μ)​Qj′⁣RI​(μ). superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′RIsuperscript𝜇′subscript𝑗𝒞subscriptsuperscriptΣRI𝑖𝑗superscript𝜇′𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑗′RI𝜇Qiprimerm,RI(muprime)=sumjincal CSigmarm RIij(muprime,mu)Qjprimerm,RI(mu). While the step-scaling matrix in principle needs to be extrapolated to the continuum limit, we use a single lattice ensemble. In Ref. cite , bilinear and V​V+A​A𝑉𝑉𝐴𝐴VV+AA step scaling functions have displayed modest discretization effects at similar scales in lattice units. We therefore anticipate these non-removed discretization errors are not dominant. Table shows a summary of the scales used for the calculation of the renormalization matrices on the 32Ifine lattice ensemble. The corresponding Zq/ZAsubscript𝑍𝑞subscript𝑍𝐴Zq/ZA is also shown for each scheme and scale. The reason for showing Zq/ZAsubscript𝑍𝑞subscript𝑍𝐴Zq/ZA instead of Zqsubscript𝑍𝑞Zq shown in Table is that the purpose of using the 32Ifine ensemble is to calculate the step-scaling matrix, which is independent of ZAsubscript𝑍𝐴ZA, and that Zq/ZAsubscript𝑍𝑞subscript𝑍𝐴Zq/ZA is calculated more directly without inputting ZAsubscript𝑍𝐴ZA separately. Note that although the fourth elements of the external momenta p1subscript𝑝1p1 and p2subscript𝑝2p2 are for the time direction and their actual unit is hence 2​π/LT=π/L2𝜋subscript𝐿𝑇𝜋𝐿2pi/LT=pi/L, they are shown in units of 2​π/L2𝜋𝐿2pi/L in the table. Note also that we identify the lower two scales as μ1subscript𝜇1mu1 and μ2subscript𝜇2mu2, although they are not identical to the ones listed in Table for the 243superscript243243 ensemble. Namely we intend to perform the step scaling with μ=μ1,μ2𝜇subscript𝜇1subscript𝜇2mu=mu1,mu2 and μ′=μhsuperscript𝜇′subscript𝜇ℎmuprime=muh neglecting the impact of the difference in these scales between the two ensembles. The difference in μ1subscript𝜇1mu1 is larger by about 6%, which would only cause a systematic error of O​(αs2​π×6%)𝑂subscript𝛼𝑠2𝜋percent6O(fracalphas2pitimes 6%) on the renormalized matrix element after the step scaling because evolution from μ𝜇mu to μ′superscript𝜇′muprime scales like 1+O(αs4​πln(μ′μ)2)1+O(fracalphas4piln(fracmuprimemu)2). Table shows the result for the step-scaling matrix from μ1subscript𝜇1mu1 to μhsubscript𝜇ℎmuh in the SMOM(q/,q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) scheme calculated on the 32Ifine lattice. We also show the corresponding renormalization matrix ΣSMOM(q/,q/)​(μh,μ1)​ZSMOM(q/,q/)←lat​(μ1)Sigmarm SMOM(hbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilscriptstyle qhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilscriptstyle qhfilhss/)(muh,mu1)Zrm SMOM(hbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilscriptstyle qhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilscriptstyle qhfilhss/)rmleftarrow lat(mu1) after the step scaling in Table. The results with different pairs of the scheme and lower scale are shown in Table for the step-scaling matrix and in Table for the renormalization matrix multiplied with the step-scaling matrix.

While the nonperturbative renormalization procedure described above removes the ultraviolet divergences of four-quark operators, and we can take the continuum limit of matrix elements of the renormalized four-quark operators, it is also necessary to convert them into the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme in order to appropriately construct the weak Hamiltonian and obtain the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes because the corresponding Wilson coefficients are calculated in the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme. We perform the perturbative matching from RI-SMOM schemes to the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme Qi′⁣MS¯​(μ)=∑j∈𝒞Ri​jMS¯←RI​(μ)​Qj′⁣RI​(μ),superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′¯MS𝜇subscript𝑗𝒞superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖𝑗←¯MSRI𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑗′RI𝜇Qiprime,rmoverlineMS(mu)=sumjincal CRijrmoverlineMSleftarrow RI(mu)Qjprime,rm RI(mu), where we suppose μ𝜇mu to be the high scale μhsubscript𝜇ℎmuh after the step scaling. We use the one-loop matching matrices between the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS and RI-SMOM schemes given in Ref. citebib.bib79. We use the strong coupling constant αsNf=3​(μh≈4.0​GeV)=0.2167​,superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑁𝑓3subscript𝜇ℎ4.0GeV0.216731alphasNf=3(muhapprox 4.0mboxGeV)=0.2167, which is determined as follows. We first input the Particle Data Group (PDG) value αsNf=5​(MZ)=0.1179​superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑁𝑓5subscript𝑀𝑍0.11799alphasNf=5(MZ)=0.1179citebib.bib6 given at the Z𝑍Z pole in the five flavor theory and perform its scale evolution with the four-loop β𝛽beta functioncitebib.bib84, bib.bib85, changing the number Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓Nf of active quark flavors from 5 to 4 at the bottom threshold. From this procedure, we obtain the four-flavor ΛQCDNf=4superscriptsubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑁𝑓4Lambdarm QCDNf=4 parameter. Using this parameter as input, we use two-loop scale evolution to the charm threshold with Nf=4subscript𝑁𝑓4Nf=4 and, again, two-loop evolution with Nf=3subscript𝑁𝑓3Nf=3 to obtain αsNf=3superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑁𝑓3alphasNf=3 given in Eq. which is consistent with the order of the perturbative calculation of the Wilson coefficients. Table shows the perturbative matching matrices from the SMOM(γμ,γμ)subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇(gammamu,gammamu) and SMOM(q/,q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) schemes to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme.

These renormalization processes can create systematic errors because of the mixing with excluded operators, the discretization error on the renormalization condition and the truncation of perturbative matching. It was found in the previous workcitebib.bib2 that these sources of systematic errors are less significant than some major ones such as Wilson coefficients. However it should be noted that the investigation on the systematic errors was done on a finer lattice with the inverse lattice spacing of a32​I​D−1≈1.38superscriptsubscript𝑎32ID11.38arm 32ID-1approx 1.38GeV, where the subscript ‘32ID’ denotes the ensemble used in Ref. citebib.bib2, and that one of the intermediate scales we employ in this work is the same as the one used in the previous work in physical units, μ2≈1.48​a−1≈1.11​a32​I​D−1subscript𝜇21.48superscript𝑎11.11subscriptsuperscript𝑎132IDmu2approx 1.48a-1approx 1.11a-1rm 32ID. This is because we need to match the momenta p1subscript𝑝1p1 and p2subscript𝑝2p2 in the renormalization conditions to ensure the renormalized matrix elements on both ensembles are on the same scaling trajectory for the continuum extrapolation. While we do not take the continuum limit at this stage, it is important to verify whether μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 is a good choice for the continuum limit, since we plan to take it in the near future. In summary the renormalization conditions in this work are imposed at relatively high scales in lattice units and therefore it is important to revisit the investigation of the systematic error from NPR. Since the systematic error considered in this context is a discretization effect in the renormalization conditions, it is reasonable to isolate the various components from continuum perturbation theory. Thus we analyze the matrix IRI​(μ2,μ1)=ZRI←lat​(μ2)−1​ΣRI​(μ2,μ1)​ZRI←lat​(μ1),superscript𝐼RIsubscript𝜇2subscript𝜇1superscript𝑍←RIlatsuperscriptsubscript𝜇21superscriptΣRIsubscript𝜇2subscript𝜇1superscript𝑍←RIlatsubscript𝜇1Irm RI(mu2,mu1)=Zrm RIleftarrow lat(mu2)-1Sigmarm RI(mu2,mu1)Zrm RIleftarrow lat(mu1), which is computed purely on the lattice. Here the Z matrices are computed on the 243superscript243243 lattice and the step-scaling matrix ΣΣSigma on the 32Ifine lattice. Table shows the results for IRI​(μ2,μ1)superscript𝐼RIsubscript𝜇2subscript𝜇1Irm RI(mu2,mu1) for the SMOM(γμ,γμsubscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇gammamu,gammamu) and SMOM(q/,q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) schemes. The difference between this matrix and the 7×7777times 7 unit matrix should represent the systematic error in the nonperturbative renormalization. While most of the off-diagonal elements are consistent with zero, many of the diagonal elements significantly deviate from one. Especially I66SMOM​(γμ,γμ)superscriptsubscript𝐼66SMOMsubscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇I66rm SMOM(gammamu,gammamu) and I88SMOM​(γμ,γμ)superscriptsubscript𝐼88SMOMsubscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇I88rm SMOM(gammamu,gammamu) show almost 30% deviations from unity. If the systematic errors on the Z matrix at μ1subscript𝜇1mu1 and μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 are similar, the corresponding renormalization matrix would have roughly a 15% systematic error. However we expect the systematic error at μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 could be significantly larger than at μ1≈1.28​a−1subscript𝜇11.28superscript𝑎1mu1approx 1.28a-1 because a similar investigation made in SectionVII. G in Ref. citebib.bib2 indicates a much smaller systematic error at μ2≈1.11​a32​I​D−1subscript𝜇21.11superscriptsubscript𝑎32ID1mu2approx 1.11arm 32ID-1. We discuss the actual impact of this difference on the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes and quote the corresponding systematic error in the following section.

We now explicitly introduce the isospin index I𝐼I of the final two-pion state to express the matrix elements MI,n,ilatsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑖latMI,n,irm lat on the lattice, although it was omitted in Section. Here we also introduce the superscript ‘lat’ indicating the unrenormalized matrix elements. In this subsection we convert these matrix elements obtained in Section to those in the chiral seven-operator basis, MI,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′latMI,n,jprime,rm lat. We follow the procedure applied in Ref. citebib.bib2 to optimize the statistical precision of the chiral-basis matrix elements. While the matrix elements in the ten-operator basis are uniquely expressed in terms of those in the chiral basis, MI,n,ilat=∑j∈𝒞Ti​j​MI,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑖latsubscript𝑗𝒞subscript𝑇𝑖𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′latMI,n,irm lat=sumjincal CTijMI,n,jprime,rm lat with the known matrix T𝑇T, the inverse problem to determine MI,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′latMI,n,jprime,rm lat is ill-defined. In other words, there are infinite number of combinations of the matrix elements in the ten-operator basis to express those in the chiral basis. Because of the violation of the Fierz symmetry due to the stochastic A2A method, the statistical precision of the chiral-basis matrix elements depends on the combinations of the ten matrix elements and can be optimized by a correlated χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 fit to Eq. . We perform the fit for each isospin channel and each two-pion final state labeled by n𝑛n individually. For the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel, we use the ten values of matrix elements, i=1,2,…,10𝑖12…10i=1,2,ldots,10, for the fit to determine the seven values of M0,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀0𝑛𝑗′latM0,n,jprime,rm lat for j∈𝒞𝑗𝒞jincal C. For the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel there is no contribution from the QCD penguin operators. Out of the remaining six matrix elements with the relevant operators, M(I=)2,n,9latM(I=)2,n,9rm lat and M2,n,10latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛10latM2,n,10rm lat are obtained from the identical contractions as M2,n,1latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛1latM2,n,1rm lat and M2,n,2latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛2latM2,n,2rm lat, respectively, as seen in Appendix, and these pairs of matrix elements are therefore 100% correlated. We hence use only four matrix elements M2,n,1latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛1latM2,n,1rm lat, M2,n,2latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛2latM2,n,2rm lat, M2,n,7latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛7latM2,n,7rm lat and M2,n,8latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛8latM2,n,8rm lat for the fit to determine the three linearly independent matrix elements M2,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛𝑗′latM2,n,jprime,rm lat for j=1,7,8𝑗178j=1,7,8. The results are summarized in Table for the both I=2𝐼2I=2 and 0 two-pion final states of the three lowest energy levels n=0,1,2𝑛012n=0,1,2.

In Section we obtained the results for the renormalization matrix Zk​lRI←lat​(μ)superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑘𝑙←RIlat𝜇Zklrm RIleftarrow lat(mu) and the step-scaling matrix Σj​kRI​(μh,μ)subscriptsuperscriptΣRI𝑗𝑘subscript𝜇ℎ𝜇Sigmarm RIjk(muh,mu) for μ=μ1,μ2𝜇subscript𝜇1subscript𝜇2mu=mu1,mu2 and for two RI schemes, SMOM(γμ,γμsubscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇gammamu,gammamu) and SMOM(q/,q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/). We also perform a finite-volume correction using the LL factors FI,nsubscript𝐹𝐼𝑛FI,n obtained in Section. Here the isospin and state indices I𝐼I and n𝑛n, respectively, which are now explicitly added to the LL factor, are not summed over. Now we interpolate these results to the physical kinematics and determine the on-shell matrix elements MI,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎMI,jprime,rm RI(muh), which are expressed without a state index. For the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel the behavior of the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements in the unphysical-kinematics region encompassing the Eπ​πsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋Epipi-dependence was investigated using ChPT in Ref. citebib.bib14, which indicates the matrix elements in the rest frame receive the same order of contributions from Eπ​πsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋Epipi-independent, Eπ​πsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋Epipi and Eπ​π2superscriptsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋2Epipi2 terms in units of mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK. Because of many unknown low-energy constants, the coefficients for these terms are not known. Ideally, we would perform this interpolation with at least three data points in order to account for these different contributions. However we expect involving the second excited two-pion state with the energy 700MeV for I=0𝐼0I=0 and 800MeV for I=2𝐼2I=2 in the interpolation would unnecessarily increase the systematic error mainly because this energy level is well beyond the range that any ChPT-inspired interpolation function might be expected to be reliable. Also this energy region is above the four-pion threshold where the LL factor is not a strictly accurate factor to normalize the finite-volume two-pion state to the infinite-volume one. For these reasons we only use the off-shell matrix elements with the ground (M2,0,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀20𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎM2,0,jprime,rm RI(muh)) and first-excited (M2,1,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀21𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎM2,1,jprime,rm RI(muh)) two-pion final states to determine the matrix elements at the on-shell point. While the energy of the first-excited state is also above the four-pion threshold for this isospin channel, some earlier works suggest that the systematic error on the Lüscher formalism for scattering phase shifts, which is closely related to the LL factor, is not significant when the energy is slightly above the four-pion thresholdcitebib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib43, bib.bib76. While further comments on the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel are given in the next paragraph, we perform two interpolations with the following functions for both Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 and Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channels MI,n,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎdisplaystyle MI,n,jprime,rm RI(muh) =MI,j′⁣RI,lin​(μh)+cI,jRI,lin​EI,n−mKmK,absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIlinsubscript𝜇ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝐼𝑗RIlinsubscript𝐸𝐼𝑛subscript𝑚𝐾subscript𝑚𝐾displaystyle=MI,jprime,rm RI,lin(muh)+cI,jrm RI,linfracEI,n-mKmK, MI,n,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎdisplaystyle MI,n,jprime,rm RI(muh) =MI,j′⁣RI,quad​(μh)+cI,jRI,quad​EI,n2−mK2mK2,absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIquadsubscript𝜇ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝐼𝑗RIquadsuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝐼𝑛2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐾2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐾2displaystyle=MI,jprime,rm RI,quad(muh)+cI,jrm RI,quadfracEI,n2-mK2mK2, where we input the kaon mass mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK and isospin-I𝐼I two-pion energies EI,nsubscript𝐸𝐼𝑛EI,n obtained in Section and the off-shell matrix elements MI,n,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎMI,n,jprime,rm RI(muh) obtained in Eq. to determine the parameters MI,j′⁣RI,lin/quad​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIlinquadsubscript𝜇ℎMI,jprime,rm RI,lin/quad(muh) and cI,jlin/quadsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝐼𝑗linquadcI,jrm lin/quad. We use MI,j′⁣RI,lin​(μh)subscriptsuperscript𝑀′RIlin𝐼𝑗subscript𝜇ℎMprime,rm RI,linI,j(muh) as our primary result for MI,j′⁣RI​(μh)subscriptsuperscript𝑀′RI𝐼𝑗subscript𝜇ℎMprime,rm RII,j(muh) for calculation of the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS matrix elements MI,iMS¯​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑖¯MSsubscript𝜇ℎMI,irmoverlineMS(muh) in Eq. , while MI,j′⁣RI,quad​(μh)subscriptsuperscript𝑀′RIquad𝐼𝑗subscript𝜇ℎMprime,rm RI,quadI,j(muh) is used to estimate the systematic error from the interpolation (see Appendix). We quote the systematic error due to the assumption of the somewhat incomplete functions and by taking the difference between MI,j′⁣RI,lin​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIlinsubscript𝜇ℎMI,jprime,rm RI,lin(muh) and MI,j′⁣RI,quad​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIquadsubscript𝜇ℎMI,jprime,rm RI,quad(muh). For the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel, since the mixing contribution from lower dimensional operators that vanish by the equations of motion is subtracted in a different way for determining the unrenormalized matrix elements in Section and for calculating the renormalization matrix in Section, multiplying the renormalization matrix with the matrix elements may not eliminate the logarithmic divergence. This remaining divergence depends on the two-pion energy and vanishes at the on-shell point. Therefore the on-shell K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitude is still physical as long as the interpolation to the on-shell point is successfully done. We perform the interpolations based on the same assumptions as for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel using Eqs and . The systematic error due to the interpolation is estimated as the difference from the quadratic interpolation and expressed by the yellow band. For the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel since the energy E0,1subscript𝐸01E0,1 of the first-excited two-pion state is close to mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK and the disconnected diagram increases the statistical error, the systematic error due to the interpolation in this isospin channel is small compared to the statistical error (green band). We follow the procedure explained in Ref. citebib.bib2 for computing the Wilson coefficients using the NLO perturbative expansion given in Ref. citebib.bib7, bib.bib8. The Wilson coefficients depend on many Standard-Model parameters that accommodate the effects of higher-energy physics and were summarized in TableXI of Ref. citebib.bib2. Since some of the values of these parameters have been updated citebib.bib6, we update the Wilson coefficients accordingly employing the latest values of these parameters. Table summarizes the Wilson coefficients. Here we use the value of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas given in Eq. . Some of the CKM matrix elements have also been updated citebib.bib6, and we therefore use their updated values and the parameter τ=−Vt​s∗​Vt​d/Vu​s∗​Vu​d𝜏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑡𝑠subscript𝑉𝑡𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑tau=-Vts*Vtd/Vus*Vud in Eq. . The new value of τ=0.001473​−0.000640​​i𝜏0.001473670.00064033itau=0.001473-0.000640rmi is determined by using the Wolfenstein parametrization expanded to the eighth order with the parameters given in Ref. citebib.bib6.

We use Eq. to determine the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI with the Wilson coefficients and the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS matrix elements given in the previous subsections. It is known that the matrix elements of different four-quark operators contribute in different ways to Re(AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI) and Im(AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI). In this subsection we discuss these different compositions as well as the final results for each Re(AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI) and Im(AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI). The contributions from each MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS matrix element at μhsubscript𝜇ℎmuh to A2subscript𝐴2A2 (the summands on the right hand side of Eq. for I=2𝐼2I=2) and their total value are summarized in Table for the real part and in Table for the imaginary part. The results obtained through multiple intermediate schemes and scales are presented. Re(A2subscript𝐴2A2) receives the dominant contribution from Q2subscript𝑄2Q2 with 20% cancellation from the subdominant contribution of Q1subscript𝑄1Q1. Im(A2subscript𝐴2A2) is contributed mainly by Q8subscript𝑄8Q8 with 30% cancellation from Q7subscript𝑄7Q7 and Q9subscript𝑄9Q9. The same breakdowns for the I=0𝐼0I=0 two-pion final state are tabulated in Tables and for Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0) and Im(A0subscript𝐴0A0), respectively. Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0) receives the dominant contribution from the matrix element of Q2subscript𝑄2Q2. It is interesting to observe that Q2subscript𝑄2Q2, which accounts for most of Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0), is the original operator for weak interaction, i. e. formulae-sequence𝑖𝑒i. e. s→u​W−→𝑠𝑢superscript𝑊sto uW- followed by W−→u¯​d→superscript𝑊¯𝑢𝑑W-tobarud. Thus, what was required to quantitatively understand Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0) was a proper non-perturbative framework for calculating the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix element of Q2subscript𝑄2Q2. As in the previous lattice calculationcitebib.bib2 we again find the QCD penguin operator Q6subscript𝑄6Q6citebib.bib86 only plays a subdominant (∼20%similar-toabsentpercent20sim 20%) role in Re(A0)subscript𝐴0(A0), which is significantly canceled by another subdominant contribution of Q1subscript𝑄1Q1. On the other hand, Im(A0subscript𝐴0A0) is dominated by the matrix element of Q6subscript𝑄6Q6. The contributions from the other operators shift the result by 15%. This observation about Im(A0subscript𝐴0A0) is also consistent with our previous GPBC calculationcitebib.bib2. There is a significant dependence on the intermediate renormalization procedure as observed in the NPR study (Section) and in the renormalized matrix elements (Section). Such a large difference was not observed in the GPBC workcitebib.bib2 on a finer lattice of a32​I​D−1≈1.38superscriptsubscript𝑎32ID11.38arm 32ID-1approx 1.38GeV and is likely due to finite lattice spacing error as it appears more significant for μ2≈1.5subscript𝜇21.5mu2approx 1.5GeV. The difference is taken into account as a part of systematic uncertainty discussed below. We choose the results obtained through the SMOM(q/,q/)(hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) intermediate scheme and scale μ1subscript𝜇1mu1 as the central value of our final results, which read Re​(A2)Resubscript𝐴2displaystylerm Re(A2) =1.74​​×10−8​GeV,absent1.741548superscript108GeVdisplaystyle=1.74times 10-8rm GeV, Im​(A2)Imsubscript𝐴2displaystylerm Im(A2) =−5.91​​(1.75)×10−13​GeV,absent5.91131.75superscript1013GeVdisplaystyle=-5.91(1.75)times 10-13rm GeV, Re​(A0)Resubscript𝐴0displaystylerm Re(A0) =2.84​​×10−7​GeV,absent2.845787superscript107GeVdisplaystyle=2.84times 10-7rm GeV, Im​(A0)Imsubscript𝐴0displaystylerm Im(A0) =−8.7​(1.2)​(2.6)×10−11​GeV,absent8.71.22.6superscript1011GeVdisplaystyle=-8.7(1.2)(2.6)times 10-11rm GeV, and are consistent with our earlier works, Ref. citebib.bib19 for A2subscript𝐴2A2 and Ref. citebib.bib2 for A0subscript𝐴0A0. The first error is statistical, while the second error is the combined systematic error, whose breakdown is summarized in Table. The NPR error is estimated as half of the maximum difference from the other sets of intermediate scheme and scale. The reason for halving is because the intermediate scale μ2subscript𝜇2mu2, which always results in the largest deviation from the central value and hence determines the NPR error, may not be sufficiently small on this coarse lattice as discussed in Section. The error from the on-shell limit is discussed in detail in Appendix but basically estimated by propagating the difference between MI,j′SMOM(q/,q/),lin​(μh)MI,jprime,rm SMOM(scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/,scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/),rm lin(muh) and MI,j′SMOM(q/,q/),quad​(μh)MI,jprime,rm SMOM(scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/,scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/),rm quad(muh) as an error on MI,j′SMOM(q/,q/)​(μh)MI,jprime,rm SMOM(scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/,scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/)(muh) in Eq. . The other errors are quoted based on Ref. citebib.bib2 as there is no reason to expect significant difference from our earlier estimations. The finite lattice spacing error is extended with an assumption of the O​(a2)𝑂superscript𝑎2O(a2) scaling and the other errors with ‘∗*’ are the same as in Ref. citebib.bib2. The error from the Wilson coefficients is an estimate dominated mostly by the matching between the three- and four-flavor theories, that needs to be done below the charm threshold. We could in principle bypass the matching for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel at one-loop level because it is to accommodate the effects of matrix elements of four-quark operators including the charm and anti-charm quarks, which are all Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2. In this paper we still assign a 12% Wilson-coefficient error to Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel since we have not computed the renormalization matrices in the four-flavor theory that could be used with the more accurate Wilson coefficients for this isospin channel and instead use the same renormalization procedure and Wilson coefficients as for the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel. The last error, which is from finite lattice spacing, is estimated with the assumption of O​(a2)𝑂superscript𝑎2O(a2) scaling with the corresponding error estimated in Ref. citebib.bib2. This estimate is based on our earlier results for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel. We also considered another estimation of the systematic error from the finite lattice spacing by comparing the results from this work with our earlier results. Table shows a comparison of A2subscript𝐴2A2 from this work and earlier works on the 323×64superscript32364323times 64 lattice with the Iwasaki ++ DSDR gauge actioncitebib.bib18 and on the 483×64superscript48364483times 64 and 643×128superscript643128643times 128 lattices with the Iwasaki gauge actioncitebib.bib19. The chiral extrapolation of the results obtained with non-unitary light quark masses was performed in the former reference, while the physical pion and kaon masses are realized, and the continuum limit was taken in the latter work. The values of Im(A2subscript𝐴2A2) from earlier works are modified according to the significantly updated value of the τ𝜏tau parameter. Table shows a comparison of A0subscript𝐴0A0 between this work and our earlier work on the 323×64superscript32364323times 64 lattice with GPBC at the physical pion and kaon massescitebib.bib2. The first error on each value in these tables is statistical and the second error combines the listed systematic errors in quadrature except for the discretization error. The tables indicate that these errors are not small enough to estimate the finite lattice spacing error from the comparisons. Therefore we stick with the estimation of the finite lattice spacing error given in the previous paragraph, though it is estimated from the results for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel and may not be an ideal estimation for the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel. Unmasking the discretization error more accurately and taking the continuum limit of the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel are important goals in the near future. With the quoted values of the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitude we can test the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 rule, which is an experimental fact that kaons are roughly 450 times more likely to decay into the I=0𝐼0I=0 channel of two pions than the I=2𝐼2I=2 counterpart, Re​(A0)/Re​(A2)=22.45​Resubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴222.456mboxRe(A0)/mboxRe(A2)=22.45. While leading-order perturbative QCD can explain the difference in the amplitudes only up to the factor of 2citebib.bib87, bib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib90, some theoretical indications of this fact were seen when we performed lattice calculations of the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channelcitebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib91, where we observed a sizable cancellation between the two Wick contractions of the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi three-point function with the dominant (27,1)271(27,1) operator. In our most recent work on the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel with GPBCcitebib.bib2, we were able to numerically verify it by combining the result for Re(A0)subscript𝐴0(A0) from the GPBC work with the continuum limit of Re(A2)subscript𝐴2(A2) from Ref. citebib.bib19, Re​(A0)/Re​(A2)=19.9​(2.3)​(4.4)Resubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴219.92.34.4mboxRe(A0)/mboxRe(A2)=19.9(2.3)(4.4), with the statistical and systematic errors, respectively. Here we quote the corresponding value calculated on the 243superscript243243 ensemble Re​(A0)/Re​(A2)=16.3​(3.7)​(6.7),Resubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴216.33.76.7mboxRe(A0)/mboxRe(A2)=16.3(3.7)(6.7), where the errors are, again, statistical and systematic, respectively.

Now we quote our numerical result for the measure of direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation, Re(ε′/ε)superscript𝜀′𝜀(varepsilonprime/varepsilon). Writing ε=|ε|​ei​ϕε𝜀𝜀superscripteisubscriptitalic-ϕ𝜀varepsilon=|varepsilon|rmermiphivarepsilon, the phase of ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon defined in Eq. is found to be δ2−δ0+π/2−ϕεsubscript𝛿2subscript𝛿0𝜋2subscriptitalic-ϕ𝜀delta2-delta0+pi/2-phivarepsilon, where π/2𝜋2pi/2 comes from the overall factor iirmi on the right hand side. Using the value ϕε=43.52​∘subscriptitalic-ϕ𝜀43.52superscript2phivarepsilon=43.52circ citebib.bib6 and the prediction from the dispersion theory with inputs from experiment and ChPT citebib.bib77 δ2−δ0=−44.8​(3.0)∘subscript𝛿2subscript𝛿044.8superscript3.0delta2-delta0=-44.8(3.0)circ at 497.6MeV, the experimental value of the K0superscript𝐾0K0 mass, we can verify the phase of ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon is approximately zero. Lattice predictions for the phase shifts are also consistent with the dispersive approach citebib.bib44, bib.bib43. The potential difference of the phase from zero does not affect the following results that have much larger statistical and systematic errors. Therefore we simply drop the phase term for the real part of ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon and find Re​(ε′ε)=ω2​|ε|​[Im​(A2)Re​(A2)−Im​(A0)Re​(A0)]. Resuperscript𝜀′𝜀𝜔2𝜀delimited-Imsubscript𝐴2Resubscript𝐴2Imsubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴0mboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)=fracomegasqrt2|varepsilon|left[fracmboxIm(A2)mboxRe(A2)-fracmboxIm(A0)mboxRe(A0)right]. It is illustrative to analyze the two terms on the right hand side separately. The first term of the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel is called electroweak penguin (EWP) term Re(ε′/ε)EWPsubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀EWP(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm EWP, while the second term of Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 is the QCD penguin (QCDP) term Re(ε′/ε)QCDPsubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀QCDP(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm QCDP. Using the results for AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI discussed in the previous subsection we obtain Re​(ε′ε)EWPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀EWPdisplaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)rm EWP =−4.80​​(1.95)×10−4,absent4.80491.95superscript104displaystyle=-4.80(1.95)times 10-4, Re​(ε′ε)QCDPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀QCDPdisplaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)rm QCDP =43.4​(12.9)​(18.5)×10−4,absent43.412.918.5superscript104displaystyle=43.4(12.9)(18.5)times 10-4, Re​(ε′ε)Resuperscript𝜀′𝜀displaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright) =38.6​(13.0)​(18.6)×10−4,absent38.613.018.6superscript104displaystyle=38.6(13.0)(18.6)times 10-4, where the errors are statistical and systematic, respectively. The results indicate that the QCDP part is the dominant source of both the statistical and systematic errors. We could reduce the errors if we input experimental values of the real parts, Re(A2)exp=1.479​×10−8subscriptsubscript𝐴2exp1.4794superscript108(A2)rm exp=1.479times 10-8GeV and Re(A0)exp=3.3201​×10−7subscriptsubscript𝐴0exp3.320118superscript107(A0)rm exp=3.3201times 10-7GeV, which are much more precise than those determined on the lattice. To give our best prediction for the ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon we also use the continuum limit of Im(A2subscript𝐴2A2) determined in Ref. citebib.bib19. Since Im(A2subscript𝐴2A2) depends on the τ𝜏tau parameter that has been significantly updated, we use the modified value shown in Table. We only use Im(A0)subscript𝐴0(A0) from this work. The EWP and QCDP contributions to Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) obtained with this approach read Re​(ε′ε)EWPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀EWPdisplaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)rm EWP =−7.69​​×10−4,absent7.692292superscript104displaystyle=-7.69times 10-4, Re​(ε′ε)QCDPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀QCDPdisplaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)rm QCDP =37.1​(5.2)​(11.1)×10−4,absent37.15.211.1superscript104displaystyle=37.1(5.2)(11.1)times 10-4, where the errors are statistical and systematic, respectively. In is interesting to note that the contribution from QCD penguin operators to Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) is sizable compared to that from the electroweak penguin operators. We obtain the ratio Re​(ε′/ε)QCDP/Re​(ε′/ε)EWP=−4.83​​(1.55)Resubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀QCDPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀EWP4.83691.55rm Re(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm QCDP/rm Re(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm EWP=-4.83(1.55), which is consistent with the one from the GPBC calculationcitebib.bib2, −3.73​​3.735689-3.73. Before quoting our final result for Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) we note that this quantity could receive significant corrections due to electromagnetic and isospin-breaking effects as discussed in Refs. citebib.bib49, bib.bib2. We quote the same size of this systematic error as the third error as in Ref. citebib.bib2 and obtain the final result Re​(ε′ε)=29.4​(5.2)​(11.1)​(5.0)×10−4. Resuperscript𝜀′𝜀29.45.211.15.0superscript104mboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)=29.4(5.2)(11.1)(5.0)times 10-4. While the errors are somewhat large, this result is compatible with the experimental value, Re(ε′/ε)exp=16.6(2.3)varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm exp=16.6(2.3)citebib.bib6.
content_sections.VII Conclusion: We have presented a new calculation of Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 and Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 processes in K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi decays using periodic boundary conditions, essentially extracting the physical amplitudes from the dominant excited state with near physical kinematics, Eπ​π=mKsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋subscript𝑚𝐾Epipi=mK. The calculation was done on a 243×64superscript24364243times 64 domain wall fermion ensemble with a−1=1.023superscript𝑎11.023a-1=1.023GeV at the physical pion and kaon masses. The GEVP method was used to compute the matrix elements and amplitudes with multiple two-pion final states in finite volume. After applying the Lellouch-Lüscher formalism to normalize these two-pion states to their infinite volume counterparts, the amplitudes were interpolated to physical kinematics while the energy of the I=0𝐼0I=0 first excited two-pion state is only 5% off from mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK. We obtained a value for the well known measure of direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation, Re(ε′/ε)=29.4​(5.2)​(11.1)​(5.0)×10−4superscript𝜀′𝜀29.45.211.15.0superscript104(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)=29.4(5.2)(11.1)(5.0)times 10-4, where the first error is statistical, the second is all systematics in an isospin symmetric world, and the third combines uncertainties in estimates of electromagnetic and isospin-breaking corrections in the real world. The result is consistent with our previous GPBC result, Re(ε′/ε)2020=21.7​(2.6)​(6.2)​(5.0)×10−4subscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀202021.72.66.25.0superscript104(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)2020=21.7(2.6)(6.2)(5.0)times 10-4citebib.bib2. In Figure we summarize all our lattice results for Re(ε′/ε)superscript𝜀′𝜀(varepsilonprime/varepsilon) obtained earlier with GPBCcitebib.bib1, bib.bib2 and in this work comparing with the world average of experimental resultscitebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6. Note that our recent analysescitebib.bib2 with multiple two-pion operators and larger statistics revealed that the excited-state systematic error was substantially underestimated in our original, 2015 workcitebib.bib1 that included only a single operator, hence the discrepancy observable in the figure. The inclusion of the additional operators, particularly the σ𝜎sigma-like operator, dramatically reduced this error. While the statistical and systematic errors in the present work are larger because of fewer measurements on a coarser lattice, the results are promising enough to motivate us to perform calculations with more configurations and on a finer lattice ensemble that are on-going. Therefore we expect more precise results will be obtained in the near future. The on-going calculations are on the 323×64superscript32364323times 64 Iwasaki ++ DSDR ensemble, which has the same lattice spacing as on the GPBC ensemble, as well as to increase the statistics on the 243×64superscript24364243times 64 ensemble employed here. We will attempt to take the continuum limit with these two ensembles. This may be challenging due to possible discretization effects on the a−1=1.023superscript𝑎11.023a-1=1.023 GeV ensemble, both for NPR factors and matrix elements, beyond the leading O​(a2)𝑂superscript𝑎2O(a2) scaling errors. The validity of taking the continuum limit with these ensembles needs to be carefully investigated. There is another important systematic error due to the perturbative truncation of the Wilson coefficients in the three-flavor theory. This is not improved by step scaling because the matching of the Wilson coefficients in the four-flavor theory to those in the three-flavor theory needs to be done below the charm threshold. This error gives a 12% systematic uncertainty in the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes, which is as large as the finite lattice spacing error on the 323superscript323323 lattice. While the error will eventually be reduced when we have enough computational resources to calculate the matrix elements on fine enough lattices where we can introduce the charm quark and the corresponding four-quark operators explicitly, it is still desired to fix this defect sooner. Perturbative matching of the Wilson coefficients to NNLOcitebib.bib92, bib.bib93, bib.bib94, bib.bib95, bib.bib96 and an alternative nonperturbative matchingcitebib.bib97 are being studied. The single largest systematic error on ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon is from the electromagnetic and isospin-breaking corrections. Despite O​(1%)𝑂percent1O(1%) effects on many quantities, the small value of A2subscript𝐴2A2 relative to A0subscript𝐴0A0 could enhance the impact on ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon to O​(20%)𝑂percent20O(20%). In order to introduce electromagnetic effects in the lattice calculation there is a significant challenge posed by the extension of the formalism by Lüschercitebib.bib50 and Lellouch-Lüschercitebib.bib51 to two-hadron systems in finite volumes due to the long-distance character of electromagnetism. One such approach under development by our collaborationcitebib.bib54 uses the Coulomb gauge to break the electromagnetic contributions into those of a static Coulomb potential and of transverse radiation. The truncation of the Coulomb potential allows the former to be treated on the lattice, and the effects of the truncation can be reintroduced through conventional quantum mechanics on the basis that they describe long-distance physics that is not capable of resolving the structure of the interaction. There has been progress in the former, but challenges remain to be overcome with the transverse radiation contributions before the approach can be applied to the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi decay calculation. Although a complete formalism for introducing these effects has not been constructed, we expect that the incorporation of elctromagnetism and strong isospin-breaking in the method with PBC may be easier compared with GPBC where the electric charge is not conserved. Department of Energy (DOE) for partial support under awards DE-FG02-92ER40716 (TB, DH), DE-SC0010339 (TB, LJ, MT), DE-SC0012704 (PAB, TI, CJ, AS) and DE-SC001704 (AS). LJ and MT were also supported in part by the U. S. DOE under Early Career Award No. DE-SC0021147. The work of CK was supported by the U. S. DOE Exascale Computing Project. TI is also supported by the Department of Energy, Laboratory Directed Research and Development (LDRD No. 23-051) of BNL and RIKEN BNL Research Center. The work of DH was supported by the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) through grant No. 200020208222. We thank the collaborators in the RBC and UKQCD collaborations, especially N. H. Christ, for fruitful discussions. The software used for this work is based on , , and . Computations were carried out with USQCD resources funded by the US DOE at BNL and JLab.

sorted_papers.title:
Mass and isovector matrix elements of the nucleon at zero-momentum
  transfer
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present the current status of our analysis of nucleon structure
observables including isovector charges and twist-2 matrix elements as well as
the nucleon mass. Results are computed on a large set of CLS $N_f=2+1$ gauge
ensembles with $M_\pi\approx0.130\mathrm{MeV}\ldots 350\mathrm{MeV}$, four
values of the lattice spacing $a=0.05\mathrm{fm}\ldots 0.09\mathrm{fm}$ and
covering a large range of physical volumes. Compared to the results presented
at last year's conference we have added data on a very fine and large box at
small light quark mass ($T\times L^3=192 \times 96^3$, $M_\pi=172\mathrm{MeV}$,
$a=0.05\mathrm{fm}$). Additional (intermediate) source-sink separations have
been computed on the coarser ensembles, further increasing effective statistics
and allowing for a more fine-grained control in the treatment of the
excited-state contamination. Excited states in the nucleon matrix elements are
tamed by a simultaneous, two-state fit ansatz using the summation method.

qualitative: Excited-state contamination in nucleon matrix elements at zero-momentum transfer is a major challenge in lattice QCD due to the signal-to-noise problem.
The nucleon mass and isovector matrix elements are used as benchmarks for testing methods to control excited-state contamination.
A two-state fit ansatz using the summation method is employed to simultaneously fit ground and first excited state contributions, improving control over excited-state contamination.
The use of multiple source-sink separations, especially at smaller tsep, enhances statistical precision and allows for better resolution of excited-state effects.
The addition of data at very fine lattice spacing and small pion mass (e.g., E300 ensemble) improves the chiral and continuum extrapolation, reducing systematic uncertainties from excited states.
Open boundary conditions in time help mitigate topological charge freezing, which is important for maintaining the reliability of the lattice data.
The fitting strategy is designed to maintain effective statistics across different source-sink separations, avoiding exponential decay of signal-to-noise ratio.
quantitative: Ensemble E300: T×L³ = 192 × 96³, Mπ ≈ 172 MeV, a = 0.05 fm
MπL ≈ 173 MeV × L ≈ 173 MeV × 96 × 0.05 fm ≈ 8.3 fm (for E300, implying large physical volume)
Lattice spacings: a = 0.05 fm to 0.09 fm
Pion masses: Mπ ≈ 130 MeV to 350 MeV
Source-sink separations tsep range from small values up to tsep ≳ 1 fm
Renormalization scale: t0/a² used to form dimensionless quantities
Physical scale: 8t₀ = 0.415(stat) ± 0.015(sys) fm²
The number of measurements Nmeas scales with tsep such that effective statistics remain approximately constant for tsep ≲ 1 fm on oBC ensembles
