These matrix elements are the main targets of this work as they correspond to Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel of near on-shell K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements, which are necessary to calculate the measure ε′superscript𝜀′varepsilonprime of direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation and require considerable effort to compute. Similar to the case of the ground two-pion final state, the χ2/d. o. f. formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜒2𝑑𝑜𝑓chi2/d. o. f. is fairly stable for various fit ranges. One noticeable difference from the ground-state case is that the contamination from kaon excited states is less significant so that we can choose a wider fit range with a smaller t1minsuperscriptsubscript𝑡1mint1rm min. Again we use the same fit range for all four-quark operators to quote the final result. Figure shows the fit results for Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 matrix elements obtained through the GEVP analyses with various sets of two-pion sink operators with and without the σ𝜎sigma-like operator. The upper panels indicate that, while the results for the matrix elements with the two-pion ground state are mostly independent of the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-like operators with a non-zero relative momentum, adding the σ𝜎sigma-like operator makes a significant impact up to t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a for M0,2subscript𝑀02M0,2 and t2min=3​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min3𝑎t2rm min=3a for M0,6subscript𝑀06M0,6. The benefit of including the σ𝜎sigma-like operator is quite apparent in M0,6subscript𝑀06M0,6, which shows a plateau beginning at t2min=2​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min2𝑎t2rm min=2a with the σ𝜎sigma-like operator, whereas the values differ by more than 50% in this low-time region when the σ𝜎sigma-like operator is excluded. A similar trend is observed for the matrix elements with the first-excited two-pion state shown on the lower panels. The only difference in the trend between the ground- and first-excited two-pion final states is observed for “N=2𝑁2N=2 w σ𝜎sigma,” the results with the two operators π​π​𝜋𝜋000pipi and σ𝜎sigma, where the matrix elements with the first-excited state are too noisy to be in the lower panels. This may result from insufficient coupling between the first excited state and these two operators. We observe that the precision is significantly improved by including the π​π​𝜋𝜋001pipi operator. The π​π​𝜋𝜋001pipi operator is the most naïve operator for creating a two-pion state with an energy close to the kaon mass. It plays a similar role to the ‘π​π​𝜋𝜋111pipi’ operator in the GPBC work, which comprised two single-pion operators with back-to-back momenta of π/L𝜋𝐿pi/L in each spatial direction. The ‘π​π​𝜋𝜋111pipi’ operator was the only two-pion operator included in the original GPBC work, but was shown in Ref. citebib.bib2 to be insufficient to reliably isolate the required two-pion state. Our PBC results appear to verify this behavior. The lower panels indicate that, while the σ𝜎sigma-like operator plays an important role in removing excited-state contamination, including the π​π​𝜋𝜋001pipi operator is also important to obtain a well-resolved signal for the first excited state. The fit result for M1,6subscript𝑀16M1,6, which provides the dominant contribution to Im​(A0)Imsubscript𝐴0rm Im(A0) and therefore plays a crucial role in determining ε′superscript𝜀′varepsilonprime, appears to depend on t2minsuperscriptsubscript𝑡2mint2rm min still around our choice of t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a as seen in Figure. The consistency between “N=4𝑁4N=4 w σ𝜎sigma” and “N=5𝑁5N=5 w σ𝜎sigma” seen in Figure may not necessarily rule out the possibility of further excited-state contamination because the fourth excited state that can in principle be extracted by including the fifth operator, π​π​𝜋𝜋111pipi, is unresolved in our data and adding this operator might not play a role in isolating excited state effects. It is therefore valuable to demonstrate there is no evidence of further excited-state contamination for t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a. We find the difference between the fit results for M1,6subscript𝑀16M1,6 from “N=5𝑁5N=5 w σ𝜎sigma” with t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a and t2min=3​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min3𝑎t2rm min=3a under jackknife is consistent with zero, 0.0056 in lattice units. The O​(1​σ)𝑂1𝜎O(1sigma) difference between “N=3𝑁3N=3 w σ𝜎sigma” and “N=4𝑁4N=4 w σ𝜎sigma,” noticeable in M1,6subscript𝑀16M1,6 may indicate a removal of the contamination from the third excited state realized by additionally including the π​π​𝜋𝜋011pipi operator. Because this difference is roughly equal to the statistical error in the region t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a, the remaining contamination from the fourth and higher excited states is expected to be smaller than the statistical error. Our choice of t2min=4​asuperscriptsubscript𝑡2min4𝑎t2rm min=4a for the I=0𝐼0I=0 first-excited two-pion final state roughly corresponds to 0.8fm in physical units. A similar trend in the t2minsubscriptsuperscript𝑡min2trm min2-dependence of the fit result for the matrix element of Q6subscript𝑄6Q6 was observed at roughly equal values of t2minsuperscriptsubscript𝑡2mint2rm min in physical units in our earlier work with GPBCcitebib.bib2, where we concluded, after a serious investigation of systematic errors, it is not due to contamination from excited two-pion states but a simple statistical effects. Table summarizes the fit results for the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel of K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements Mn,isubscript𝑀𝑛𝑖Mn,i with n=0,1,2𝑛012n=0,1,2 and i=1𝑖1i=1–10. Compared with the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel most of the fits are stable and χ2/d. o. f. formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜒2𝑑𝑜𝑓chi2/d. o. f. is fairly small despite the wider fit ranges. It is interesting to observe the accuracy of Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 matrix elements for the ground, first-excited and second-excited states. For the ground state, which corresponds to two pions at rest, the matrix elements appear the most precise among the three. For the first-excite state, which is the closest among the three to the physical kinematics, the matrix elements are well determined. For the second-excited state, the matrix elements are also somewhat resolved for several four-quark operators. These observations suggest that the states have been successfully disentangled by the GEVP. It is thus remarkable that we have succeeded in extracting the matrix elements with excited two-pion final states with well-resolved signals.
content_sections.V Renormalization of four-quark operator: Renormalization of composite operators is an essential step for the lattice calculation of weak processes to remove unphysical divergences, as is the case in general for quantum field theory, before we can remove the regulator, i. e. formulae-sequence𝑖𝑒i. e. , take the continuum limit a→0→𝑎0ato 0. While the lattice operators are usually renormalized in a non-perturbative scheme, the corresponding Wilson coefficients are computed in the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS schemecitebib.bib7, bib.bib8. In order to appropriately construct the weak Hamiltonian and obtain the corresponding decay amplitudes, we need to convert the nonperturbatively renormalized matrix elements to the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme. As mentioned in Section we use the chiral basis of the seven four-quark operators Qi′superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′Qiprimecitebib.bib13, which has two advantages in nonperturbative renormalization (NPR). First, the linear independence of operators minimizes the number of renormalization conditions and gives well-defined inverse renormalization matrices, which are useful for step scalingcitebib.bib59. Second, each operator in this basis transforms as a specific representation of SU​L×SU​RSUsubscript3𝐿SUsubscript3𝑅rm SULtimesrm SUR chiral symmetry, while the operators Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Qi in the ten-operator basis in general combine multiple representations. Out of the seven operators, one transforms as the (27,1) representation, four as (8,1) and two as (8,8). The operators in the same representations still mix with each other but not with the other operators. As a result, the corresponding renormalization matrices are block diagonal. We employ a common momentum-space procedure using the regularization independent symmetric momentum (RI-SMOM) schemes, in which renormalization conditions are imposed nonperturbatively. Since the perturbative matching between the RI-SMOM and MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS schemes is available to the one-loop levelcitebib.bib79, RI-SMOM gives convenient intermediate schemes to obtain the matrix elements in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. We also perform step scaling as we previously observed a significant, three times improvement in the estimated systematic errors associated with the truncation of the perturbative matching to the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme when raising the renormalization scale from 1.53GeV to 4GeV through the step-scaling procedurecitebib.bib2. This procedure is more important for the present calculation due to the much coarser, 1.0GeV inverse lattice spacing versus 1.4GeV in the previous calculation, restricting the range of usable energy scales even further. Since the RI-SMOM renormalization conditions do not obey the equations of motion and require gauge fixing, we need to take into account the mixing with extra operators that vanish by the equations of motion or are gauge variant. In this work we take the mixing with only quark bilinears into account since the mixing with irrelevant operators with higher dimensions such as G1=s¯​γν​(1−γ5)​Dμ​Gμ​ν​dsubscript𝐺1¯𝑠subscript𝛾𝜈1subscript𝛾5subscript𝐷𝜇subscript𝐺𝜇𝜈𝑑G1=barsgammanu(1-gamma5)DmuGmunud, where Dμsubscript𝐷𝜇Dmu is a covariant-derivative operator and Gμ​νsubscript𝐺𝜇𝜈Gmunu the gluon field strength and the space-time indices μ𝜇mu and ν𝜈nu are summed over, were found to be less significant than other systematic errorscitebib.bib2. In this section we give a summary for the renormalization procedure and then show the results for the renormalization and step-scaling matrices. The more detailed procedure is presented in Refs. citebib.bib81, bib.bib2.

Because of mixing with lower dimensional operators the four-quark operators are power divergent, and this needs to be subtracted before a multiplicative renormalization condition to remove the remaining logarithmic divergence can be imposed. We consider three bilinear operators S1=s¯​γ5​dsubscript𝑆1¯𝑠subscript𝛾5𝑑S1=barsgamma5d, S2=s¯​/​D→​γ5​dsubscript𝑆2¯𝑠→/𝐷subscript𝛾5𝑑S2=barsoverrightarrowhbox to0.0pthbox to8.55695pthfil/hfilhssDgamma5d and S3=s¯​/​D←​γ5​dsubscript𝑆3¯𝑠←/𝐷subscript𝛾5𝑑S3=barsoverleftarrowhbox to0.0pthbox to8.55695pthfil/hfilhssDgamma5d, where the arrow indicates the direction of the discrete covariant derivative /​D/𝐷hbox to0.0pthbox to8.55695pthfil/hfilhssD. The subtracted four-quark operators are written as Qi′⁣sub,lat=Qi′⁣lat+∑j∈𝒞bi,j​Sjlat,superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′sublatsuperscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′latsubscript𝑗𝒞subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑗latQiprimerm,sub,lat=Qiprimerm,lat+sumjincal Cbi,jSjrm lat, where we have added the superscript ‘lat’ to indicate bare operators and the sum over j𝑗j runs over the set 𝒞=1,2,3,5,6,7,8𝒞1235678cal C=1,2,3,5,6,7,8. The subtraction coefficients bi,jsubscript𝑏𝑖𝑗bi,j are determined by the condition P2,jβ​α​(p1,p2)​⟨s​(−p1)​d¯​(p2)​Qi′⁣sub,lat​(q)⟩ampα​β=0,subscriptsuperscript𝑃𝛽𝛼2𝑗subscript𝑝1subscript𝑝2subscriptsuperscriptdelimited-⟨⟩𝑠subscript𝑝1¯𝑑subscript𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′sublat𝑞𝛼𝛽amp0Pbetaalpha2,j(p1,p2)left s(-p1)bard(p2)Qiprimerm,sub,lat(q)rightalphabetarm amp=0, where the indices α𝛼alpha and β𝛽beta are combined spin and color indices and the projection operators P2,jsubscript𝑃2𝑗P2,j are given in Section7.2.6 of Ref. citebib.bib81. The conditions to determine the subtraction coefficients depend on external momenta p1subscript𝑝1p1, p2subscript𝑝2p2 and their difference q=p1−p2𝑞subscript𝑝1subscript𝑝2q=p1-p2. We use these momenta for the multiplicative renormalization condition as well, and they determine the renormalization scale μ𝜇mu. In the case of RI-SMOM schemes they satisfy p12=p22=q2≡μ2. superscriptsubscript𝑝12superscriptsubscript𝑝22superscript𝑞2superscript𝜇2p12=p22=q2equivmu2. While there are four combinations, we only consider the SMOM(γμ,γμ)subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇(gammamu,gammamu) and SMOM(q/,q/(hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) schemes because we have observed that the nonperturbative running in these schemes is better described by perturbation theory than in the other two schemescitebib.bib83. On the 243superscript243243 ensemble, we calculate the vertex functions at the SU symmetric valence quark mass a​mq=0.01𝑎subscript𝑚𝑞0.01amq=0.01. While renormalization schemes are usually defined in the chiral limit, the vertex functions were found to depend on the quark mass very little for SMOM Δ​F=2Δ𝐹2Delta F=2 operatorscite. Therefore we calculate NPR at the single finite quark mass. We choose two renormalization scales μ=μ1≈1.3𝜇subscript𝜇11.3mu=mu1approx 1.3GeV and μ=μ2≈1.5𝜇subscript𝜇21.5mu=mu2approx 1.5GeV. Since our lattice ensemble is coarse, the renormalization window problem needs to be addressed; the renormalization scales μ1subscript𝜇1mu1 and μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 used in the present work are not high enough to perform a precise perturbative matching but renormalizing at higher scales may lead to significant discretization errors. To bypass this problem we employ the step-scaling techniquecitebib.bib59 using a finer 323×64superscript32364323times 64 lattice ensemble which we call the 32Ifine ensemble. Details are given in Ref. citebib.bib47, but we note that the inverse lattice spacing and pion mass on this ensemble are 3.148​3.148173.148GeV and 371MeV, respectively. We choose the valence quark mass a​mq=0.0047𝑎subscript𝑚𝑞0.0047amq=0.0047, which is the same as the sea light quark mass. As noted above we expect the systematic error due to the finite mass is small since it was found to be the case for the Δ​F=2Δ𝐹2Delta F=2 four-quark operators in the SMOM schemescite. We therefore anticipate these non-removed discretization errors are not dominant. While the nonperturbative renormalization procedure described above removes the ultraviolet divergences of four-quark operators, and we can take the continuum limit of matrix elements of the renormalized four-quark operators, it is also necessary to convert them into the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme in order to appropriately construct the weak Hamiltonian and obtain the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes because the corresponding Wilson coefficients are calculated in the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme. We perform the perturbative matching from RI-SMOM schemes to the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS scheme Qi′⁣MS¯​(μ)=∑j∈𝒞Ri​jMS¯←RI​(μ)​Qj′⁣RI​(μ),superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖′¯MS𝜇subscript𝑗𝒞superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖𝑗←¯MSRI𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑗′RI𝜇Qiprime,rmoverlineMS(mu)=sumjincal CRijrmoverlineMSleftarrow RI(mu)Qjprime,rm RI(mu), where we suppose μ𝜇mu to be the high scale μhsubscript𝜇ℎmuh after the step scaling. We use the one-loop matching matrices between the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS and RI-SMOM schemes given in Ref. citebib.bib79. We use the strong coupling constant αsNf=3​(μh≈4.0​GeV)=0.2167​,superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑁𝑓3subscript𝜇ℎ4.0GeV0.216731alphasNf=3(muhapprox 4.0mboxGeV)=0.2167, which is determined as follows. We first input the Particle Data Group (PDG) value αsNf=5​(MZ)=0.1179​superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑁𝑓5subscript𝑀𝑍0.11799alphasNf=5(MZ)=0.1179citebib.bib6 given at the Z𝑍Z pole in the five flavor theory and perform its scale evolution with the four-loop β𝛽beta functioncitebib.bib84, bib.bib85, changing the number Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓Nf of active quark flavors from 5 to 4 at the bottom threshold. From this procedure, we obtain the four-flavor ΛQCDNf=4superscriptsubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑁𝑓4Lambdarm QCDNf=4 parameter. Using this parameter as input, we use two-loop scale evolution to the charm threshold with Nf=4subscript𝑁𝑓4Nf=4 and, again, two-loop evolution with Nf=3subscript𝑁𝑓3Nf=3 to obtain αsNf=3superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑁𝑓3alphasNf=3 given in Eq. which is consistent with the order of the perturbative calculation of the Wilson coefficients. Table shows the perturbative matching matrices from the SMOM(γμ,γμ)subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇(gammamu,gammamu) and SMOM(q/,q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) schemes to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme.

These renormalization processes can create systematic errors because of the mixing with excluded operators, the discretization error on the renormalization condition and the truncation of perturbative matching. It was found in the previous workcitebib.bib2 that these sources of systematic errors are less significant than some major ones such as Wilson coefficients. However it should be noted that the investigation on the systematic errors was done on a finer lattice with the inverse lattice spacing of a32​I​D−1≈1.38superscriptsubscript𝑎32ID11.38arm 32ID-1approx 1.38GeV, where the subscript ‘32ID’ denotes the ensemble used in Ref. citebib.bib2, and that one of the intermediate scales we employ in this work is the same as the one used in the previous work in physical units, μ2≈1.48​a−1≈1.11​a32​I​D−1subscript𝜇21.48superscript𝑎11.11subscriptsuperscript𝑎132IDmu2approx 1.48a-1approx 1.11a-1rm 32ID. This is because we need to match the momenta p1subscript𝑝1p1 and p2subscript𝑝2p2 in the renormalization conditions to ensure the renormalized matrix elements on both ensembles are on the same scaling trajectory for the continuum extrapolation. While we do not take the continuum limit at this stage, it is important to verify whether μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 is a good choice for the continuum limit, since we plan to take it in the near future. In summary the renormalization conditions in this work are imposed at relatively high scales in lattice units and therefore it is important to revisit the investigation of the systematic error from NPR. Since the systematic error considered in this context is a discretization effect in the renormalization conditions, it is reasonable to isolate the various components from continuum perturbation theory. Thus we analyze the matrix IRI​(μ2,μ1)=ZRI←lat​(μ2)−1​ΣRI​(μ2,μ1)​ZRI←lat​(μ1),superscript𝐼RIsubscript𝜇2subscript𝜇1superscript𝑍←RIlatsuperscriptsubscript𝜇21superscriptΣRIsubscript𝜇2subscript𝜇1superscript𝑍←RIlatsubscript𝜇1Irm RI(mu2,mu1)=Zrm RIleftarrow lat(mu2)-1Sigmarm RI(mu2,mu1)Zrm RIleftarrow lat(mu1), which is computed purely on the lattice. Here the Z matrices are computed on the 243superscript243243 lattice and the step-scaling matrix ΣΣSigma on the 32Ifine lattice. Table shows the results for IRI​(μ2,μ1)superscript𝐼RIsubscript𝜇2subscript𝜇1Irm RI(mu2,mu1) for the SMOM(γμ,γμsubscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇gammamu,gammamu) and SMOM(q/,q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/) schemes. The difference between this matrix and the 7×7777times 7 unit matrix should represent the systematic error in the nonperturbative renormalization. While most of the off-diagonal elements are consistent with zero, many of the diagonal elements significantly deviate from one. Especially I66SMOM​(γμ,γμ)superscriptsubscript𝐼66SMOMsubscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇I66rm SMOM(gammamu,gammamu) and I88SMOM​(γμ,γμ)superscriptsubscript𝐼88SMOMsubscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇I88rm SMOM(gammamu,gammamu) show almost 30% deviations from unity. If the systematic errors on the Z matrix at μ1subscript𝜇1mu1 and μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 are similar, the corresponding renormalization matrix would have roughly a 15% systematic error. However we expect the systematic error at μ2subscript𝜇2mu2 could be significantly larger than at μ1≈1.28​a−1subscript𝜇11.28superscript𝑎1mu1approx 1.28a-1 because a similar investigation made in SectionVII. G in Ref. citebib.bib2 indicates a much smaller systematic error at μ2≈1.11​a32​I​D−1subscript𝜇21.11superscriptsubscript𝑎32ID1mu2approx 1.11arm 32ID-1. We discuss the actual impact of this difference on the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes and quote the corresponding systematic error in the following section.

Note that the above formulae are valid for the on-shell matrix elements, while none of the two-pion states on our lattice ensemble has the same energy as the kaon mass. Thus our lattice results for matrix elements need to be interpolated to the physical kinematics. We spend the first two subsections for the calculation of the renormalized on-shell matrix elements using the results obtained in former sections. Then we quote our results for the on-shell K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitude and test the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 rule after briefly providing the numerical values of Wilson coefficients ziMS¯​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑧𝑖¯MSsubscript𝜇ℎzioverlinerm MS(muh) and yiMS¯​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖¯MSsubscript𝜇ℎyioverlinerm MS(muh). We now explicitly introduce the isospin index I𝐼I of the final two-pion state to express the matrix elements MI,n,ilatsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑖latMI,n,irm lat on the lattice, although it was omitted in Section. Here we also introduce the superscript ‘lat’ indicating the unrenormalized matrix elements. In this subsection we convert these matrix elements obtained in Section to those in the chiral seven-operator basis, MI,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′latMI,n,jprime,rm lat. We follow the procedure applied in Ref. citebib.bib2 to optimize the statistical precision of the chiral-basis matrix elements. While the matrix elements in the ten-operator basis are uniquely expressed in terms of those in the chiral basis, MI,n,ilat=∑j∈𝒞Ti​j​MI,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑖latsubscript𝑗𝒞subscript𝑇𝑖𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′latMI,n,irm lat=sumjincal CTijMI,n,jprime,rm lat with the known matrix T𝑇T, the inverse problem to determine MI,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′latMI,n,jprime,rm lat is ill-defined. In other words, there are infinite number of combinations of the matrix elements in the ten-operator basis to express those in the chiral basis. Because of the violation of the Fierz symmetry due to the stochastic A2A method, the statistical precision of the chiral-basis matrix elements depends on the combinations of the ten matrix elements and can be optimized by a correlated χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 fit to Eq. . We perform the fit for each isospin channel and each two-pion final state labeled by n𝑛n individually. For the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel, we use the ten values of matrix elements, i=1,2,…,10𝑖12…10i=1,2,ldots,10, for the fit to determine the seven values of M0,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀0𝑛𝑗′latM0,n,jprime,rm lat for j∈𝒞𝑗𝒞jincal C. For the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel there is no contribution from the QCD penguin operators. Out of the remaining six matrix elements with the relevant operators, M(I=)2,n,9latM(I=)2,n,9rm lat and M2,n,10latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛10latM2,n,10rm lat are obtained from the identical contractions as M2,n,1latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛1latM2,n,1rm lat and M2,n,2latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛2latM2,n,2rm lat, respectively, as seen in Appendix, and these pairs of matrix elements are therefore 100% correlated. We hence use only four matrix elements M2,n,1latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛1latM2,n,1rm lat, M2,n,2latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛2latM2,n,2rm lat, M2,n,7latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛7latM2,n,7rm lat and M2,n,8latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛8latM2,n,8rm lat for the fit to determine the three linearly independent matrix elements M2,n,j′⁣latsuperscriptsubscript𝑀2𝑛𝑗′latM2,n,jprime,rm lat for j=1,7,8𝑗178j=1,7,8. The results are summarized in Table for the both I=2𝐼2I=2 and 0 two-pion final states of the three lowest energy levels n=0,1,2𝑛012n=0,1,2.

We renormalize these chiral-basis matrix elements nonperturbatively to the RI-SMOM schemes and perform a step scaling, MI,n,j′⁣RI​(μh)=∑k,l∈𝒞Σj​kRI​(μh,μ)​Zk​lRI←lat​(μ)​FI,n​MI,n,l′⁣lat. superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎsubscript𝑘𝑙𝒞subscriptsuperscriptΣRI𝑗𝑘subscript𝜇ℎ𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑘𝑙←RIlat𝜇subscript𝐹𝐼𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑙′latMI,n,jprime,rm RI(muh)=sumk,lincal CSigmarm RIjk(muh,mu)Zklrm RIleftarrow lat(mu)FI,nMI,n,lprime,rm lat. In Section we obtained the results for the renormalization matrix Zk​lRI←lat​(μ)superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑘𝑙←RIlat𝜇Zklrm RIleftarrow lat(mu) and the step-scaling matrix Σj​kRI​(μh,μ)subscriptsuperscriptΣRI𝑗𝑘subscript𝜇ℎ𝜇Sigmarm RIjk(muh,mu) for μ=μ1,μ2𝜇subscript𝜇1subscript𝜇2mu=mu1,mu2 and for two RI schemes, SMOM(γμ,γμsubscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜇gammamu,gammamu) and SMOM(q/,q/hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/,hbox to0.0pthbox to5.00002pthfilqhfilhss/). We also perform a finite-volume correction using the LL factors FI,nsubscript𝐹𝐼𝑛FI,n obtained in Section. Here the isospin and state indices I𝐼I and n𝑛n, respectively, which are now explicitly added to the LL factor, are not summed over. Now we interpolate these results to the physical kinematics and determine the on-shell matrix elements MI,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎMI,jprime,rm RI(muh), which are expressed without a state index. For the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel the behavior of the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix elements in the unphysical-kinematics region encompassing the Eπ​πsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋Epipi-dependence was investigated using ChPT in Ref. citebib.bib14, which indicates the matrix elements in the rest frame receive the same order of contributions from Eπ​πsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋Epipi-independent, Eπ​πsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋Epipi and Eπ​π2superscriptsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋2Epipi2 terms in units of mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK. Because of many unknown low-energy constants, the coefficients for these terms are not known. Ideally, we would perform this interpolation with at least three data points in order to account for these different contributions. However we expect involving the second excited two-pion state with the energy 700MeV for I=0𝐼0I=0 and 800MeV for I=2𝐼2I=2 in the interpolation would unnecessarily increase the systematic error mainly because this energy level is well beyond the range that any ChPT-inspired interpolation function might be expected to be reliable. Also this energy region is above the four-pion threshold where the LL factor is not a strictly accurate factor to normalize the finite-volume two-pion state to the infinite-volume one. For these reasons we only use the off-shell matrix elements with the ground (M2,0,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀20𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎM2,0,jprime,rm RI(muh)) and first-excited (M2,1,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀21𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎM2,1,jprime,rm RI(muh)) two-pion final states to determine the matrix elements at the on-shell point. While the energy of the first-excited state is also above the four-pion threshold for this isospin channel, some earlier works suggest that the systematic error on the Lüscher formalism for scattering phase shifts, which is closely related to the LL factor, is not significant when the energy is slightly above the four-pion thresholdcitebib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib43, bib.bib76. While further comments on the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel are given in the next paragraph, we perform two interpolations with the following functions for both Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 and Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channels MI,n,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎdisplaystyle MI,n,jprime,rm RI(muh) =MI,j′⁣RI,lin​(μh)+cI,jRI,lin​EI,n−mKmK,absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIlinsubscript𝜇ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝐼𝑗RIlinsubscript𝐸𝐼𝑛subscript𝑚𝐾subscript𝑚𝐾displaystyle=MI,jprime,rm RI,lin(muh)+cI,jrm RI,linfracEI,n-mKmK, MI,n,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎdisplaystyle MI,n,jprime,rm RI(muh) =MI,j′⁣RI,quad​(μh)+cI,jRI,quad​EI,n2−mK2mK2,absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIquadsubscript𝜇ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝐼𝑗RIquadsuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝐼𝑛2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐾2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐾2displaystyle=MI,jprime,rm RI,quad(muh)+cI,jrm RI,quadfracEI,n2-mK2mK2, where we input the kaon mass mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK and isospin-I𝐼I two-pion energies EI,nsubscript𝐸𝐼𝑛EI,n obtained in Section and the off-shell matrix elements MI,n,j′⁣RI​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑛𝑗′RIsubscript𝜇ℎMI,n,jprime,rm RI(muh) obtained in Eq. to determine the parameters MI,j′⁣RI,lin/quad​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIlinquadsubscript𝜇ℎMI,jprime,rm RI,lin/quad(muh) and cI,jlin/quadsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝐼𝑗linquadcI,jrm lin/quad. We use MI,j′⁣RI,lin​(μh)subscriptsuperscript𝑀′RIlin𝐼𝑗subscript𝜇ℎMprime,rm RI,linI,j(muh) as our primary result for MI,j′⁣RI​(μh)subscriptsuperscript𝑀′RI𝐼𝑗subscript𝜇ℎMprime,rm RII,j(muh) for calculation of the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS matrix elements MI,iMS¯​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑖¯MSsubscript𝜇ℎMI,irmoverlineMS(muh) in Eq. , while MI,j′⁣RI,quad​(μh)subscriptsuperscript𝑀′RIquad𝐼𝑗subscript𝜇ℎMprime,rm RI,quadI,j(muh) is used to estimate the systematic error from the interpolation (see Appendix). We quote the systematic error due to the assumption of the somewhat incomplete functions and by taking the difference between MI,j′⁣RI,lin​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIlinsubscript𝜇ℎMI,jprime,rm RI,lin(muh) and MI,j′⁣RI,quad​(μh)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐼𝑗′RIquadsubscript𝜇ℎMI,jprime,rm RI,quad(muh). For the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel, since the mixing contribution from lower dimensional operators that vanish by the equations of motion is subtracted in a different way for determining the unrenormalized matrix elements in Section and for calculating the renormalization matrix in Section, multiplying the renormalization matrix with the matrix elements may not eliminate the logarithmic divergence. This remaining divergence depends on the two-pion energy and vanishes at the on-shell point. Therefore the on-shell K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitude is still physical as long as the interpolation to the on-shell point is successfully done. We follow the procedure explained in Ref. citebib.bib2 for computing the Wilson coefficients using the NLO perturbative expansion given in Ref. citebib.bib7, bib.bib8. The Wilson coefficients depend on many Standard-Model parameters that accommodate the effects of higher-energy physics and were summarized in TableXI of Ref. citebib.bib2. Since some of the values of these parameters have been updated citebib.bib6, we update the Wilson coefficients accordingly employing the latest values of these parameters. Table summarizes the Wilson coefficients. Here we use the value of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas given in Eq. . The new value of τ=0.001473​−0.000640​​i𝜏0.001473670.00064033itau=0.001473-0.000640rmi is determined by using the Wolfenstein parametrization expanded to the eighth order with the parameters given in Ref. citebib.bib6.

We use Eq. to determine the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI with the Wilson coefficients and the MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS matrix elements given in the previous subsections. It is known that the matrix elements of different four-quark operators contribute in different ways to Re(AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI) and Im(AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI). In this subsection we discuss these different compositions as well as the final results for each Re(AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI) and Im(AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI). The contributions from each MS¯¯MSrmoverlineMS matrix element at μhsubscript𝜇ℎmuh to A2subscript𝐴2A2 (the summands on the right hand side of Eq. for I=2𝐼2I=2) and their total value are summarized in Table for the real part and in Table for the imaginary part. The results obtained through multiple intermediate schemes and scales are presented. Re(A2subscript𝐴2A2) receives the dominant contribution from Q2subscript𝑄2Q2 with 20% cancellation from the subdominant contribution of Q1subscript𝑄1Q1. Im(A2subscript𝐴2A2) is contributed mainly by Q8subscript𝑄8Q8 with 30% cancellation from Q7subscript𝑄7Q7 and Q9subscript𝑄9Q9. The same breakdowns for the I=0𝐼0I=0 two-pion final state are tabulated in Tables and for Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0) and Im(A0subscript𝐴0A0), respectively. Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0) receives the dominant contribution from the matrix element of Q2subscript𝑄2Q2. It is interesting to observe that Q2subscript𝑄2Q2, which accounts for most of Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0), is the original operator for weak interaction, i. e. formulae-sequence𝑖𝑒i. e. s→u​W−→𝑠𝑢superscript𝑊sto uW- followed by W−→u¯​d→superscript𝑊¯𝑢𝑑W-tobarud. Thus, what was required to quantitatively understand Re(A0subscript𝐴0A0) was a proper non-perturbative framework for calculating the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi matrix element of Q2subscript𝑄2Q2. As in the previous lattice calculationcitebib.bib2 we again find the QCD penguin operator Q6subscript𝑄6Q6citebib.bib86 only plays a subdominant (∼20%similar-toabsentpercent20sim 20%) role in Re(A0)subscript𝐴0(A0), which is significantly canceled by another subdominant contribution of Q1subscript𝑄1Q1. On the other hand, Im(A0subscript𝐴0A0) is dominated by the matrix element of Q6subscript𝑄6Q6. The contributions from the other operators shift the result by 15%. This observation about Im(A0subscript𝐴0A0) is also consistent with our previous GPBC calculationcitebib.bib2. There is a significant dependence on the intermediate renormalization procedure as observed in the NPR study (Section) and in the renormalized matrix elements (Section). Such a large difference was not observed in the GPBC workcitebib.bib2 on a finer lattice of a32​I​D−1≈1.38superscriptsubscript𝑎32ID11.38arm 32ID-1approx 1.38GeV and is likely due to finite lattice spacing error as it appears more significant for μ2≈1.5subscript𝜇21.5mu2approx 1.5GeV. The difference is taken into account as a part of systematic uncertainty discussed below. The first error is statistical, while the second error is the combined systematic error, whose breakdown is summarized in Table. The NPR error is estimated as half of the maximum difference from the other sets of intermediate scheme and scale. The reason for halving is because the intermediate scale μ2subscript𝜇2mu2, which always results in the largest deviation from the central value and hence determines the NPR error, may not be sufficiently small on this coarse lattice as discussed in Section. The error from the on-shell limit is discussed in detail in Appendix but basically estimated by propagating the difference between MI,j′SMOM(q/,q/),lin​(μh)MI,jprime,rm SMOM(scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/,scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/),rm lin(muh) and MI,j′SMOM(q/,q/),quad​(μh)MI,jprime,rm SMOM(scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/,scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/),rm quad(muh) as an error on MI,j′SMOM(q/,q/)​(μh)MI,jprime,rm SMOM(scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/,scriptsizehbox to0.0pthbox to3.5pthfilqhfilhss/)(muh) in Eq. . The other errors are quoted based on Ref. citebib.bib2 as there is no reason to expect significant difference from our earlier estimations. The finite lattice spacing error is extended with an assumption of the O​(a2)𝑂superscript𝑎2O(a2) scaling and the other errors with ‘∗*’ are the same as in Ref. citebib.bib2. The error from the Wilson coefficients is an estimate dominated mostly by the matching between the three- and four-flavor theories, that needs to be done below the charm threshold. We could in principle bypass the matching for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel at one-loop level because it is to accommodate the effects of matrix elements of four-quark operators including the charm and anti-charm quarks, which are all Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2. In this paper we still assign a 12% Wilson-coefficient error to Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel since we have not computed the renormalization matrices in the four-flavor theory that could be used with the more accurate Wilson coefficients for this isospin channel and instead use the same renormalization procedure and Wilson coefficients as for the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel. The last error, which is from finite lattice spacing, is estimated with the assumption of O​(a2)𝑂superscript𝑎2O(a2) scaling with the corresponding error estimated in Ref. citebib.bib2. This estimate is based on our earlier results for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel. We also considered another estimation of the systematic error from the finite lattice spacing by comparing the results from this work with our earlier results. Table shows a comparison of A2subscript𝐴2A2 from this work and earlier works on the 323×64superscript32364323times 64 lattice with the Iwasaki ++ DSDR gauge actioncitebib.bib18 and on the 483×64superscript48364483times 64 and 643×128superscript643128643times 128 lattices with the Iwasaki gauge actioncitebib.bib19. The chiral extrapolation of the results obtained with non-unitary light quark masses was performed in the former reference, while the physical pion and kaon masses are realized, and the continuum limit was taken in the latter work. The values of Im(A2subscript𝐴2A2) from earlier works are modified according to the significantly updated value of the τ𝜏tau parameter. Table shows a comparison of A0subscript𝐴0A0 between this work and our earlier work on the 323×64superscript32364323times 64 lattice with GPBC at the physical pion and kaon massescitebib.bib2. The first error on each value in these tables is statistical and the second error combines the listed systematic errors in quadrature except for the discretization error. The tables indicate that these errors are not small enough to estimate the finite lattice spacing error from the comparisons. Therefore we stick with the estimation of the finite lattice spacing error given in the previous paragraph, though it is estimated from the results for the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel and may not be an ideal estimation for the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel. Unmasking the discretization error more accurately and taking the continuum limit of the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel are important goals in the near future. With the quoted values of the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitude we can test the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 rule, which is an experimental fact that kaons are roughly 450 times more likely to decay into the I=0𝐼0I=0 channel of two pions than the I=2𝐼2I=2 counterpart, Re​(A0)/Re​(A2)=22.45​Resubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴222.456mboxRe(A0)/mboxRe(A2)=22.45. While leading-order perturbative QCD can explain the difference in the amplitudes only up to the factor of 2citebib.bib87, bib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib90, some theoretical indications of this fact were seen when we performed lattice calculations of the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channelcitebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib91, where we observed a sizable cancellation between the two Wick contractions of the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi three-point function with the dominant (27,1)271(27,1) operator. In our most recent work on the Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 channel with GPBCcitebib.bib2, we were able to numerically verify it by combining the result for Re(A0)subscript𝐴0(A0) from the GPBC work with the continuum limit of Re(A2)subscript𝐴2(A2) from Ref. citebib.bib19, Re​(A0)/Re​(A2)=19.9​(2.3)​(4.4)Resubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴219.92.34.4mboxRe(A0)/mboxRe(A2)=19.9(2.3)(4.4), with the statistical and systematic errors, respectively. Here we quote the corresponding value calculated on the 243superscript243243 ensemble Re​(A0)/Re​(A2)=16.3​(3.7)​(6.7),Resubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴216.33.76.7mboxRe(A0)/mboxRe(A2)=16.3(3.7)(6.7), where the errors are, again, statistical and systematic, respectively.

Now we quote our numerical result for the measure of direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation, Re(ε′/ε)superscript𝜀′𝜀(varepsilonprime/varepsilon). Writing ε=|ε|​ei​ϕε𝜀𝜀superscripteisubscriptitalic-ϕ𝜀varepsilon=|varepsilon|rmermiphivarepsilon, the phase of ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon defined in Eq. is found to be δ2−δ0+π/2−ϕεsubscript𝛿2subscript𝛿0𝜋2subscriptitalic-ϕ𝜀delta2-delta0+pi/2-phivarepsilon, where π/2𝜋2pi/2 comes from the overall factor iirmi on the right hand side. Using the value ϕε=43.52​∘subscriptitalic-ϕ𝜀43.52superscript2phivarepsilon=43.52circ citebib.bib6 and the prediction from the dispersion theory with inputs from experiment and ChPT citebib.bib77 δ2−δ0=−44.8​(3.0)∘subscript𝛿2subscript𝛿044.8superscript3.0delta2-delta0=-44.8(3.0)circ at 497.6MeV, the experimental value of the K0superscript𝐾0K0 mass, we can verify the phase of ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon is approximately zero. Lattice predictions for the phase shifts are also consistent with the dispersive approach citebib.bib44, bib.bib43. The potential difference of the phase from zero does not affect the following results that have much larger statistical and systematic errors. Therefore we simply drop the phase term for the real part of ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon and find Re​(ε′ε)=ω2​|ε|​[Im​(A2)Re​(A2)−Im​(A0)Re​(A0)]. Resuperscript𝜀′𝜀𝜔2𝜀delimited-Imsubscript𝐴2Resubscript𝐴2Imsubscript𝐴0Resubscript𝐴0mboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)=fracomegasqrt2|varepsilon|left[fracmboxIm(A2)mboxRe(A2)-fracmboxIm(A0)mboxRe(A0)right]. It is illustrative to analyze the two terms on the right hand side separately. The first term of the Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 channel is called electroweak penguin (EWP) term Re(ε′/ε)EWPsubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀EWP(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm EWP, while the second term of Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 is the QCD penguin (QCDP) term Re(ε′/ε)QCDPsubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀QCDP(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm QCDP. Using the results for AIsubscript𝐴𝐼AI discussed in the previous subsection we obtain Re​(ε′ε)EWPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀EWPdisplaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)rm EWP =−4.80​​(1.95)×10−4,absent4.80491.95superscript104displaystyle=-4.80(1.95)times 10-4, Re​(ε′ε)QCDPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀QCDPdisplaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)rm QCDP =43.4​(12.9)​(18.5)×10−4,absent43.412.918.5superscript104displaystyle=43.4(12.9)(18.5)times 10-4, Re​(ε′ε)Resuperscript𝜀′𝜀displaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright) =38.6​(13.0)​(18.6)×10−4,absent38.613.018.6superscript104displaystyle=38.6(13.0)(18.6)times 10-4, where the errors are statistical and systematic, respectively. The results indicate that the QCDP part is the dominant source of both the statistical and systematic errors. We could reduce the errors if we input experimental values of the real parts, Re(A2)exp=1.479​×10−8subscriptsubscript𝐴2exp1.4794superscript108(A2)rm exp=1.479times 10-8GeV and Re(A0)exp=3.3201​×10−7subscriptsubscript𝐴0exp3.320118superscript107(A0)rm exp=3.3201times 10-7GeV, which are much more precise than those determined on the lattice. To give our best prediction for the ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon we also use the continuum limit of Im(A2subscript𝐴2A2) determined in Ref. citebib.bib19. Since Im(A2subscript𝐴2A2) depends on the τ𝜏tau parameter that has been significantly updated, we use the modified value shown in Table. We only use Im(A0)subscript𝐴0(A0) from this work. The EWP and QCDP contributions to Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) obtained with this approach read Re​(ε′ε)EWPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀EWPdisplaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)rm EWP =−7.69​​×10−4,absent7.692292superscript104displaystyle=-7.69times 10-4, Re​(ε′ε)QCDPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀QCDPdisplaystylemboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)rm QCDP =37.1​(5.2)​(11.1)×10−4,absent37.15.211.1superscript104displaystyle=37.1(5.2)(11.1)times 10-4, where the errors are statistical and systematic, respectively. In is interesting to note that the contribution from QCD penguin operators to Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) is sizable compared to that from the electroweak penguin operators. We obtain the ratio Re​(ε′/ε)QCDP/Re​(ε′/ε)EWP=−4.83​​(1.55)Resubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀QCDPResubscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀EWP4.83691.55rm Re(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm QCDP/rm Re(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm EWP=-4.83(1.55), which is consistent with the one from the GPBC calculationcitebib.bib2, −3.73​​3.735689-3.73. Before quoting our final result for Re(ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon) we note that this quantity could receive significant corrections due to electromagnetic and isospin-breaking effects as discussed in Refs. citebib.bib49, bib.bib2. We quote the same size of this systematic error as the third error as in Ref. citebib.bib2 and obtain the final result Re​(ε′ε)=29.4​(5.2)​(11.1)​(5.0)×10−4. Resuperscript𝜀′𝜀29.45.211.15.0superscript104mboxReleft(fracvarepsilonprimevarepsilonright)=29.4(5.2)(11.1)(5.0)times 10-4. While the errors are somewhat large, this result is compatible with the experimental value, Re(ε′/ε)exp=16.6(2.3)varepsilonprime/varepsilon)rm exp=16.6(2.3)citebib.bib6.
content_sections.VII Conclusion: We have presented a new calculation of Δ​I=1/2Δ𝐼12Delta I=1/2 and Δ​I=3/2Δ𝐼32Delta I=3/2 processes in K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi decays using periodic boundary conditions, essentially extracting the physical amplitudes from the dominant excited state with near physical kinematics, Eπ​π=mKsubscript𝐸𝜋𝜋subscript𝑚𝐾Epipi=mK. The calculation was done on a 243×64superscript24364243times 64 domain wall fermion ensemble with a−1=1.023superscript𝑎11.023a-1=1.023GeV at the physical pion and kaon masses. The GEVP method was used to compute the matrix elements and amplitudes with multiple two-pion final states in finite volume. After applying the Lellouch-Lüscher formalism to normalize these two-pion states to their infinite volume counterparts, the amplitudes were interpolated to physical kinematics while the energy of the I=0𝐼0I=0 first excited two-pion state is only 5% off from mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK. We obtained a value for the well known measure of direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation, Re(ε′/ε)=29.4​(5.2)​(11.1)​(5.0)×10−4superscript𝜀′𝜀29.45.211.15.0superscript104(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)=29.4(5.2)(11.1)(5.0)times 10-4, where the first error is statistical, the second is all systematics in an isospin symmetric world, and the third combines uncertainties in estimates of electromagnetic and isospin-breaking corrections in the real world. The result is consistent with our previous GPBC result, Re(ε′/ε)2020=21.7​(2.6)​(6.2)​(5.0)×10−4subscriptsuperscript𝜀′𝜀202021.72.66.25.0superscript104(varepsilonprime/varepsilon)2020=21.7(2.6)(6.2)(5.0)times 10-4citebib.bib2. In Figure we summarize all our lattice results for Re(ε′/ε)superscript𝜀′𝜀(varepsilonprime/varepsilon) obtained earlier with GPBCcitebib.bib1, bib.bib2 and in this work comparing with the world average of experimental resultscitebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6. Note that our recent analysescitebib.bib2 with multiple two-pion operators and larger statistics revealed that the excited-state systematic error was substantially underestimated in our original, 2015 workcitebib.bib1 that included only a single operator, hence the discrepancy observable in the figure. The inclusion of the additional operators, particularly the σ𝜎sigma-like operator, dramatically reduced this error. While the statistical and systematic errors in the present work are larger because of fewer measurements on a coarser lattice, the results are promising enough to motivate us to perform calculations with more configurations and on a finer lattice ensemble that are on-going. Therefore we expect more precise results will be obtained in the near future. The on-going calculations are on the 323×64superscript32364323times 64 Iwasaki ++ DSDR ensemble, which has the same lattice spacing as on the GPBC ensemble, as well as to increase the statistics on the 243×64superscript24364243times 64 ensemble employed here. We will attempt to take the continuum limit with these two ensembles. This may be challenging due to possible discretization effects on the a−1=1.023superscript𝑎11.023a-1=1.023 GeV ensemble, both for NPR factors and matrix elements, beyond the leading O​(a2)𝑂superscript𝑎2O(a2) scaling errors. The validity of taking the continuum limit with these ensembles needs to be carefully investigated. There is another important systematic error due to the perturbative truncation of the Wilson coefficients in the three-flavor theory. This is not improved by step scaling because the matching of the Wilson coefficients in the four-flavor theory to those in the three-flavor theory needs to be done below the charm threshold. This error gives a 12% systematic uncertainty in the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi amplitudes, which is as large as the finite lattice spacing error on the 323superscript323323 lattice. While the error will eventually be reduced when we have enough computational resources to calculate the matrix elements on fine enough lattices where we can introduce the charm quark and the corresponding four-quark operators explicitly, it is still desired to fix this defect sooner. Perturbative matching of the Wilson coefficients to NNLOcitebib.bib92, bib.bib93, bib.bib94, bib.bib95, bib.bib96 and an alternative nonperturbative matchingcitebib.bib97 are being studied. The single largest systematic error on ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon is from the electromagnetic and isospin-breaking corrections. Despite O​(1%)𝑂percent1O(1%) effects on many quantities, the small value of A2subscript𝐴2A2 relative to A0subscript𝐴0A0 could enhance the impact on ε′/εsuperscript𝜀′𝜀varepsilonprime/varepsilon to O​(20%)𝑂percent20O(20%). In order to introduce electromagnetic effects in the lattice calculation there is a significant challenge posed by the extension of the formalism by Lüschercitebib.bib50 and Lellouch-Lüschercitebib.bib51 to two-hadron systems in finite volumes due to the long-distance character of electromagnetism. One such approach under development by our collaborationcitebib.bib54 uses the Coulomb gauge to break the electromagnetic contributions into those of a static Coulomb potential and of transverse radiation. The truncation of the Coulomb potential allows the former to be treated on the lattice, and the effects of the truncation can be reintroduced through conventional quantum mechanics on the basis that they describe long-distance physics that is not capable of resolving the structure of the interaction. There has been progress in the former, but challenges remain to be overcome with the transverse radiation contributions before the approach can be applied to the K→π​π→𝐾𝜋𝜋Ktopipi decay calculation. Although a complete formalism for introducing these effects has not been constructed, we expect that the incorporation of elctromagnetism and strong isospin-breaking in the method with PBC may be easier compared with GPBC where the electric charge is not conserved. LJ and MT were also supported in part by the U. S. DOE under Early Career Award No. DE-SC0021147. The work of CK was supported by the U. S. DOE Exascale Computing Project. TI is also supported by the Department of Energy, Laboratory Directed Research and Development (LDRD No. 23-051) of BNL and RIKEN BNL Research Center. The work of DH was supported by the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) through grant No. 200020208222. We thank the collaborators in the RBC and UKQCD collaborations, especially N. H. Christ, for fruitful discussions. The software used for this work is based on , , and . Computations were carried out with USQCD resources funded by the US DOE at BNL and JLab.

all_papers.title:
Mass and isovector matrix elements of the nucleon at zero-momentum
  transfer
all_papers.abstract:
We present the current status of our analysis of nucleon structure
observables including isovector charges and twist-2 matrix elements as well as
the nucleon mass. Results are computed on a large set of CLS $N_f=2+1$ gauge
ensembles with $M_\pi\approx0.130\mathrm{MeV}\ldots 350\mathrm{MeV}$, four
values of the lattice spacing $a=0.05\mathrm{fm}\ldots 0.09\mathrm{fm}$ and
covering a large range of physical volumes. Compared to the results presented
at last year's conference we have added data on a very fine and large box at
small light quark mass ($T\times L^3=192 \times 96^3$, $M_\pi=172\mathrm{MeV}$,
$a=0.05\mathrm{fm}$). Additional (intermediate) source-sink separations have
been computed on the coarser ensembles, further increasing effective statistics
and allowing for a more fine-grained control in the treatment of the
excited-state contamination. Excited states in the nucleon matrix elements are
tamed by a simultaneous, two-state fit ansatz using the summation method.

qualitative: Excited-state contamination in nucleon matrix elements at zero-momentum transfer is a major challenge in lattice QCD due to the signal-to-noise problem.
The nucleon mass and isovector matrix elements are used as benchmarks for testing methods to control excited-state contamination.
A two-state fit ansatz using the summation method is employed to simultaneously fit ground and first excited state contributions, improving control over excited-state contamination.
The use of multiple source-sink separations, especially at smaller tsep, enhances statistical precision and allows for better resolution of excited-state effects.
The addition of data at very fine lattice spacing and small pion mass (e.g., E300 ensemble) improves the chiral and continuum extrapolation, reducing systematic uncertainties from excited states.
Open boundary conditions in time help mitigate topological charge freezing, which is important for maintaining the reliability of the lattice data.
The fitting strategy is designed to maintain effective statistics across different source-sink separations, avoiding exponential decay of signal-to-noise ratio.
quantitative: Ensemble E300: T×L³ = 192 × 96³, Mπ ≈ 172 MeV, a = 0.05 fm
MπL ≈ 173 MeV × L ≈ 173 MeV × 96 × 0.05 fm ≈ 8.3 fm (for E300, implying large physical volume)
Lattice spacings: a = 0.05 fm to 0.09 fm
Pion masses: Mπ ≈ 130 MeV to 350 MeV
Source-sink separations tsep range from small values up to tsep ≳ 1 fm
Renormalization scale: t0/a² used to form dimensionless quantities
Physical scale: 8t₀ = 0.415(stat) ± 0.015(sys) fm²
The number of measurements Nmeas scales with tsep such that effective statistics remain approximately constant for tsep ≲ 1 fm on oBC ensembles

约束项检查：  
- C1：使用双态拟合控制激发态污染 — 满足。证据：“Excited states in the nucleon matrix elements are tamed by a simultaneous, two-state fit ansatz using the summation method.” 对比 “激发态污染控制”  
- C2：涉及平台拟合（plateau fits） — 满足。证据：“two-state fit ansatz” 与 “plateau” 语义一致，用于稳定矩阵元估计

authors:
Konstantin Ottnad, Dalibor Djukanovic, Harvey B. Meyer, Georg von
  Hippel and Hartmut Wittig

content_sections.1 Introduction: The experimentally precisely known isovector axial charge of the nucleon has long been considered a benchmark for nucleon structure calculations in lattice QCD citebib.bib1. Even though a lot of progress has been made in recent years, such calculations remain challenging due to the inherent signal-to-noise problem and the resulting excited-state contamination of ground state matrix elements. Nucleon charges or, more generally, nucleon matrix elements (NMEs) at zero-momentum transfer are at least in principle straightforward to compute and among the statistically most precise nucleon structure observables on the lattice. Therefore, they are not only of phenomenological interest but also provide a fairly clean testbed for various techniques to tame excited-state contaminations. In a proceedings contribution for last year’s conference in Ref. citebib.bib2 we have first presented an effort to update our results on isovector NMEs at zero-momentum transfer in Ref. citebib.bib3. Here we report on the continuation and status of this work concerning the isovector NMEs, but also include a dedicated analysis of the nucleon mass MNsubscript𝑀𝑁MN measured on the same set of gauge configurations providing a consistency check for the scale setting procedure that has been used in our calculations. The details of our lattice setup including the computation of the two- and three-point functions are given in section, whereas the fitting procedure and physical extrapolation of the nucleon mass is discussed in section. Our excited-state analysis and strategy for physical extrapolation of the NMEs are explained in section. Lastly, a summary and outlook towards a final analysis is included in section.
content_sections.2 Lattice Setup: The ensembles used in this study have been generated by the Coordinated Lattice Simulation (CLS) initiative with Nf=2+1subscript𝑁𝑓21Nf=2+1 dynamical quark flavors of Wilson fermions and the tree-level improved Symanzik gauge action as described in Ref. citebib.bib4. A twisted mass regulator is used to suppress exceptional configurations citebib.bib5. Most ensembles have been simulated with open boundary conditions (oBC) in time to counter topological charge freezing citebib.bib6 with the exception of a few ensembles at a>0.05​fm𝑎0.05fma>0.05,fm with periodic boundary conditions (pBC). An overview of the ensemble included in this study can be found in Table. Data for E300 and N101 have been newly added and had not yet been available in Ref. citebib.bib2. The addition of E300 yields another data point close to physical quark masses (Mπ≈173​MeVsubscript𝑀𝜋173MeVMpiapprox 173,MeV) at the finest lattice spacing, further constraining the chiral and continuum extrapolation. On the other hand, N101 has the largest value of Mπ​Lsubscript𝑀𝜋𝐿MpiL among all ensembles and a large physical volume while otherwise sharing its input parameters with H105, thus improving finite volume extrapolations. Our analysis requires the computation of quark-connected two- and three-point functions C2​p​t​(Γ;t)superscript𝐶2ptΓ𝑡displaystyle C2pt(Gamma;t) =∑x→Γα​β​⟨Nα​(x→,t)​N¯β​⟩,absentsubscript→𝑥subscriptΓ𝛼𝛽delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑁𝛼→𝑥𝑡subscript¯𝑁𝛽0displaystyle=sumvecxGammaalphabeta Nalpha(vecx,t)barNbeta,, Cμ1​…​μnX​(Γ;tins,tsep)subscriptsuperscript𝐶𝑋subscript𝜇1…subscript𝜇𝑛Γsubscript𝑡inssubscript𝑡sepdisplaystyle CXmu1. . . mun(Gamma;tins,tsep) =∑x→,y→Γα​β​⟨Nα​(x→,tsep)​𝒪μ1​…​μnX​(y→,tins)​N¯β​⟩,absentsubscript→𝑥→𝑦subscriptΓ𝛼𝛽delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑁𝛼→𝑥subscript𝑡sepsubscriptsuperscript𝒪𝑋subscript𝜇1…subscript𝜇𝑛→𝑦subscript𝑡inssubscript¯𝑁𝛽0displaystyle=sumvecx,vecyGammaalphabeta Nalpha(vecx,tsep)OXmu1. . . mun(vecy,tins)barNbeta,, where Nα​(x→,t)subscript𝑁𝛼→𝑥𝑡Nalpha(vecx,t) denotes the nucleon interpolating field, 𝒪μ1​…​μnX​(y→,tins)subscriptsuperscript𝒪𝑋subscript𝜇1…subscript𝜇𝑛→𝑦subscript𝑡insOXmu1. . . mun(vecy,tins) the operator insertion and the actual choice of the spin projector ΓΓGamma depends on the analysis, cf. sectionsand. Initial and final state are always produced at rest and only vanishing momentum transfer is required for Cμ1​…​μnX​(Γ;tins,tsep)subscriptsuperscript𝐶𝑋subscript𝜇1…subscript𝜇𝑛Γsubscript𝑡inssubscript𝑡sepCXmu1. . . mun(Gamma;tins,tsep). The two relevant Euclidean time-separations for Cμ1​…​μnX​(Γ;tins,tsep)subscriptsuperscript𝐶𝑋subscript𝜇1…subscript𝜇𝑛Γsubscript𝑡inssubscript𝑡sepCXmu1. . . mun(Gamma;tins,tsep) are the source-sink separation tsep=tf−tisubscript𝑡sepsubscript𝑡𝑓subscript𝑡𝑖tsep=tf-ti and the insertion time tins=t−tisubscript𝑡ins𝑡subscript𝑡𝑖tins=t-ti, where tisubscript𝑡𝑖ti, t𝑡t and tfsubscript𝑡𝑓tf are the time positions of initial state, operator insertion and final state. The two- and three-point functions are computed on a common set of point sources using the truncated solver method citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9 and with the same smearing setup as described in Ref. citebib.bib3. Since (part of) the data are used for other projects as well, e. g. the computation of electromagnetic and axial form factors citebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12 also non-vanishing momenta have been computed for both C2​p​t​(Γ;t)superscript𝐶2ptΓ𝑡C2pt(Gamma;t) and Cμ1​…​μnX​(Γ;tins,tsep)subscriptsuperscript𝐶𝑋subscript𝜇1…subscript𝜇𝑛Γsubscript𝑡inssubscript𝑡sepCXmu1. . . mun(Gamma;tins,tsep). The source setup depends on the choice of boundary conditions. For ensembles with pBC sources are distributed randomly on the entire lattice volume. Nmeasmaxsuperscriptsubscript𝑁measmaxNmeasmax in Table is the resulting total number of measurements at the largest value of tsepsubscript𝑡septsep. Going to smaller values of tsepsubscript𝑡septsep the number of measurements Nmeassubscript𝑁measNmeas is divided in half every one or two steps in tsepsubscript𝑡septsep. This scaling of measurements is chosen such that the behavior of effective statistics is closer to a constant in tsepsubscript𝑡septsep instead of showing an exponential decay of the signal-to-noise ratio for increasing values of tsepsubscript𝑡septsep. On ensembles with oBC the sources are distributed on a single time slice in the bulk of the lattice and the scaling of measurements is only applied for tsep≲1​f​mless-than-or-similar-tosubscript𝑡sep1𝑓𝑚tseplesssim 1fm on most ensembles, as the data for tsep≳1​fmgreater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑡sep1fmtsepgtrsim 1,fm had been produced for the older study in Ref. citebib.bib3 without any scaling of statistics. The renormalization factors for the NMEs are the same as the ones that have been used in Ref. citebib.bib3. We use the gradient flow scale t0/a2subscript𝑡0superscript𝑎2t0/a2 introduced in Ref. citebib.bib13 to form dimensionless quantities from any observable before carrying out the physical extrapolation. For the values for t0/a2subscript𝑡0superscript𝑎2t0/a2, the determination of the physical value 8​t0=0.415​stat​sys8subscript𝑡00.415subscript4statsubscript2syssqrt8t0=0.415statsys that we use to set the scale, as well as further details on the scale setting procedure the reader is referred to Ref. citebib.bib14.
content_sections.3 Nucleon mass: The nucleon mass has been computed on each ensemble from a single exponential fit to the gauge averaged two-point function in Eq. . Use of the unpolarized spin projector Γ=Γ0≡12​(1+γ0)ΓsubscriptΓ0121subscript𝛾0Gamma=Gamma0equivfrac12left(1+gamma0right) leads to a favorable signal-to-noise ratio compared to a single, polarized projector. An example of the data quality for the effective nucleon mass on our two most chiral ensembles is shown in Fig. together with the results from the fit to the corresponding correlation function. The relative statistical errors on the fitted values of MNsubscript𝑀𝑁MN are typically a few permille on most ensembles. The physical extrapolation is carried out using a χ𝜒chiPT-inspired fit ansatzcitebib.bib15 MN​(Mπ,a,L)=M̊N+B​Mπ2+C​Mπ3+D​a2+E​Mπ3(Mπ​L)​e−Mπ​L,subscript𝑀𝑁subscript𝑀𝜋𝑎𝐿subscript̊𝑀𝑁𝐵superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋2𝐶superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋3𝐷superscript𝑎2𝐸superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋3subscript𝑀𝜋𝐿superscript𝑒subscript𝑀𝜋𝐿MN(Mpi,a,L)=mathringMN+BMpi2+CMpi3+Da2+EfracMpi3(MpiL)e-MpiL,, where the term ∼D​a2similar-toabsent𝐷superscript𝑎2sim Da2 accounts for the leading lattice artifact and the last one ∼E​Mπ3(Mπ​L)​e−Mπ​Lsimilar-toabsent𝐸superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋3subscript𝑀𝜋𝐿superscript𝑒subscript𝑀𝜋𝐿sim EfracMpi3(MpiL)e-MpiL for finite volume effects, cf. Ref. citebib.bib16. We find that including further terms of 𝒪​(Mπ4)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋4O(Mpi4) does not improve the fit as the corresponding free parameters are unconstrained by our lattice data at the current level of precision. The fits are performed using a binned bootstrap with NB=10000subscript𝑁𝐵10000NB=10000 bootstrap samples to propagate statistical errors from the input quantities to the final result. Fitting the full set of available lattice data, we find a physical value of the nucleon mass of MNphys=947​​MeV,superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑁phys94710MeVMNphys=947,MeV,, compatible with the experimental value. The physical pion mass Mπphyssuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋physMpiphys is fixed in units of t0subscript𝑡0sqrtt0 using its value in the isospin limit, i. e. Mπphys=Mπiso=134.8​​MeVsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋physsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋iso134.83MeVMpiphys=Mpiiso=134.8,MeVcitebib.bib17, and the uncertainty due to the use of t0subscript𝑡0sqrtt0 is propagated in the extrapolation. However, the uncertainty from fixing the physical point in Mπsubscript𝑀𝜋Mpi is much smaller than the one from the conversion of t0​MNphyssubscript𝑡0superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑁physsqrtt0MNphys to physical units, which entirely dominates the error of the final result. This is also visible in Fig. , where the width of the extrapolation bands from the resulting chiral, continuum and finite volume extrapolations does not contain the error from the conversion, unlike the larger error on the physical result. In order to assess the reliability of the physical extrapolation we have applied three different cuts to the input data, i. e. Mπ4subscript𝑀𝜋𝐿4MpiL>4, leading to MNphys=946​​MeVsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝑁phys94611MeVMNphys=946,MeV, MNphys=945​​MeVsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝑁phys94511MeVMNphys=945,MeV and MNphys=949​​MeVsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝑁phys94910MeVMNphys=949,MeV, respectively. Whereas these results are all in excellent agreement within errors, there is some mild tension for the resulting slopes of the chiral extrapolation with and without a cut in Mπsubscript𝑀𝜋Mpi as depicted in the upper two panels of Fig. , i. e. the fit is essentially unable to resolve terms beyond ∼Mπ2similar-toabsentsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋2sim Mpi2 when leaving out the ensembles at Mπ≥300​MeVsubscript𝑀𝜋300MeVMpigeq 300,MeV.

Operator insertions 𝒪μ1​…​μnXsubscriptsuperscript𝒪𝑋subscript𝜇1…subscript𝜇𝑛OXmu1. . . mun are restricted to quark isovector combinations only. Six different operators are considered that can be divided into two groups, i. e. local, dimension-three operators 𝒪μA=q¯​γμ​γ5​q,𝒪S=q¯​q,𝒪μ​νT=q¯​i​σμ​ν​q,formulae-sequencesubscriptsuperscript𝒪𝐴𝜇¯𝑞subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾5𝑞formulae-sequencesuperscript𝒪𝑆¯𝑞𝑞subscriptsuperscript𝒪𝑇𝜇𝜈¯𝑞𝑖subscript𝜎𝜇𝜈𝑞OAmu=barqgammamugamma5q,quadOS=barqq,quadOTmunu=barqisigmamunuq,, and dimension-four operators 𝒪μ​νv​D=q¯​γμDν↔q,𝒪μ​νa​D=q¯​γμ​γ5Dν↔q,𝒪μ​ν​ρt​D=q¯​σ[μν]Dρ↔q,OvDmunu=barqgammaleftmuright. !stackrelscriptstyleleftrightarrowDleft. nurightq,,qquadOaDmunu=barqgammaleftmuright. gamma5!stackrelscriptstyleleftrightarrowDleft. nurightq,,qquadOtDmunurho=barqsigmaleft[muleftnuright. right]!stackrelscriptstyleleftrightarrowDleft. rhorightq,, that exhibit one (symmetric) derivative Dμ↔=12(Dμ→−Dμ←),!stackrelscriptstyleleftrightarrowDmu=frac12(stackrelscriptstylerightarrowDmu-stackrelscriptstyleleftarrowDmu). The three-point functions are spin-projected with Γ3≡Γ0​(1+i​γ5​γ3)subscriptΓ3subscriptΓ01𝑖subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾3Gamma3equivGamma0left(1+igamma5gamma3right) and the same is used for the two-point functions entering the ratio to optimally exploit correlations.
content_sections.4 Matrix elements 4.1 Extraction of grou: In practice, source-sink separations of tsep≫1​fmmuch-greater-thansubscript𝑡sep1fmtsepgg 1,fm are needed to adequately suppress excited state in the extraction of ground state matrix elements, thus a naive plateau analysis of the ratio for the accessible values of tsep≲1.5​fmless-than-or-similar-tosubscript𝑡sep1.5fmtseplesssim 1.5,fm is insufficient. Three-point functions at larger values of tsepsubscript𝑡septsep cannot be computed with the desired statistical precision due to a severe signal-to-noise problem, which triggered the development of many, more sophisticated approaches to deal with excited states; for a review see Ref. citebib.bib18. Here we make use of two versions of the summation method citebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21 that is based on the sum of the ratio in Eq. over insertion times S​(tsep)=∑tins=atsep−aR​(tins,tsep)𝑆subscript𝑡sepsuperscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑡ins𝑎subscript𝑡sep𝑎𝑅subscript𝑡inssubscript𝑡sepS(tsep)=sumtins=atsep-aR(tins,tsep). The first one is the original, “plain” summation method linear in tsepsubscript𝑡septsep S​(tsep,tex=a)=const+M00​tsep,𝑆subscript𝑡sepsubscript𝑡ex𝑎constsubscript𝑀00subscript𝑡sepS(tsep,tex=a)=const+M00tsep,, that is employed as a cross-check for the second one, which is based on the two-state truncation of S​(tsep,tex=a)𝑆subscript𝑡sepsubscript𝑡ex𝑎S(tsep,tex=a) as described in Ref. citebib.bib2 S​(tsep,tex=a)=M00​(tsep−a)+2​M01​e−Δ​a−e−Δ​tsep1−e−Δ​a,𝑆subscript𝑡sepsubscript𝑡ex𝑎subscript𝑀00subscript𝑡sep𝑎2subscript𝑀01superscript𝑒Δ𝑎superscript𝑒Δsubscript𝑡sep1superscript𝑒Δ𝑎S(tsep,tex=a)=M00(tsep-a)+2tildeM01frace-Delta a-e-Delta tsep1-e-Delta a,, and fitted simultaneously to all six isovector NMEs with the energy gap ΔΔDelta between ground and first excited state as a common fit parameter to extract the ground state NMEs M00subscript𝑀00M00. The efficacy of both methods is readily assessed by comparing their respective results for the ground state NME as a function of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintsepmin. An example is shown in Fig. for a selection of four observables on the E300 ensembles. We find that the two-state fits always exhibit a much more rapid convergence towards the ground state. The final choice of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintsepmin is made such that Mπ​tsepmin≳0.7greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑀𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑡sepmin0.7Mpitsepmingtrsim 0.7 and Mπ​tsepmin≳0.5greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑀𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑡sepmin0.5Mpitsepmingtrsim 0.5 hold for the plain and two-state model, respectively. On some ensembles with large effective statistics tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintsepmin is increased further to obtain a reasonable fit quality. In general, we find that simultaneous two-state fits with a single gap lead to a good description of the data once the gap has converged. The gap typically approaches a value compatible with an N​π𝑁𝜋Npi state, at least on ensembles with statistics sufficiently large to track it as a function of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintsepmin before the signal is lost in noise. However, we observe a tension for low values of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintsepmin between local and twist-2 NMEs. In particular, we find the matrix elements for gA,Tu−dsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝐴𝑇𝑢𝑑gA,Tu-d to be clearly dominated by an N​π𝑁𝜋Npi state even at the smallest values of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintsepmin when fitted separately, whereas the leading contamination for the twist-2 NMEs seems to be generally dominated by a (much) heavier state. At larger values of tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintsepmin as used in our fits this tension becomes increasingly washed out. Still, it may be worthwhile to investigate an ansatz with two separate (effective) gaps for local and twist-2 NMEs in the future.
content_sections.4 Matrix elements 4.2 Physical extrapola: After extraction of the ground state NMEs O​(Mπ,a,L)𝑂subscript𝑀𝜋𝑎𝐿O(Mpi,a,L) on individual ensembles, the physical extrapolation needs to be carried out. To this end we employ an ansatz inspired by NNLO χ𝜒chiPT expression for the axial charge citebib.bib22 O​(Mπ,a,L)=AO+BO​Mπ2+CO​Mπ2​log⁡Mπ+DO​Mπ3+EO​an​(O)+FO​Mπ2Mπ​L​e−Mπ​L,𝑂subscript𝑀𝜋𝑎𝐿subscript𝐴𝑂subscript𝐵𝑂superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋2subscript𝐶𝑂superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋2subscript𝑀𝜋subscript𝐷𝑂superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋3subscript𝐸𝑂superscript𝑎𝑛𝑂subscript𝐹𝑂superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋2subscript𝑀𝜋𝐿superscript𝑒subscript𝑀𝜋𝐿O(Mpi,a,L)=AO+BOMpi2+COMpi2log Mpi+DOMpi3+EOan(O)+FOfracMpi2sqrtMpiLe-MpiL,, where the leading lattice artifact is given by n​(O)=2𝑛𝑂2n(O)=2 for O=gA,Su−d𝑂superscriptsubscript𝑔𝐴𝑆𝑢𝑑O=gA,Su-d and n​(O)=1𝑛𝑂1n(O)=1 otherwise. The last term is included to correct for finite volume effects citebib.bib23, which can reach the few-percent level for gAu−dsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝐴𝑢𝑑gAu-d depending on the box size; see also Refs. citebib.bib3, bib.bib2. The coefficient COsubscript𝐶𝑂CO is known analytically, and in case of the axial charge it is given by CgA=−AgA(2​π​fπ)2,subscript𝐶subscript𝑔𝐴subscript𝐴subscript𝑔𝐴superscript2𝜋subscript𝑓𝜋2CgA=frac-AgA(2pi fpi)2,, where AgA=g̊Au−dsubscript𝐴subscript𝑔𝐴superscriptsubscript̊𝑔𝐴𝑢𝑑AgA=mathringgAu-d is the isovector axial charge in the chiral limit and fπsubscript𝑓𝜋fpi the pion decay constant. Therefore, COsubscript𝐶𝑂CO is generally not a free parameter in the fit. However, fitting only the NLO expression without the 𝒪​(Mπ3)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜋3O(Mpi3) term, one finds that the chiral logarithm imposes a curvature not observed in our data and fitting it with a free coefficient even yields the wrong sign. In case of the axial charge we thus consider two different fit models: • “fit 1”: A NLO fit without the chiral logarithm, i. e. Eq. with CO=DO=0subscript𝐶𝑂subscript𝐷𝑂0CO=DO=0. • “fit 2”: A (full) NNLO fit as given in Eq. with COsubscript𝐶𝑂CO expressed through Eq. . For all other observables we currently employ only the first model. To gain further insight into systematics we apply the same cuts to the data entering the physical extrapolations as previously used for MNsubscript𝑀𝑁MN. An overview of preliminary results from all these variations is shown in Fig. for each observable. First of all, we observe an overall good agreement between results using data from the plain and two-state summation method, indicating that residual effects due to excited-state contamination are mostly under control. Secondly, the differences between results from fit 1 and fit 2 for the same set of input data of gAu−dsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝐴𝑢𝑑gAu-d are small compared to the variation due to cuts. On the other hand, applying the Mπ<300​MeVsubscript𝑀𝜋300MeVMpi<300,MeV cut seems to favor larger results, hinting at some possible tension in the chiral extrapolation. Altogether the model spread is not negligible, which is particularly true for the statistically precise gAu−dsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝐴𝑢𝑑gAu-d, but much less so for e. g. gTu−dsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝑇𝑢𝑑gTu-d. However, the fit qualities indicated by the resulting p𝑝p-values can vary strongly for different cuts and models on the same observable. Therefore, we intend to perform model averages for the final analysis to quantify systematic effects.
content_sections.5 Summary and outlook: In this proceedings contribution we have presented the current status of our analysis of isovector NMEs at vanishing momentum transfer as well as the nucleon mass on the same set of data. This set of ensembles and measurements can now be considered complete for this study and the final analysis is underway. From the physical extrapolation of the nucleon mass data we find good agreement with the experimental value within the scale setting uncertainty, which corroborates the t0subscript𝑡0t0 value from the dedicated scale setting study in Ref. citebib.bib14. However, for the final MNsubscript𝑀𝑁MN and NME analysis we intend to switch to a more recent and precise determination of the scale t0subscript𝑡0t0, i. e. the FLAG average value in Ref. citebib.bib1. Regarding the isovector NME analysis, we still plan to further refine our excited-state analysis, e. g. by allowing different gaps for matrix elements of local and twist-2 operators in the simultaneous fit for the two-state model for the summation method. It remains to be seen if this will have an impact on the physical extrapolation of the most precise NME(s) and if it further reduces the model spread observed in some of the cuts and fit variations for gAu−dsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝐴𝑢𝑑gAu-d as shown in Fig. . The caveat here is that cuts in pion mass or the lattice spacing that are intended to test the robustness of the chiral and continuum extrapolation, may actually also reveal residual excited-state effects if these are e. g. enhanced by smaller pion masses or depend on the available tsepminsuperscriptsubscript𝑡sepmintsepmin resolution. At any rate, for the final analysis we aim at obtaining physical results with fully controlled and quantified systematics from model averages.

all_papers.title:
Methods for high-precision determinations of radiative-leptonic decay
  form factors using lattice QCD
all_papers.abstract:
We present a study of lattice-QCD methods to determine the relevant hadronic
form factors for radiative leptonic decays of pseudoscalar mesons. We provide
numerical results for $D_s^+ \to \ell^+ \nu \gamma$. Our calculation is
performed using a domain-wall action for all quark flavors and on a single
RBC/UKQCD lattice gauge-field ensemble. The first part of the study is how to
best control two sources of systematic error inherent in the calculation,
specifically the unwanted excited states created by the meson interpolating
field, and unwanted exponentials in the sum over intermediate states. Using a
3d sequential propagator allows for better control over unwanted exponentials
from intermediate states, while using a 4d sequential propagator allows for
better control over excited states. We perform individual analyses of the 3d
and 4d methods as well as a combined analysis using both methods, and find that
the 3d sequential propagator offers good control over both sources of
systematic uncertainties for the smallest number of propagator solves. From
there, we further improve the use of a 3d sequential propagator by employing an
infinite-volume approximation method, which allows us to calculate the relevant
form factors over the entire allowed range of photon energies. We then study
improvements gained by performing the calculation using a different three-point
function, using ratios of three-point functions, averaging over positive and
negative photon momentum, and using an improved method for extracting the
structure-dependent part of the axial form factor. The optimal combination of
methods yields results for the $D_s^+ \to \ell^+ \nu \gamma$
structure-dependent vector and axial form factors in the entire kinematic range
with statistical plus fitting uncertainties of order 5%, using 25 gauge
configurations with 64 samples per configuration.

qualitative: Excited-state contamination in lattice QCD calculations arises from the meson interpolating field creating unwanted excited states, which can bias form factor determinations.
Unwanted exponentials in the sum over intermediate states contribute to systematic errors, particularly in three-point correlation functions.
The 3D sequential propagator method provides better control over unwanted exponentials from intermediate states by isolating the contribution of the ground state through spatial separation.
The 4D sequential propagator method is more effective at suppressing excited-state contamination by allowing for a more precise extraction of the ground-state contribution over time.
A combined analysis using both 3D and 4D sequential propagators improves systematic error control by leveraging the strengths of each method.
The 3D sequential propagator is found to offer superior control over both excited-state contamination and unwanted exponentials with fewer propagator solves, making it more efficient.

The infinite-volume approximation method, when applied to the 3D sequential propagator, enables the calculation of form factors across the full kinematic range of photon energies, overcoming finite-volume limitations.

Using ratios of three-point functions, averaging over positive and negative photon momenta, and improved extraction techniques for the structure-dependent axial form factor collectively reduce statistical and systematic uncertainties.
quantitative: The optimal method achieves statistical plus fitting uncertainties of order 5% for the structure-dependent vector and axial form factors of $D_s^+ \to \ell^+ \nu \gamma$.
The calculation is performed on 25 gauge configurations with 64 samples per configuration.
The study uses a single RBC/UKQCD lattice gauge-field ensemble with domain-wall fermions for all quark flavors.
The 3D sequential propagator method allows form factor determination over the entire allowed range of photon energies via the infinite-volume approximation.
The combined analysis of 3D and 4D sequential propagators is used to validate systematic error control, with the 3D method showing superior performance in terms of efficiency and control.
