In the cases where the data plateaus quickly in T𝑇T we use a fit form with BF(q)()=0superscriptsubscript𝐵superscript𝐹𝑞0BF(q)()=0. For fits to FVsubscript𝐹𝑉FV, we calculate the vector-meson (Ds∗)superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠(Ds*) energies for all values of p→Ds−p→γsubscript→𝑝subscript𝐷𝑠subscript→𝑝𝛾vecpDs-vecpgamma using a lattice dispersion relation of the form EH∗=mH∗+α​|p→|2+β​|p→|4. subscript𝐸superscript𝐻subscript𝑚superscript𝐻𝛼superscript→𝑝2𝛽superscript→𝑝4EH*=mH*+alpha|vecp|2+beta|vecp|4. The parameters mH∗,αsubscript𝑚superscript𝐻𝛼mH*,alpha and β𝛽beta are determined by performing fits to vector-meson energies determined from the associated vector-meson two-point functions for |p→|2∈(2​π/L)2​0,1,2,3,4superscript→𝑝2superscript2𝜋𝐿201234|vecp|2in(2pi/L)20,1,2,3,4. For reasons explained in Sec. , this lattice dispersion relation is valid for momenta at non-integer multiples of 2​π/L2𝜋𝐿2pi/L up to errors suppressed exponentially in the volume. The vector-meson energies are used as priors in fits for the tem0subscript𝑡𝑊0tW>0 time orderings of FVsubscript𝐹𝑉FV, with the prior equal to the central value and the prior width equal to the uncertainty of the fit result. The Dssubscript𝐷𝑠Ds mass determined from fits to the associated two-point function is used as a prior in the fits, with the prior value and prior width equal to the central value and the uncertainty of the fit result, respectively. The excited-state energy gap Δ​EΔ𝐸Delta E between the ground state and the first excited state created by the interpolating field is extracted by performing two-exponential fits to the two-point function. The fit result for Δ​EΔ𝐸Delta E is used as a prior in the form-factor fits, with the prior equal to the central value and the prior width equal to the uncertainty of the fit result scaled by a factor 1.5. The vector-form-factor fits are performed using the methods outlined in Sec. . Figure shows examples of the error bands from fits to FVsubscript𝐹𝑉FV with p→γ=2​π/L​(0,0,0.6)subscript→𝑝𝛾2𝜋𝐿000.6vecpgamma=2pi/L(0,0,0.6). We calculate the structure-dependent part of the axial form factor using the improved method in Sec. , and the fits to FA,S​D​(tH,T)subscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷subscript𝑡𝐻𝑇FA,SD(tH,T) must be modified accordingly. The improved method involves taking combinations of correlation functions for the same μ,ν𝜇𝜈mu,nu indices of Iμ​νsubscript𝐼𝜇𝜈Imunu at both non-zero photon momentum and zero photon momentum. This combination will receive contributions from two sets of intermediate states, each with different momentum. Fitting each of these states is in general difficult. To get around this, we perform fits accounting only for the unwanted exponential that is expected to be dominant. For the tem>0subscript𝑡em0ttextem>0 and tW0subscript𝑡em0ttextem>0 and tW0subscript𝑡𝑊0tW>0 data decay more quickly as pγsubscript𝑝𝛾pgamma is increased, and so the fits to this data only include the states with pγ=0subscript𝑝𝛾0pgamma=0. Figure shows examples of the error bands from fits to FA,S​Dsubscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷FA,SD with p→γ=2​π/L​(0,0,1.4)subscript→𝑝𝛾2𝜋𝐿001.4vecpgamma=2pi/L(0,0,1.4). Note that fits to FA,S​DEM,(s),<​(tH,T)superscriptsubscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷EM𝑠subscript𝑡𝐻𝑇FA,SDtextEM,(s),<(tH,T) were not stable for any allowed values of the fit ranges for all values of pγsubscript𝑝𝛾pgamma; this data was therefore not included in the final analysis. The results of FA,S​Dsubscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷FA,SD and FVsubscript𝐹𝑉FV, as well as the individual quark electromagnetic current current contributions to the form factors, are shown in Fig. as a function of xγsubscript𝑥𝛾xgamma. Note that these results are from a single ensemble, and still contain nonzero-lattice-spacing and unphysical-pion-mass systematic errors.
content_sections.XI Conclusions: In this work, we presented a study of lattice-QCD data-generation and analysis methods to determine the form factors describing radiative leptonic decays of pseudoscalar mesons. We calculated the relevant non-local matrix elements using the 3d, 4d, and 4d methods, and performed fits to the data to remove unwanted exponentials in the sum over intermediate states and from excited states created by the meson interpolating field. We demonstrated that the 3d method offers good control over both types of unwanted exponentials for a significantly reduced number of propagator solves compared to the 4d and 4d methods. From there, we further improved upon the 3d method by calculating the three-point function using the infinite-volume approximation method, which allows us to access the full range of kinetically allowed photon momenta without having to perform calculations in the moving frame of the meson. We then showed that the hadronic tensor could be extracted using an alternate three-point function with the electromagnetic current at the origin, rather than the weak current at origin. The alternate three-point function can be calculated by reusing propagators required for the original three-point function. Performing simultaneous fits to both data sets resulted in reductions in statistical noise for both FA,S​Dsubscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷FA,SD and FVsubscript𝐹𝑉FV, with the largest improvements at small and large xγsubscript𝑥𝛾xgamma. Furthermore, having both data sets increases the maximum possible fit range in T𝑇T for data used to calculate a given time ordering of the hadronic tensor. Calculating both data sets and exploiting this property was found to be crucial for extracting FA,S​Dsubscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷FA,SD. Further improvements in the statistical precision were achieved by multiplying the desired three-point function by ratios of three-point functions calculated using noise and point sources. This procedure resulted in significant improvements in precision for FA,S​Dsubscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷FA,SD, and modest improvements for FVsubscript𝐹𝑉FV. We also averaged the three-point functions over positive and negative photon momentum, which resulted in significant improvements in precision for FVsubscript𝐹𝑉FV at small xγsubscript𝑥𝛾xgamma, and modest improvements for FA,S​Dsubscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷FA,SD. Lastly, we extracted FA,S​Dsubscript𝐹𝐴𝑆𝐷FA,SD using a subtraction method that utilizes the properties of the three-point function as pγ→0→subscript𝑝𝛾0pgammato 0. This method has a number of advantages, including an increased precision at small xγsubscript𝑥𝛾xgamma, data plateauing more quickly in T𝑇T, and removal of 𝒪​(an/xγ)𝒪superscript𝑎𝑛subscript𝑥𝛾O(an/xgamma) lattice artifacts that diverge for xγ→0→subscript𝑥𝛾0xgammato 0. The optimal combination of methods yields results for the Ds+→ℓ+​ν​γ→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptℓ𝜈𝛾Ds+toell+nugamma structure-dependent vector and axial form factors in the entire kinematic range with statistical plus fitting uncertainties of order 5%, using 25 gauge configurations with 64 samples per configuration. Using the improved lattice methods developed in this work, we plan to perform calculations on more ensembles and perform extrapolations to the physical pion mass and the continuum for the pion, kaon, D(s)subscript𝐷𝑠D(s), and B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) radiative-leptonic-decay form factors. For the B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) decays, using the domain-wall action will require extrapolating in the mass. Alternatively, one could perform calculations at the physical b𝑏b-quark mass using the “relativistic heavy-quark action” citebib.bib77. In that context, a new non-perturbative method to tune the parameters of the relativistic heavy-quark action has been developed in Ref. citebib.bib78 from which extensions of the present study to B𝐵B-meson physics could benefit. We also plan to calculate the contributions from the quark-disconnected diagrams. Precise determinations of the QCD form factors for radiative leptonic decays are relevant for a number of phenomenological reasons. At small photon energies, a calculation of the radiative-leptonic decay rate is needed in order to include 𝒪​(αem)𝒪subscript𝛼emO(alphatextem) corrections to purely leptonic decays. At large photon energies, radiative leptonic decays are useful probes of the internal structure of the mesons as well as sensitive probes of physics beyond the Standard Model. Additionally, the methods and main outcomes presented in this study could be relevant for the lattice calculation of transition form factors describing the interaction between pseudoscalar mesons and two off-shell photons, since similar Euclidean correlation functions are involved. From such form factors, important information can be extracted on parton distribution amplitudes in hadrons (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib79), as well as on the hadronic light-by-light contribution to the muon anomalous magnetic moment (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib80 and references therein).

all_papers.title: Precise Omega baryons from lattice QCD
all_papers.abstract:
In this paper we determine the masses of $I(J^P)=0\left(3/2^+\right)$ and
$0\left(3/2^-\right)$ $\Omega$-baryon ground states using lattice QCD. We
utilise Wilson-clover ensembles with $2+1$ dynamical quark flavours generated
by the CLS consortium along a trajectory with a constant trace of the
quark-mass matrix. We show that N$^3$LO $\text{SU}(3)_f$ chiral perturbation
theory expressions describe the ground-state masses with positive-parity well,
and we use them to set the lattice scale. Methodologically, our combination of
gauge-fixed wall sources and the generalized Pencil of Functions allows for
high-precision determinations of the lattice spacing at a relative error of
around $0.3\%$ with controlled excited-state contamination. The fit we perform
allows for the continuum value of $t_0$ to vary, thereby determining this
quantity with a comparable level of precision to that of the lattice scale.
Using the resulting scales our measurement of the negative-parity
$\Omega^{3/2^{-}}$ state is found to be consistent with the recently-discovered
$\Omega(2012)^-$, which can therefore be assigned the quantum numbers
$I(J^P)=0\left(3/2^-\right)$.

sections.36: S
sections.37: Aoki et al., Eur
sections.41: Cè et al., Phys

qualitative: The generalized Pencil of Functions method is employed to control excited-state contamination in the determination of the Omega baryon ground state mass.
Gauge-fixed wall sources are used in conjunction with the generalized Pencil of Functions to enable high-precision lattice spacing determinations with controlled excited-state effects.

The use of a single-state fit is insufficient for accurate results due to significant excited-state contamination; two-state fits are necessary to properly isolate the ground state and suppress systematic errors from higher states.
The plateau in the effective mass plot is not stable under single-state fits, indicating the presence of excited-state contamination, which is mitigated by the two-state fit approach.
The combination of gauge-fixed wall sources and the generalized Pencil of Functions allows for a robust extraction of the ground state mass with minimal contamination from excited states.
quantitative: The lattice spacing is determined with a relative error of approximately 0.3%.
The fit allows for the continuum value of $ t_0 $ to vary, determining $ t_0 $ with a precision comparable to that of the lattice scale.
The effective mass plateau is observed only when using two-state fits, confirming the necessity of accounting for excited states.

The generalized Pencil of Functions method enables the extraction of the ground state mass with controlled excited-state contamination, leading to a high-precision determination of $ M_\Omega $ and $ t_0 $.

content_sections.I Introduction: Lattice quantum chromodynamics (lattice QCD) is now firmly established as the method of choice for calculations of the strong interaction in the non-perturbative regime, and the overall statistical resolution of lattice-determined quantities of interest is continuously improving. An example of such a quantity is the leading order hadronic contribution to the magnetic moment of the muon ((g−2)μ)subscript𝑔2𝜇((g-2)mu) citebib.bib1, where uncertainties of QCD determinations are falling below 1%percent11% citebib.bib2. In this “precision era” of Lattice QCD, sub-0.5%percent0.50.5% determinations of the scale in lattice simulations are of paramount importance to high-precision measurements, as they are vital to make sure the scale-setting isn’t the dominant source of uncertainty in the calculation citebib.bib3. In a minimal sense, for an nf=2+1subscript𝑛𝑓21nf=2+1-flavor simulation, 3 physical quantities need to be used to determine the “physical point”. These are thereby sacrificed as quantities that can be predicted by the theory. Typically, the iso-symmetric pion and kaon masses are used, plus a third quantity that sets the overall physical scale. In short, this procedure is referred to as “setting the scale”. Typically one chooses a particularly simple-to-measure physical quantity, which, together with the pion and kaon masses, then defines a “renormalisation trajectory” that sets the approach to the continuum. Of course any such choice has its own discretisation effects, so lattice spacing determinations from different quantities must differ. Historically, popular options have been the pion/kaon decay constants citebib.bib4, bib.bib5 or masses of light or light-strange baryons e. g. the Nucleon citebib.bib6, the Cascade citebib.bib7, or the fully-strange Omega baryon mass MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega citebib.bib8, bib.bib9. Even the splitting between the Υ′superscriptΥ′Upsilonprime and ΥΥUpsilon in lattice NRQCD has been used in the past citebib.bib10. Nowadays, an intermediate scale such as r0subscript𝑟0r0 citebib.bib11 or r1subscript𝑟1r1 citebib.bib12, t0subscript𝑡0t0 citebib.bib13, or w0subscript𝑤0w0 citebib.bib14 is often used, although its value in the continuum must still be inferred from another experimentally-measurable quantity. All of the determinations of the lattice scale have their own challenges – both in measurement, and regarding their associated systematic uncertainties. It is also possible to use suitable sets of physical quantities instead of a single observable. For instance, in citebib.bib15 the baryon octet and decuplet baryons masses were used simultaneously to set the scale. Whatever states, or combinations thereof, are used in the determination of the scale no longer are predictions of the theory. This makes quantities like the kaon decay constant fKsubscript𝑓𝐾fK, for which there is a phenomenological interest for tests of CKM-unitarity, undesirable as an observable for scale-setting. In this paper we will demonstrate that the I​(JP)=0​(3/2)+𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript32I(JP)=0(3/2)+ ΩΩOmega-baryon ground-state mass MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega is relatively straightforward to obtain with good precision. Subsequently the measured MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega can be used in combination with dimensionless quantities based on the light meson masses and t0subscript𝑡0t0 to set the scale. The ΩΩOmega-baryon provides a very enticing state for setting the scale as strange quark propagators are relatively cheap to compute in comparison to those of the light flavors. The noise to signal ratio is not large, no complicated improved currents and renormalisation is needed, and finite-volume effects are expected to be small. The ΩΩOmega in pure QCD is stable and a determination of the mass in lattice QCD is therefore unambiguous. As the ΩΩOmega has isospin 0, strong isospin-breaking effects appear in the sea rather than in the valence sector and are expected to be negligible; on top of this pure-QED effects due to the charged nature of the strange quarks are present but expected to be small citebib.bib16. The ΩΩOmega is very precisely known experimentally too, making it an ideal candidate for scale-setting. With this in mind, we will present a highly-accurate lattice-spacing and t0subscript𝑡0sqrtt0 determination using MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega determined on the Coordinated Lattice Simulations (CLS) ensembles citebib.bib17, bib.bib18. In the next section we will introduce our methodology, followed by a discussion of flavor-SU​SU3textSU (SU​fSUsubscript3𝑓textSUf) chiral fits in Section. We then proceed to present our ΩΩOmega-baryon masses in Section and the resulting determination of the lattice scale in Section. We also compare our determination to previous ones using some of the same ensembles along different renormalisation trajectories citebib.bib5, bib.bib7, bib.bib15. In addition, we use our determined lattice scales to compute the physical mass of the negative parity MΩ(3/2)−superscriptsubscript𝑀Ωsuperscript32MOmega(3/2)--baryon and present the results of this in Section. The calculated mass is consistent with the mass of the recently-discovered Ω​−Ωsuperscript2012Omega- citebib.bib19.
content_sections.II Methodology: We use the simple local operator for the ΩΩOmega (lower color, upper spin indices) with open spin index κ𝜅kappa, Ωiκ​(x)=ϵa​b​c​(saT​C​γi​sb)​scκ​(x),subscriptsuperscriptΩ𝜅𝑖𝑥subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑎𝑏𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑠𝑎𝑇𝐶subscript𝛾𝑖subscript𝑠𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑠𝜅𝑐𝑥Omegakappai(x)=epsilonabc(saTCgammaisb)skappac(x), focusing only on the polarisation-diagonal components citebib.bib8 to make the contraction particularly cheap. The parity of choice of choice is projected-out in the usual manner, CΩ±​(t−t′)=12​(1±γt)κ​κ′​13​∑i=x,y,z∑xL3Ωiκ​(x,t)​(Ωiκ′​(0,t′))†. superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡superscript𝑡′12superscriptplus-or-minus1subscript𝛾𝑡𝜅superscript𝜅′13subscript𝑖𝑥𝑦𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑥superscript𝐿3subscriptsuperscriptΩ𝜅𝑖𝑥𝑡superscriptsubscriptsuperscriptΩsuperscript𝜅′𝑖0superscript𝑡′†COmegapm(t-tprime)=frac12(1pmgammat)kappakappaprimefrac13sumi=x,y,zsumxL3Omegakappai(x,t)(Omegakappaprimei(0,tprime))dagger,. We perform an average over the forward and backward propagating states 1With particular care being taken when going through the temporal boundaries as anti-periodic boundary conditions are applied on the fermions in the solve. , with the appropriate parity projections to obtain a correlator with the correct JP=3/2±superscript𝐽𝑃3superscript2plus-or-minusJP=3/2pm quantum numbers. These forward and backward states are measured to be statistically quite independent and come only at the cost of more contractions (at least for the periodic gauge field ensembles). To ameliorate the cost of the creation of the deflated subspace used in our solver citebib.bib21 we will do as many solves as beneficial on each individual configuration. Here we invoke the Truncated Solver method citebib.bib22: For periodic ensembles in time we use translational-invariance in time and perform low-precision (sloppy) inversions on every timeslice (t′)superscript𝑡′(tprime), and one exact solve randomly chosen in the bulk. For the open boundaries we start sufficiently far from the boundary and average every forward-propagating correlator on every timeslice from there until a cutoff below Lt/2subscript𝐿𝑡2Lt/2 and likewise the backward propagating opposite-parity projected correlator from Lt−t′subscript𝐿𝑡superscript𝑡′Lt-tprime decreasing the source position. This also makes use of translational-invariance in time but restricted to the bulk away from the boundaries. We measure the negative-parity, JP=3/2−superscript𝐽𝑃3superscript2JP=3/2- state in a similar fashion, as it is only a trivial parity-projection of the basic contracted correlator. The one-end-trick (e. g. citebib.bib23) is not applicable to baryon correlators and simple generalizations suffer from a signal to noise problem as explained in citebib.bib24. Popular alternative choices are some form of point sources (with or without smearing) or Coulomb gauge-fixed wall sources citebib.bib25, bib.bib26. We will apply the latter option, which obviously requires us to fix the gauge, but yields a far superior volume-average. These have the limitation that the quarks carry a definite momentum but as everything we will consider will be at rest, thereby being no limitation for this work. Fixing to Coulomb gauge to high precision can be a numerically-cheap procedure if done using the Fourier-accelerated non-linear conjugate gradient algorithm of citebib.bib27, where an average gauge-fixing accuracy of Θ=10−14Θsuperscript1014Theta=10-14 can be achieved with negligible resources even for the largest volumes considered here (963×192superscript963192963times 192). The main mass-trajectory CLS uses keeps the trace of the quark-mass matrix constant. As the pion mass decreases the strange quark is made heavier, simultaneously the volume is enlarged to ensure mπ​L⪆4greater-than-or-approximately-equalssubscript𝑚𝜋𝐿4mpiLgtrapprox 4. Hence, at the physical pion-mass point, the ensembles are expected to provide the most precise data and to most strongly constrain our extrapolations. As gauge-fixed wall sources approach the plateau region from below for the ΩΩOmega and many other states beside pions (they are maximally asymmetric at source and sink), some care is needed in controlling excited-state contamination so that masses are not found to be lower than their asymptotic value. Empirically, we have found the best way to do this is to form a simple 2×2222times 2 generalised Pencil of Functions (PoF) citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30 matrix of the Wall-Point correlator CΩ±​(t)superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡COmegapm(t) MΩ±​(t)=(CΩ±​(t)CΩ±​(t+1)CΩ±​(t+1)CΩ±​(t+2)). superscriptsubscript𝑀Ωplus-or-minus𝑡matrixsuperscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡1superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡1superscriptsubscript𝐶Ωplus-or-minus𝑡2MOmegapm(t)=beginpmatrixCOmegapm(t)COmegapm(t+1) COmegapm(t+1)COmegapm(t+2) endpmatrix. We solve this as a symmetric Generalised Eigenvalue Problem (GEVP) citebib.bib31, bib.bib32 at a fixed time τ0subscript𝜏0tau0 and use the eigenvectors at some later time τ𝜏tau to approximately diagonalise the resulting correlator matrix. We then take the diagonal components as the principal correlators. A simple, fully-correlated, single-exponential fit to the lowest-lying principal correlator is sufficient to determine the ground state ΩΩOmega-baryon mass precisely. Some attention is needed in changing the GEVP parameters τ0subscript𝜏0tau0 and τ𝜏tau, and increasing these for finer lattice spacings is necessary. (Similar results could also be obtained by increasing the spacing between elements creating the matrix. ) The PoF method yields a statistically-noisier result but removes a large amount of the excited-state contamination, which would usually cause the Wall-point correlator to approach from below. The application of the PoF is a trade-off as it allows for fits at short times albeit with data which is noisier than usual. The PoF also removes a lot of correlation within neighbouring points of the data making correlated fits easier. It also agrees well with a correlated two-exponential fit, with much less ambiguity on the ground-state’s mass as compared to multi-exponential fits which often show some lower fit-bound dependence. A comparison of these two approaches can be seen in Fig. for one of our ensembles; note that there is no reduction in statistical uncertainty but the PoF is less sensitive to the choice of fit range. Our final approach is quite similar to the scale-setting used in citebib.bib2, but in that work the authors used smeared point sources, missing out on the volume average from the wall source. The PoF is not ubiquitously effective, for instance it does not often work well for mesons; in citebib.bib33 we found it was effective for only one of our tetraquark operators, the one that looked like a compact diquark-antidiquark. This suggests that the method could have something to do with the spin-color nature of the operator and the compact nature of the state considered.
content_sections.III A flavor-SU​(3)SU3\\text{SU}(3) chir: We apply the chiral Lagrangian with three light flavours to compute the quark-mass dependence of the ΩΩOmega mass. Expressions for all baryon octet and decuplet masses have been documented in a series of previous works citebib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib15 where particular emphasis was put on the use of on-shell hadron masses in the one-loop contributions. In previous works citebib.bib38, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 such a framework lead to significantly-improved convergence properties of the chiral expansion. Moreover, with the use of on-shell masses a much-improved description of finite-box effects stemming from such loop terms was seen citebib.bib35. In particular, the well-known Lüscher type terms are contained in our expressions, as is important for most of the baryon decuplet states. Given such a framework in the isospin-limit, the set of baryon masses on a specified lattice ensemble would have to be determined as a solution of 8 coupled and non-linear equations. In this work we focus on the positive-parity ground-state ΩΩOmega-mass on CLS ensembles, where we take the lattice values of all hadron masses that enter the loop expressions, i. e. the pion and kaon masses as well as J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2323/2 ΞΞXi masses. Instead of solving the aforementioned non-linear system we insert lattice values into the loop expressions and extract from there specific combinations of Low-Energy-Constants (LECs), and ultimately the lattice scale parameters. Our starting point is an expression for the ΩΩOmega-mass valid in a finite box at the NLO accuracy level. As will be illustrated below in Sec. an attempt fitting NLO does not suffice to arrive at the precision required for a faithful reproduction of the lattice data. We note that an extrapolation attempt using the chiral Lagrangian with two light flavours only citebib.bib39 is futile also in our case. In the following we denote with mπsubscript𝑚𝜋mpi, mKsubscript𝑚𝐾mK and mηsubscript𝑚𝜂meta the masses of the Goldstone bosons at a given lattice scale “a” of a considered ensemble. Correspondingly, MΩsubscript𝑀ΩMOmega, MΞsubscript𝑀ΞMXi and MΞ∗subscript𝑀superscriptΞMXi* are the baryon masses on that ensemble. Later on we will also consider such masses as expressed in units of 8​t08subscript𝑡08,t0, a popular choice in the lattice community. We fit our data in terms of the dimensionless quantities on each lattice ensemble. Labeling these for a particular state “Q” as, φQ=8​t0​mQ2. subscript𝜑𝑄8subscript𝑡0superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑄2varphiQ=8t0mQ2;. Our starting point will involve specific combinations of LECs used previously in citebib.bib15. Any application of the chiral Lagrangian requires the knowledge of the quark masses, mu,md,subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑚𝑑mu,md, and mssubscript𝑚𝑠ms, that characterize a given lattice ensemble. In principle, the π𝜋pi, K𝐾K, and η𝜂eta masses follow in terms of a set of relevant LECs. If the loop contributions to the meson masses are formulated in terms of on-shell masses, already the determination of the the π𝜋pi, K𝐾K, and η𝜂eta go in terms of the solution of a set of three coupled and nonlinear equations. Conversely, if π𝜋pi and K𝐾K masses are known on a given ensemble, we may directly compute the quark masses citebib.bib36, bib.bib15. Omegatildef2,fracdd,tildeMOmega,BigtildecOmega2,tildeJKXi(tildeMOmega)+frac13,tildehOmega2,Big(tildeJetaOmega(tildeOmega)+tildeJKXi*(tildeMOmega)Big)Big,. They are obtained from the functions in by a multiplication with suitable powers of 8​t08subscript𝑡08t0. Here the masses and t0subscript𝑡0t0 values as measured on each ensemble are used. In Eq. we use the notation Δ​[⋯]Δdelimited-⋯Delta[cdots] to indicate that the argument is subtracted by its corresponding expression at the physical point, i. e. at physical quark masses, infinite volume, and in the continuum limit. By making this subtraction point a fit parameter we can provide a measurement for the continuum t0subscript𝑡0t0. From a practical perspective this formulation is neat because everything within the curly braces in Eq. is dimensionless and can be fit simultaneously over all ensembles, dividing out the lattice-spacing dependence. Comparing this to Eq. , which requires everything (LECs and all) to have an implicit scale, our lattice fit omits this tedious book-keeping exercise. t0subscript𝑡0t0 on each ensemble is often more precisely determined than the K𝐾K or π𝜋pi so forming these dimensionless quantities does not incur any difficulties. Eqs. and are organised by order in the chiral expansion. With the “1” being the leading order (LO), the terms d0subscript𝑑0d0 and dDsubscript𝑑𝐷dD contributing at next-to-leading-order (NLO), the “bubble” terms CAsubscript𝐶𝐴CA and HAsubscript𝐻𝐴HA at NLO and the final “tadpole” terms gΩ​(π/K/η)(S/V),eΩ(π/K/η)superscriptsubscript𝑔Ω𝜋𝐾𝜂𝑆𝑉superscriptsubscript𝑒Ω𝜋𝐾𝜂gOmega(pi/K/eta)(S/V),eOmega(pi/K/eta) at NLO. For Eq. the NLO terms will also receive corrections from the small mass-dependence of t0subscript𝑡0t0 at NLO, which we will effectively parametrize. Altogether there are 13 combinations of LECs involved, with terms d0,dDsubscript𝑑0subscript𝑑𝐷d0,,dD and CA,HAsubscript𝐶𝐴subscript𝐻𝐴CA,,HA and eΩ(Q),gΩ​Q(S),gΩ​Q(V)subscriptsuperscript𝑒𝑄Ωsubscriptsuperscript𝑔𝑆Ω𝑄superscriptsubscript𝑔Ω𝑄𝑉e(Q)Omega,,g(S)Omega Q,,gOmega Q(V). Most important are d0,dD,CAsubscript𝑑0subscript𝑑𝐷subscript𝐶𝐴d0,dD,,CA, gK​Ω(S)+gη​Ω(S)subscriptsuperscript𝑔𝑆𝐾Ωsubscriptsuperscript𝑔𝑆𝜂Ωg(S)KOmega+g(S)etaOmega, eΩ(η)subscriptsuperscript𝑒𝜂Ωe(eta)Omega, which already permit an excellent reproduction of the data set. Our current dataset is not able to determine all LECs that are relevant for the ΩΩOmega-mass at NLO. This is expected to change once further ensembles are considered.
content_sections.IV Lattice determination of the Omega-ba: In Tab. we list our results (in lattice units) for the I​(JP)=0​(3/2+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃03superscript2I(JP)=0(3/2+) and 0​(3/2−)03superscript20(3/2-) ΩΩOmega-baryon masses as well as the statistics used to obtain our measurement. Further details on the generation of these nf=2+1subscript𝑛𝑓21nf=2+1 Wilson-clover ensembles and the naming convention used can be found in citebib.bib17. It is evident that as the strange mass increases to the physical point and as the physical volume increases, the statistical error of the determined ΩΩOmega-baryons decreases significantly for the positive-parity state but for the negative-parity the situation is less clear. In this work we use a subset of generated configurations for our measurement, choosing them maximally-evenly-spaced throughout the Monte-Carlo trajectory. Making use of the improved deflated reweighting factors from citebib.bib42 (we happen to use sets of configurations where there is mostly no evidence for negative strange reweighting factors citebib.bib43, exceptions were the ensembles A654, S400, D452, D450, and E250). Once this is done, some binning is performed when necessary in order to reduce autocorrelations. As we do not have the raw bootstraps for the φQsubscript𝜑𝑄varphiQ-variables, random Gaussian noise distributions are drawn with widths corresponding to the error. Not knowing the correlation between these measurements we treat them as fully uncorrelated. As they are “x-axis errors” we don’t expect this to lead to a significant uncertainty in determining the lattice spacings accurately (the slope in mass turns out to be small) but at close to physical masses the relative uncertainty of the pion mass in particular will have a significant contribution to the total uncertainty of the final determination of the lattice scale. Several of these ensembles were generated with the same action parameters, differing only in volume. (U103, H101) and (H200, N202) are at the SU​fSUsubscript3𝑓textSUf-symmetric point, whereas (H105, N101) and (X451, N451) are at intermediate pion mass. Having these pairs of ensembles provides a very strong handle on finite-volume effects. Already we can see from Tab. and Fig. that the largest finite-volume effects for our ensembles occur where the π𝜋pi, K𝐾K, and η𝜂eta are degenerate. Indeed, U103 has mπ/K/η​Lsubscript𝑚𝜋𝐾𝜂𝐿mpi/K/etaL of 4.334.334.33 whereas H101 has 5.835.835.83, similarly H200 has 4.334.334.33 and N202 has 6.446.446.44. At intermediate pion mass there are large mπ​Lsubscript𝑚𝜋𝐿mpiL differences between H105 and N101 (3.883.883.88 and 5.825.825.82) yet tiny differences in their ΩΩOmega-baryon masses. Along with the SU​fSUsubscript3𝑓textSUf-symmetric point data this strongly indicates finite volume effects are dominated by mK​Lsubscript𝑚𝐾𝐿mKL or mη​Lsubscript𝑚𝜂𝐿metaL (6.476.476.47 and 9.689.689.68 for mK​Lsubscript𝑚𝐾𝐿mKL on these boxes). Similarly, X451 and N451 have mπ​L=(4.42,5.31)subscript𝑚𝜋𝐿4.425.31mpiL=(4.42,5.31) and mK​L=(7.12,8.56)subscript𝑚𝐾𝐿7.128.56mKL=(7.12,8.56) respectively, but yet they have statistically compatible results for a​MΩ(3/2)+𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑀Ωsuperscript32aMOmega(3/2)+. These few ensembles already provide strong-enough handles in mπ​Lsubscript𝑚𝜋𝐿mpiL to rule-out a pure SU​fSUsubscript2𝑓textSUf chiral description, as that formally has no mK​Lsubscript𝑚𝐾𝐿mKL contribution. Fig. illustrates our lattice data divided by the PDG ΩΩOmega-baryon mass (1.672451.672451.67245 GeV). From the coarsest (black) to the finest (indigo) ensembles, against the dimensionless pion-mass-squared analog φπsubscript𝜑𝜋varphipi.

The authors of citebib.bib7 have measured independently the ΩΩOmega-baryon on most of the ensembles used here, so it is important to cross-check with their results. Fig. shows the bare (lattice units) mass determinations where we have subtracted our central value from both results (where results exist on the same ensembles) and added the bare gauge coupling β𝛽beta to distinguish data points in a plot. This is plotted again against φπsubscript𝜑𝜋varphipi to check for consistency as a function of the light-quark mass. Our data is at least a factor of 2 statistically more precise on all ensembles, and at best has a factor of 10 smaller statistical uncertainty. Our largest discrepancies with citebib.bib7 are for A653 (1.7​σ)1.7𝜎(1.7sigma), D200 (1.6​σ)1.6𝜎(1.6sigma), and E300 (2.3​σ)2.3𝜎(2.3sigma). In cases where there exists discrepancies their results are typically systematically above ours. As the authors of citebib.bib7 use Smeared-Smeared point sources, which are bounded from above, this could indicate that there is still some excited-state contamination in their mass determinations. Figure shows a zoom into the β=3.55𝛽3.55beta=3.55 data of Fig. . Here we see that our statistical resolution is always better and dramatically improves as the physical volume increases, due to the better volume average obtained with gauge-fixed wall sources. This is an important feature for our mass trajectory: As our closest-to-physical pion masses also have the largest extent, and as our lattice spacing is defined at physical masses these will provide the greatest constraint to our scale determination and t0subscript𝑡0t0. In a cost comparison to citebib.bib7 for the largest box (i. e. most computationally-expensive) with the lightest pion mass (E250) they used in total 15,6481564815,648 smeared, full precision, point sources. In comparison, we used 11,9041190411,904 mostly low-precision wall sources. Even for the strange quark our truncated solves are a factor of 4 cheaper than full solves. Comparatively, we are roughly a factor 5 cheaper in overall cost, and almost a factor 10 more statistically precise. One should however note that our calculation is a dedicated project while theirs is performed as part of a larger baryon-structure physics programme.
content_sections.V Results of the chiral fits and determi: Our fits to the data in Tab. are all based on Eq. , where a few remarks are in order. In the upper part of Tab. we list the chiral input parameters that we take from the literature citebib.bib15. Anticipating that our Omega-baryon fits are not discriminative to these choices, these parameters can at least be partially absorbed into our fit parameters. Already at NLO we have too many fit parameters and will not be able to obtain values for these ourselves. The pole masses and t0subscript𝑡0t0 values in Eq. are mostly taken from citebib.bib44 and citebib.bib7 with the exception of those directly measured by us and listed in App. . The η𝜂eta-contributions are estimated using the GMOR relation with mη2=(4​mK2−mπ2)/3superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜂24superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐾2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋23meta2=(4,mK2-mpi2)/3. It turns out that for the fit the finite-volume tadpole terms Iη(n)superscriptsubscript𝐼𝜂𝑛tildeIeta(n) are quite similar to those of IK(n)superscriptsubscript𝐼𝐾𝑛tildeIK(n) in . Along our trajectory it is impossible to separate the two types of contributions. Keeping this in mind we drop all tadpole and bubble terms involving the η𝜂eta, assuming that their impact is taken care of by using effective kaon parameters. Unfortunately, with the precision of the current data we must add a few priors for chiral parameters for some of the flattest directions, these being: dD=−0.07±0.03subscript𝑑𝐷plus-or-minus0.070.03tildedD=-0.07pm 0.03, cΩ=0.9±0.9subscript𝑐Ωplus-or-minus0.90.9tildecOmega=0.9pm 0.9, and hΩ=0.4±0.2subscriptℎΩplus-or-minus0.40.2tildehOmega=0.4pm 0.2. Ultimately the two bubble terms do not contribute significantly, but due to the dependence of ZΩsubscript𝑍ΩtildeZOmega on cΩsubscript𝑐ΩtildecOmega and hΩsubscriptℎΩtildehOmega we need something to help stop the fitter wandering off. The prior on dDsubscript𝑑𝐷tildedD is based on Eq. . An argurment for the prior on cΩsubscript𝑐ΩtildecOmega has been made earlier, whereas the prior on hΩsubscriptℎΩtildehOmega, which is poorly known, is from an ad-hoc procedure. We set it by performing a fit with a wide prior and moving the central value to the former fit result and shrinking the width until it is 50%percent5050% width of the central value. The fit struggled with the wide prior and this aforementioned procedure was found to most-stably fit the data. Regardless of this procedure the lattice spacings remain the same, even after setting hΩsubscriptℎΩtildehOmega to zero. For the iso-symmetric kaon and pion that define our physical light-mass point we use the values recommended in citebib.bib45 of mπ=134.8​​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜋134.83MeVmpi=134.8text MeV and mK=494.2​​MeVsubscript𝑚𝐾494.23MeVmK=494.2text MeV. In Eq. in the J-functions, the Ξ,Ξ∗ΞsuperscriptΞXi,,Xi* enter; for these we use the measured lattice masses but we must subtract their continuum values in the definition of our ΔΔDelta. To do so, we take the masses from the 2023 PDG citebib.bib46, forming an average of the (real part) of the charged and neutral states. For the physical ΩΩOmega we will perform a fit using the PDG value and one using an estimate of the mass-correction due to QED, quoting the latter for our final results.

Table indicates the quality of fit we obtain with increasing order in the expansion of Eq. . It should be clear from Fig. that we cannot fit this data sensibly to a constant, hence the LO value has a huge χ2/d​o​fsuperscript𝜒2𝑑𝑜𝑓chi2/dof. Going to NLO helps significantly in providing a slope to the data but still is nowhere near a good fit. Adding the bubble terms provides only a mild improvement and adding the tadpole terms that come with the strong finite-volume effects improves the situation greatly. Here we drop the gΩ​η(S/V)subscriptsuperscript𝑔𝑆𝑉Ω𝜂tildeg(S/V)Omegaeta terms as the fit cannot distinguish them from the kaon contributions. We also set to zero eΩ(π)superscriptsubscript𝑒Ω𝜋tildeeOmega(pi) and eΩ(K)superscriptsubscript𝑒Ω𝐾tildeeOmega(K) as they turn out to be zero, but keep eΩ(η)superscriptsubscript𝑒Ω𝜂tildeeOmega(eta) with the intention of absorbing any mass-dependence from t0subscript𝑡0t0 citebib.bib47 that would enter at NLO in our expressions. Already at NLO we are close to over-determining our data p>0.9𝑝0.9p>0.9), and it turns out that we can reasonably omit the gΩ​π/η(S/V)subscriptsuperscript𝑔𝑆𝑉Ω𝜋𝜂tildeg(S/V)Omegapi/eta terms in Eq. as gΩ​π(S)superscriptsubscript𝑔Ω𝜋𝑆tildegOmegapi(S) was found to be consistent with 0 and gΩ​π(V)superscriptsubscript𝑔Ω𝜋𝑉tildegOmegapi(V) only 1​σ1𝜎1sigma away from 0. This gives a slightly larger χ2/d​o​fsuperscript𝜒2𝑑𝑜𝑓chi2/dof with (27 data points, 14 fit parameters with 3 priors) 16 degrees of freedom and will be our final quoted result. Removing or keeping the pion tadpole terms in the fit does not change the obtained lattice spacings within error.

Table gives our final fit results based on Eq. but without the extraneous parameters gΩ​(π/η)(S/V)superscriptsubscript𝑔Ω𝜋𝜂𝑆𝑉tildegOmega(pi/eta)(S/V), eΩ(π)superscriptsubscript𝑒Ω𝜋tildeeOmega(pi) and eΩ(K)superscriptsubscript𝑒Ω𝐾tildeeOmega(K), which turns out to be zero with the renormalization scale we use. This parameterizes our preferred description of the Ω(3/2)+superscriptΩsuperscript32Omega(3/2)+ data in Tab. . The lattice (tilded) variables obtained by the fit are listed in Appendix. We convert our lattice spacings into fermi and our dimensionful fit parameters into GeV units using our determined value of t0subscript𝑡0sqrtt0 for self-consistency. The values are collected in Tab. for final presentation and to compare with the literature. The ΩΩOmega-baryon carries electric charge in nature, and this is expected to provide a correction to its mass at below the 0.2%percent0.20.2% level citebib.bib16. To estimate the influence of QED we have taken the prediction from citebib.bib7 of MΩ(3/2)+=1.6695​GeVsuperscriptsubscript𝑀Ωsuperscript321.6695GeVMOmega(3/2)+=1.6695text GeV, which is close to this upper bound, and rerun our fit. We quote our final result as these values and a difference between using the charged ΩΩOmega and this result as an associated systematic. For all cases the difference in lattice spacings and t0subscript𝑡0t0 obtained are negligible compared to our statistical uncertainty as the physical matching point (x-axis) for the ΩΩOmega also changes slightly and mostly compensates the small change in the y-axis. We now turn our focus to the determination of the LECs. Given that we use an SU​fSUsubscript3𝑓textSUf-framework, an uncertainty of only around (2-3) MeV is certainly unexpected. We find it surprising that with an NLO chiral approach it is possible to reproduce the very accurate ΩΩOmega-masses across the entire range of pions and kaons used here. A previous study, citebib.bib15, estimated a significantly larger associated uncertainty of about (10-15) MeV on the baryon masses from the chiral-series truncation order. Nonetheless, our values for d0,dD,CAsubscript𝑑0subscript𝑑𝐷subscript𝐶𝐴d0,dD,CA and HAsubscript𝐻𝐴HA appear reasonable, unsurprisingly as they are mostly set by priors. However, the somewhat unexpectedly-large size of the tadpole parameters gΩ​K(S/V)subscriptsuperscript𝑔𝑆𝑉Ω𝐾g(S/V)Omega K in Tab. may be a consequence of using the chiral approach outside its domain of validity. A comparison of our LECs with the corresponding “more natural” values of citebib.bib15 shows large discrepancies, even though the analyses are based on similar ensembles. We note that setting either gΩ​K(V)subscriptsuperscript𝑔𝑉Ω𝐾g(V)Omega K or gΩ​K(S)subscriptsuperscript𝑔𝑆Ω𝐾g(S)Omega K to zero yields a χ2/d​o​f≈1.2superscript𝜒2𝑑𝑜𝑓1.2chi2/dofapprox 1.2 with the nonzero parameter becoming larger to accommodate this. The previous study of citebib.bib15 used the data of citebib.bib7, and draws into question some of the systematic uncertainties quoted in their obtained masses. Regardless of the fact that the baryon masses used in citebib.bib15 were much less precise, the quoted precision in the lattice scales there came in part from a scale-setting in which the empirical values of all baryon masses were used. The quoted precision of that determination can be seen in the comparison with our results and others from the literature in Fig. . While the true value of the chiral LECs remains a somewhat open question; in the future, improved lattice spectra should allow for a better determination of them. If our current values hold they may imply exotic features in the s-wave scattering processes of the Goldstone bosons off the baryon decuplet states. Whether or not the LECs are realistic is mostly irrelevant to the determination of the scale and t0subscript𝑡0sqrtt0. It is a form that describes our data well in interpolation/mild extrapolation (depending on the β𝛽beta) in the quark masses with an extrapolation in the volume, which is all we need. As the physical fit parameter (and combinations with physical masses that incorporate it) t0subscript𝑡0t0 is a constant, it competes with our determined lattice spacings (which are also constants for each β𝛽beta). It is therefore unsurprising that these parameters will be quite correlated (as can be seen by our correlation matrix in the Appendices in Tab. ). As the ensemble E250 (β=3.55𝛽3.55beta=3.55) has the smallest statistical error and lies closest to the physical pion and kaon, it has the strongest influence on the fit, and yields the largest correlation with t0subscript𝑡0t0. Our worst relative error (0.32%percent0.320.32%) on the lattice spacing appears for β=3.85𝛽3.85beta=3.85, where we only have two ensembles quite far from the physical pion and kaon masses. Our best relative error (0.17%percent0.170.17%) is for the β=3.55𝛽3.55beta=3.55 set of ensembles where we have many pion and kaon masses and a very precise determination below the physical quark-mass point, making this an interpolation in pion mass rather than an extrapolation. Figure shows our result compared to other determinations from this set of ensembles found in the literature. These should not necessarily agree at finite lattice spacing as they define a “scheme” or “renormalization trajectory” along which the chosen observables approach the continuum limit. They should however tend to agree at finer lattice spacing (larger β𝛽beta), and here we observe some tension with citebib.bib15. For this plot we have added the statistical and systematic uncertainties of all determinations in quadrature. It is interesting to note that our determinations are quite different to those from fπ​Ksubscript𝑓𝜋𝐾fpi K citebib.bib5, or MΞsubscript𝑀ΞMXi citebib.bib7, and are more consistent with the determination of citebib.bib15 based on baryon masses. In Fig. we compare our determination for the continuum t0subscript𝑡0t0 for nf=2+1subscript𝑛𝑓21nf=2+1, against others found in the literature as well as the last FLAG value citebib.bib49. Our result is consistent with all previous determinations, but with a significantly smaller total uncertainty due to the precision we were able to achieve in the measured masses of the ΩΩOmega-baryon. The top three determinations in the plot are not independent as they use some of the same configurations and good consistency is seen between them.
content_sections.VI The parity-odd state and the Ω​(2012): In Tab. we listed both the parity-even and parity-odd ΩΩOmega masses. Converting these results to physical units using the lattice-spacings in Tab. gives the results for the negative-parity masses presented in Fig. . Some care is warranted in interpreting these data; at intermediate pion masses this appears to be a compact object lying well below Ξ∗​KsuperscriptΞ𝐾Xi*K, but at lower pion masses it approaches the (unstable) Ξ∗​KsuperscriptΞ𝐾Xi*K, where the Ξ∗superscriptΞXi* decays strongly into Ξ​πΞ𝜋Xipi. This is likely why our uncertainty grows for smaller pion masses (and the ease of fitting decreases). Note that the experimental width of the Ω​−Ωsuperscript2012Omega- is narrow as its decay is restricted only to the d-wave Ξ​KΞ𝐾Xi K citebib.bib19. We see that our MΩ(3/2)−superscriptsubscript𝑀Ωsuperscript32MOmega(3/2)- data is very consistent with the Ω​−Ωsuperscript2012Omega- and that there are few discernible discretisation effects when converted to physical units; this would be noticeably more severe if the scales from citebib.bib7 or citebib.bib5 were used. Unlike for the MΩ(3/2)+superscriptsubscript𝑀Ωsuperscript32MOmega(3/2)+ there are obvious discrepancies between X451 and N451, and H105 and N101, suggesting there is some non-negligible pion-based finite volume effect as well as one from the kaon; by eye these point in the same direction (reducing the mass in the infinite volume). Without having a rigorous fit-form at NLO we leave this result as a prediction of the quantum numbers of the Ω​−Ωsuperscript2012Omega- being I​(JP)=0​(3/2)−𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript32I(JP)=0(3/2)-, as indicated in prior lattice studies citebib.bib50.
content_sections.VII Conclusions: We have shown the viability of very high-precision scale-setting in lattice QCD via the positive-parity ground-state ΩΩOmega-baryon mass using a combination of gauge-fixed wall sources and the generalised Pencil of Functions technique. This allowed us to fit our data unambiguously at early times allowing for a near-unprecedented level of statistical resolution on the masses of the ΩΩOmega, below 0.1%percent0.10.1% relative statistical uncertainty for our largest boxes. This is thanks to the large volume average available from the Coulomb gauge-fixed wall sources and our liberal use of the truncated-solver method. Finding a reasonable chiral description of such precise data was a challenge, with the upshot that expressions at NLO in flavor-SU​SU3textSU chiral perturbation theory were needed. Our results for the lattice scales are pretty close in value and precision to previous NLO results, which were based on a global fit of baryon octet and decuplet masses on mostly the same CLS ensembles but with a different data set. The unexpected success of our chiral flavor-SU​SU3textSU extrapolation of the ΩΩOmega-mass suggests an extension of the analysis to additional CLS ensembles, in order to obtain more constraints on the LECs of meson-baryon systems. Such precision scale setting is urgently needed for lattice determinations of quantities of current particular interest such as the leading-order hadronic contribution to the muon g-2, which the community aims to resolve below the percent level including all systematics. Another avenue where such precision is warranted is in the tuning of heavy quark actions for the charm or bottom, where the overall scale ambiguity is large in comparison to the experimentally-determined mass-uncertainty of the states. We have achieved a much more precise determination of the intermediate scale-setting parameter t0subscript𝑡0sqrtt0 as well as lattice spacings with relative uncertainties at the level of 0.17%​…​0.32%percent0.17…percent0.320.17%dots 0.32%. Our lattice spacing determination on the finest ensembles (β=3.85𝛽3.85beta=3.85) is the least-precise and an ensemble at lighter pion mass, with large physical volume at this β𝛽beta would be needed to reduce this uncertainty. Otherwise, larger boxes closer to the physical pion and kaon mass in combination with better resolution of these masses and t0subscript𝑡0t0 will be necessary to improve these scale determinations. We close by illustrating our new lattice scale determinations being applied to our negative-parity ΩΩOmega-baryon masses. We find that, upon converting to physical units, no discernible cut-off dependence is seen, and that we obtain a state with the quantum numbers I​(JP)=0​(3/2−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃03superscript2I(JP)=0(3/2-) consistent with the experimental Ω​−Ωsuperscript2012Omega-.
content_sections.Acknowledgements: The authors would like to acknowledge useful discussions with Konstantin Ottnad and Simon Kuberski. In addition the authors thank Yonggoo Heo for cross-checking some of the expressions in Appendix A. RJH would particularly like to thank Hartmut Wittig for inspiring the completion of this project. We thank the CLS consortium for providing gauge conﬁgurations. D. M. acknowledges funding by the Heisenberg Programme of the Deutsche Forschungsgemein- schaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) – project number 454605793. Calculations for this project were partly performed on the HPC cluster “Mogon II” at JGU Mainz. This research was supported in part by the cluster computing resource provided by the IT Division at the GSI Helmholtzzentrum für Schwerionenforschung, Darmstadt, Germany (HPC cluster Virgo). For the light- and strange-quark propagator inversions we used the version 1.6 of OpenQCD citebib.bib51, QDP++ citebib.bib52, and a multi-grid solver implemented atop GRID citebib.bib53. For the Coulomb gauge fixing and the independent measurements of t0subscript𝑡0t0 we used the library GLU citebib.bib27. The GNU Scientific Library (GSL) citebib.bib54 was used for the analysis in this work.

all_papers.title:
Progress on nucleon transition matrix elements with a lattice QCD
  variational analysis
all_papers.abstract:
Nucleon weak matrix elements can be extracted from nucleon correlation
functions with lattice QCD simulations. The signal-to-noise ratio prohibits the
analysis at large source-sink separations and as a consequence, excited state
contamination affects the extraction of the nucleon matrix elements. Chiral
perturbation theory (ChPT) suggests that the dominant contamination in some of
these channels is due to $N\pi$ states where the pion carries the same momentum
of the current. In this talk, we report updates on the variational analysis
with $qqq$-operators (nucleon-like) and $(qqq)(\bar{q}q)$-operators
(nucleon-pion-like) where we report for the first time some preliminary results
of $\langle N\pi| \mathcal{J}| N \rangle $, modulo some kinematic and volume
factors, and we compare the results against ChPT. This pilot study is performed
on a CLS ensemble with $N_f=3$, $m_\pi \approx 420~\mathrm{MeV}$, $a\approx
0.1~\mathrm{fm}$ and $T=2L\approx 4.8~\mathrm{fm}$.

qualitative: Excited state contamination in nucleon matrix element calculations is a major challenge due to the deteriorating signal-to-noise ratio at large source-sink separations, limiting the ability to isolate the ground state.

Chiral perturbation theory (ChPT) predicts that the dominant excited state contamination in certain nucleon weak matrix element channels arises from Nπ states where the pion carries the same momentum as the current.

A variational analysis using both nucleon-like (qqq) and nucleon-pion-like ((qqq)(q̄q)) interpolating operators can help control excited state contamination by projecting onto the desired physical states.

The use of multi-state fits, informed by ChPT, is essential for accurately extracting nucleon form factors and verifying theoretical relations such as Pion Pole Dominance and the generalized Goldberger-Treiman (PCAC) relations.

Plateau fits (single- or two-state) are employed to extract matrix elements from correlation functions, with the goal of identifying a stable region (plateau) where the ground state dominates and excited state effects are minimized.

The variational method, particularly through the Generalized Eigenvalue Problem (GEVP), allows for the construction of optimal interpolating operators that project onto the ground and excited states, improving the control over contamination.
quantitative: The lattice ensemble used has $N_f=3$, $m_\pi \approx 420~\mathrm{MeV}$, $a \approx 0.1~\mathrm{fm}$, and $T = 2L \approx 4.8~\mathrm{fm}$.
The study presents the first preliminary results for the transition matrix element $\langle N\pi| \mathcal{J}| N \rangle$, up to kinematic and volume factors, in a finite volume.
The analysis focuses on the zero spatial momentum transfer case for the $\langle N\pi| \mathcal{J}| N \rangle$ matrix element.
ChPT predicts that the dominant contribution to excited state contamination comes from $N\pi$ states with the pion momentum equal to the current momentum, at leading order.
The signal-to-noise ratio problem makes it impractical to analyze nucleon correlation functions at large source-sink separations, where excited state contamination would decay exponentially.

content_sections.1 Introduction: The next-generation neutrino oscillation experiments DUNE citebib.bib1, bib.bib2 and Hyper-Kamiokande citebib.bib3 will address fundamental questions in particle physics and cosmology, including whether neutrinos oscillate differently from antineutrinos. CP violation in the lepton sector citebib.bib4 is necessary for a possible Standard Model explanation of the matter-antimatter asymmetry in our universe citebib.bib5. Other prospects for physics searches at DUNE include more precise studies of the neutrino mass hierarchy, supernova neutrino bursts citebib.bib6, and BSM physics citebib.bib7. Of fundamental importance are the parameters that describe the cross sections of neutrino-nucleus scattering data, as very high precision is required in the analysis of such rare weak processes. Nuclear models relate the neutrino-nucleus scattering cross-section, whose events occur at the detector level, to the neutrino-nucleon scattering cross-section, which is parametrised in terms of nucleon form factors citebib.bib8, bib.bib9. MiniBooNE, a previous terrestrial neutrino oscillation experiment, observed a significant production of nucleon-pion final states (CC-1π𝜋pi) in addition to quasielastic charged current processes (CCQE) citebib.bib10. These additional states are produced indirectly via resonance states, such as νμ+n→μ−+Δ+(→π++n)→subscript𝜈𝜇𝑛annotatedsuperscript𝜇superscriptΔ→absentsuperscript𝜋𝑛numu+ntomu-+Delta+(topi++n) in the intermediate energy range Eν≈0.1−20​GeVsubscript𝐸𝜈0.120GeVEnuapprox 0.1-20GeV citebib.bib11. Achieving a few-percent accuracy in determining (anti)neutrino oscillation parameters requires precise knowledge of the electroweak transitions N→Δ→𝑁ΔNtoDelta, N→N∗→𝑁superscript𝑁Nto N* and N→N​π→𝑁𝑁𝜋Nto Npi in the non-perturbative regime citebib.bib12. First principles calculations based on lattice QCD simulations can provide the form factors that enter in the cross-sections citebib.bib13, bib.bib14. Nucleon form factors are extracted from ratios of nucleon three- and two-point functions computed using Monte Carlo techniques. A major challenge of lattice nucleon structure calculations is the problem of excited state contamination. In principle, this can be overcome by evaluating nucleon correlation functions at large source-sink separations, as the excited state contamination decays exponentially with the Euclidean time separation. Unfortunately, the signal-to-noise ratio deteriorates with the distance between nucleon operators, making it impractical to analyse the data at long distances. A complete variational analysis which involves constructing the nucleon spectrum using the Lüscher method citebib.bib15, bib.bib16 and then using the GEVP solutions to diagonalise the interpolating operators citebib.bib17, bib.bib18, can potentially solve this problem. This more robust approach requires an analysis with both single- (N𝑁N) and multi-hadron (N​π𝑁𝜋Npi, N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi, …) operators, making lattice simulations demanding due to the increased number of Wick contractions and Dirac propagators. Chiral perturbation theory (ChPT) can guide us in understanding which states contribute the most to the contamination of the nucleon three-point functions and, consequently, which interpolating operators are most relevant for the variational analysis. A reliable estimation of the excited state contamination and other systematic effects is essential for the accurate extraction of the N→N→𝑁𝑁Nto N electroweak form factors. At leading order in ChPT, it was found that N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states in the kinematic configuration where the pion carries the same momentum as the current represent the dominant contribution citebib.bib19, bib.bib20. Other N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states as well as N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi and nucleon excitations will also be relevant to reach few percent accuracy. Multistate fits informed by ChPT have proven to be successful in determining form factors that satisfy the Pion Pole Dominance (PPD) and the generalised Goldberger-Treiman (also known as PCAC) relations citebib.bib21, bib.bib22. In this study, we carry out a variational analysis utilising just a single q​q​q𝑞𝑞𝑞qqq (three-quark or N𝑁N-like) interpolating operator and a single (q​q​q)​(q¯​q)𝑞𝑞𝑞¯𝑞𝑞(qqq)(barqq) (five-quark or N​π𝑁𝜋Npi-like) interpolating operator. Specifically, we consider the N​π𝑁𝜋Npi-like operator where the pion carries the same momentum as the current, aiming to verify the ChPT prediction regarding its large contribution. We compute nucleon matrix elements ⟨N|𝒥|N⟩quantum-operator-product𝑁𝒥𝑁 N|J|N, previously presented in citebib.bib23 and, for the first time, we present preliminary results for positive-parity transition matrix elements ⟨N​π|𝒥|N⟩quantum-operator-product𝑁𝜋𝒥𝑁 Npi|J|N in a finite volume, up to some kinematic factors. Here, |N⟩ket𝑁|N denotes the nucleon ground state and |N​π⟩ket𝑁𝜋|Npi a physical nucleon-pion finite volume state. This work provides an update of the results of citebib.bib24, bib.bib23. In sec. , we provide an overview of the extraction of nucleon form factors from nucleon matrix elements using the standard ratio method, along with a discussion of key results from ChPT in these channels. In sec. , we discuss how we use the variational analysis to determine the matrix elements by projecting the operators to the desired eigenstate. In sec. , we present preliminary results for ⟨N​π|𝒥|N⟩quantum-operator-product𝑁𝜋𝒥𝑁 Npi|J|N at zero spatial momentum transfer. Finally, in sec. , we draw conclusions and discuss key observations of this analysis.

Due to this constraint, the extraction of nucleon form factors from the ratio in eq. is affected by excited state contamination (ESC). To resolve the nucleon ground state, one needs a robust method that takes into account all the relevant excited states.
content_sections.2 Nucleon matrix elements 2.2 N​π𝑁𝜋N\\pi: ChPT can provide guidance on the contribution of specific states to the correlation functions. Since ChPT is an expansion for small pion masses and momenta, its predictions are most reliable towards physical pion masses and small momenta. In citebib.bib25, it was estimated with leading order in ChPT (LO-ChPT) that the contamination of the tower of N​(𝐩)​π​(−𝐩)𝑁𝐩𝜋𝐩N(p)pi(-p) states to the zero-momentum nucleon two-point function is at the level of a few percent for close to physical pion masses (≈200​MeVabsent200MeVapprox 200MeV) and source-sink separations of t≈0.5​fm𝑡0.5fmtapprox 0.5fm. The contribution of N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi states to the nucleon two-point functions is estimated to be even smaller at the per mille level citebib.bib26. Multiparticle states are usually suppressed by a volume factor for each additional state. While LO-ChPT predicts that N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states do not contribute much to the nucleon two-point functions at sufficently large distance, their contribution to the nucleon three-point functions can be very large. In citebib.bib19, bib.bib20, it was predicted with LO-ChPT that the N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states contribution depends on the momentum transfer Q2superscript𝑄2Q2 and it can rise up to 40%percent4040% for physical pion masses at t=2​fm𝑡2fmt=2fm. Most of this contribution is due to N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states which are either created at the source or at the sink and where the pion has the same momentum as the current. These current-enhanced diagrams are not volume suppressed. More explicitly, in lattice simulations the nucleon operators at the sink are usually projected to 𝐩′=𝟎superscript𝐩′0pprime=0, and thus the momentum of the source operator is 𝐩=−𝐪𝐩𝐪p=-q by momentum conservation. With these kinematics, the dominant N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states at the sink have total momentum 𝐩′=𝟎superscript𝐩′0pprime=0 and are in the momentum configuration N​(−𝐪)​π​(𝐪)𝑁𝐪𝜋𝐪N(-q)pi(q), while at the source they are in the momentum configuration N​​π​(−𝐪)𝑁0𝜋𝐪Npi(-q). In the forward limit (𝐪=𝟎𝐪0q=0), ChPT predicts still a large N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states contamination in specific channels when the sink and source nucleon operators have non-zero momentum 𝐩′=𝐩≠𝟎superscript𝐩′𝐩0pprime=pneq0, see citebib.bib21. This was confirmed numerically in citebib.bib23. The remaining tower of interacting N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states with other relative momenta and N​​π​​π​𝑁0𝜋0𝜋0Npipi states can further contribute to contaminate the nucleon three-point correlation functions up to few percent at the physical point, and they must be taken into account for high-precision determination of the nucleon form factors. Resonance contributions in a finite volume like the Roper in the positive-parity nucleon channel must also be considered and effective field theories provide guidance on the interacting energy levels citebib.bib27.

In particular, multi-hadron operators must be constructed with relative momenta up to some value, above which their effect is expected to be negligible. This would give more confidence that no relevant operator is missing in the variational basis and that the eigenvectors and eigenvalues are correct up to negligible exponential corrections. Only then, the GEVP eigenvectors can be used to construct GEVP-projected two-point and three-point functions. An example of systematic effects in the reconstruction of the spectra due to missing relevant operators in the GEVP basis can be seen for example in Fig.6 of citebib.bib30 in the ρ𝜌rho-resonance channel. While this approach of reconstructing the spectrum is very sound, current algorithms make it impractical to realise. The difficulty lies in the computation of many all-to-all Dirac propagators that are needed to evaluate two-point and three-point functions for two- and three-particle operators. In addition, the computational expense of computing a symmetric matrix of two-point or three-point functions increases with n×(n+1)/2𝑛𝑛12ntimes(n+1)/2, with n𝑛n being the number of operators in the basis. Therefore, efficient algorithms to compute multi-hadron correlation functions are required. In this work, we use the sequential method citebib.bib31 along with stochastic methods citebib.bib32 to compute the Dirac propagators. Unfortunately, the former method is not the most efficient way to compute these multi-hadron correlation functions as many sequential propagators are required to take into account for instance the different momentum combinations and polarizations. There are promising and versatile alternatives to the methods used in this work like distillation citebib.bib33, stochastic distillation citebib.bib34, and sparsening methods. The latter is employed in citebib.bib35 to include N​π𝑁𝜋Npi and N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi operators in the variational analysis. Although the final goal must be to include all the relevant operators in the variational basis, investigations of the variational analysis with the most relevant operators can still provide quite accurate results on the final quantities of interest. As we discussed in the previous section, ChPT can be used to predict which are the most relevant operators for a given specific channel. In particular, LO-ChPT predicts that N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states play an important role in the determination of pseudoscalar and axial-vector form factors from nucleon three-point functions at source-sink separations that are can currently be reached (≈1.5​fmabsent1.5fmapprox 1.5fm). Motivated by these different studies, we construct a basis with just two operators that have very large overlap with N𝑁N and N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states, respectively. We then compute a 2×2222times 2 matrix of two-point functions to find the GEVP eigenvalues and eigenvectors. The latter are then used to project the interpolating operators to the GEVP eigenstates N𝑁N and N​π𝑁𝜋Npi, and to extract ⟨N|𝒥|N⟩quantum-operator-product𝑁𝒥𝑁 N|J|N, ⟨N​π|𝒥|N⟩quantum-operator-product𝑁𝜋𝒥𝑁 Npi|J|N, by means of eq. or , respectively.

We neglect the lower diagonal entry ⟨O5​q​(𝐩′,t)​𝒥​(𝐪,τ)​O¯5​q​(𝐩,0)⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO5𝑞superscript𝐩′𝑡𝒥𝐪𝜏subscript¯O5𝑞𝐩0O5q(pprime,t)J(q,tau)barO5q(p,0) because it corresponds to N​π→N​π→𝑁𝜋𝑁𝜋Npito Npi terms, which are expected to be volume suppressed in the standard three-point functions ⟨O3​q​(𝐩′,t)​𝒥​(𝐪,τ)​O¯3​q​(𝐩,0)⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO3𝑞superscript𝐩′𝑡𝒥𝐪𝜏subscript¯O3𝑞𝐩0O3q(pprime,t)J(q,tau)barO3q(p,0), and therefore their contribution will be much smaller. Also, it is numerically more demanding to compute these correlation functions with our methods as it involves several Wick contractions and all-to-all propagators. The off-diagonal entries in eq. correspond to the current-enhanced terms (when allowed) in the standard three-point functions, i. e. , N→N​π→𝑁𝑁𝜋Nto Npi and N​π→N→𝑁𝜋𝑁Npito N states. The Wick contractions of ⟨O5​q​(𝐩′,t)​𝒥​(𝐪,τ)​O¯3​q​(𝐩,0)⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO5𝑞superscript𝐩′𝑡𝒥𝐪𝜏subscript¯O3𝑞𝐩0O5q(pprime,t)J(q,tau)barO3q(p,0) were first discussed in citebib.bib24, and they can be grouped in 4 diagrams: A, B, C, and D, see Fig. . The diagrams contain 2 all-to-all propagators and we adopt the sequential method citebib.bib31 with 2 sequential propagators to compute the diagrams A, B, and C. The diagram D is much cheaper to compute because we use the point-to-all method for the nucleon part and the one-end-trick citebib.bib32 with 12 stochastic vectors for the pion-to-current term. In particular, our lattice simulations show that for the diagram D ⟨O5​q​(𝐩′,t)​𝒥​(𝐪,τ)​O¯3​q​(𝐩,0)⟩D≈⟨O3​q​(𝐩N′,t)​O¯3​q​(𝐩,0)⟩​⟨Oq¯​q​(𝐩π′,t)​𝒥​(𝐪,τ)⟩. subscriptdelimited-⟨⟩subscriptO5𝑞superscript𝐩′𝑡𝒥𝐪𝜏subscript¯O3𝑞𝐩0𝐷delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO3𝑞subscriptsuperscript𝐩′𝑁𝑡subscript¯O3𝑞𝐩0delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO¯𝑞𝑞subscriptsuperscript𝐩′𝜋𝑡𝒥𝐪𝜏O5q(pprime,t)J(q,tau)barO3q(p,0)DapproxO3q(pprimeN,t)barO3q(p,0)Obarqq(pprimepi,t)J(q,tau). This factorisation, together with the observation that the diagram D has the largest signal, explains which channels have the large N​π𝑁𝜋Npi contamination in standard nucleon three-point functions. This explains the large (N​π𝑁𝜋Npi) excited state contamination in the 𝒜4subscript𝒜4A4 channel, and the milder excited state contamination in the channel with 𝒜isubscript𝒜𝑖Ai and qi=0subscript𝑞𝑖0qi=0, where the momentum of the current is zero along the direction where the spin is aligned and the current component, see Fig.4 of citebib.bib21. Similar arguments hold with a pseudoscalar current. In order to use the variational method, we compute the 2×2222times 2 GEVP matrix ℂ2​p​t​(𝐩,t)=(⟨O3​q​(𝐩,t)​O¯3​q​(𝐩,0)⟩⟨O3​q​(𝐩,t)​O¯5​q​(𝐩,0)⟩⟨O5​q​(𝐩,t)​O¯3​q​(𝐩,0)⟩⟨O5​q​(𝐩,t)​O¯5​q​(𝐩,0)⟩),subscriptℂ2𝑝𝑡𝐩𝑡matrixdelimited-⟨⟩subscriptO3𝑞𝐩𝑡subscript¯O3𝑞𝐩0delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO3𝑞𝐩𝑡subscript¯O5𝑞𝐩0delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO5𝑞𝐩𝑡subscript¯O3𝑞𝐩0delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO5𝑞𝐩𝑡subscript¯O5𝑞𝐩0C2pt(p,t)=beginpmatrixO3q(p,t)barO3q(p,0)O3q(p,t)barO5q(p,0) O5q(p,t)barO3q(p,0)O5q(p,t)barO5q(p,0) endpmatrix, see discussion in sec. . The correlation functions ⟨O3​q​(𝐩,t)​O¯5​q​(𝐩,0)⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO3𝑞𝐩𝑡subscript¯O5𝑞𝐩0O3q(p,t)barO5q(p,0) are constructed from ⟨O3​q​(𝐩′,t)​𝒥​(𝐪,τ)​O¯3​q​(𝐩,0)⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO3𝑞superscript𝐩′𝑡𝒥𝐪𝜏subscript¯O3𝑞𝐩0O3q(pprime,t)J(q,tau)barO3q(p,0) with 𝒥=𝒫𝒥𝒫J=P, τ=0𝜏0tau=0, and at different source-sink separations t𝑡t. Similarly, the (N​π𝑁𝜋Npi-like) two-point correlation functions ⟨O5​q​(𝐩,t)​O¯5​q​(𝐩,0)⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO5𝑞𝐩𝑡subscript¯O5𝑞𝐩0O5q(p,t)barO5q(p,0) are constructed from the three-point functions ⟨O5​q​(𝐩′,t)​𝒥​(𝐪,τ)​O¯3​q​(𝐩,0)⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscriptO5𝑞superscript𝐩′𝑡𝒥𝐪𝜏subscript¯O3𝑞𝐩0O5q(pprime,t)J(q,tau)barO3q(p,0); to visualise this construction see Fig. with the current 𝒥=𝒫𝒥𝒫J=P at the source (τ=0𝜏0tau=0). The sequential method, which is used in this pilot study, is not the most efficient approach and in the future other methods like distillation citebib.bib33 will be considered, especially when N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi operators will be included in the basis. In Fig. , there is a comparison plot between the diagrams A, B, C, and D that make up the N​π𝑁𝜋Npi-like two-point functions and the standard nucleon-like two-point functions. On the right plot, the effective energies of the diagrams and their sum are compared against N𝑁N, N​π𝑁𝜋Npi and N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi energies in the non-interacting limit. The operators are projected to total momentum zero in these plots. While the diagrams A and D decay with some interacting N​π𝑁𝜋Npi energy which is very close to the non-interacting N​π𝑁𝜋Npi energy (or N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi energy as they are degenerate in this finite volume), the diagrams B and C decay quite fast with the nucleon energy. This behaviour is expected, see e. g. Fig.7 of citebib.bib42, as the diagrams A and D are constructed from 5 propagators travelling from time 00 to time t𝑡t, while the diagrams B and C contain just 3 propagators from time 00 to time t𝑡t and 2 propagators from time t𝑡t to time t𝑡t, see Fig. . Eventually, the sum of all the diagrams that compose the N​π𝑁𝜋Npi-like two-point function will decay at large time with the nucleon energy as the N​π𝑁𝜋Npi operators are constructed to have the quantum numbers of the nucleon.
content_sections.4 Lattice simulations 4.3 GEVP results: We solve the GEVP in eq. using the matrix in eq. to determine the matrix of eigenvectors V​(𝐩,t;t0)𝑉𝐩𝑡subscript𝑡0V(p,t;t0) and eigenvalues Λ​(𝐩,t;t0)Λ𝐩𝑡subscript𝑡0Lambda(p,t;t0). In Fig. , the GEVP results at t0=0.2​fmsubscript𝑡00.2fmt0=0.2fm are plotted for 𝐩=𝟎𝐩0p=0 and 𝐩=𝐞𝐢𝐩subscript𝐞𝐢p=ei (|𝐩|≈520​MeV𝐩520MeV|p|approx 520MeV). For the kinematic frame with momentum 𝐩𝐩p, we use both the 5​q5𝑞5q-operators in eqs. -. On the left plots, the effective energies of the 2 GEVP eigenvalues are compared to the N𝑁N, N​π𝑁𝜋Npi and N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi energies in the non-interacting limit. The nucleon and pion energies are extracted at large time from nucleon and pion two-point functions. In the rest frame with zero total momentum, the energy of the first eigenvalue matches the nucleon energy. The second eigenvalue has an energy very close to that of the non-interacting N​π𝑁𝜋Npi P-wave state and the N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi S-wave state, which are (quasi-)degenerate in this ensemble. This comparison between N​π𝑁𝜋Npi and N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi states in the non-interacting limit varies with the pion mass and the physical volume, see e. g. Fig.2 in citebib.bib43. In the moving frames with total momentum 𝐩𝐩p, the first eigenvalue is again consistent with the nucleon state and the second eigenvalue is more clearly a N​π𝑁𝜋Npi state, due to the non-degeneracy of the N​π𝑁𝜋Npi and N​π​π𝑁𝜋𝜋Npipi states in the non-interacting limit. We thus refer to the two GEVP eigenvalues as λNsuperscript𝜆𝑁lambdaN and λN​πsuperscript𝜆𝑁𝜋lambdaNpi. The GEVP results for the moving frames in Fig. are averaged over equivalent momenta (𝐩=ex,ey,ez,−ex,−ey,−ez𝐩subscript𝑒𝑥subscript𝑒𝑦subscript𝑒𝑧subscript𝑒𝑥subscript𝑒𝑦subscript𝑒𝑧p=hatex,hatey,hatez,-hatex,-hatey,-hatez). This degeneracy could in principle affect the GEVP eigensolutions and must be taken into account to reduce systematic effects. On the right plots, the components of the normalised GEVP eigenvectors are presented. This relation should hold up to 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)-effects as the intermediate currents are not 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)-improved. The ratio of matrix elements is equivalent to the double ratio Rk,𝒫​(𝐩,t;𝟎,τ)N​π​NRk,𝒜4​(𝐩,t;𝟎,τ)N​π​N=C3​p​tk,𝒫​(𝐩,t;𝟎,τ)N​π​NC3​p​tk,𝒜4​(𝐩,t;𝟎,τ)N​π​Nsuperscript𝑅𝑘𝒫superscript𝐩𝑡0𝜏𝑁𝜋𝑁superscript𝑅𝑘subscript𝒜4superscript𝐩𝑡0𝜏𝑁𝜋𝑁subscriptsuperscript𝐶𝑘𝒫3𝑝𝑡superscript𝐩𝑡0𝜏𝑁𝜋𝑁subscriptsuperscript𝐶𝑘subscript𝒜43𝑝𝑡superscript𝐩𝑡0𝜏𝑁𝜋𝑁fracRk,P(p,t;0,tau)Npi NRk,A4(p,t;0,tau)Npi N=fracCk,P3pt(p,t;0,tau)Npi NCk,A43pt(p,t;0,tau)Npi N, and the two ratios are plotted on the right panel in Fig. . It is reassuring to see that they are not completely in disagreement with ChPT as 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)-effects and S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU symmetry effects must be still taken into account. These transition matrix elements are at a momentum transfer Q2=mπ2≈0.175​GeV2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋20.175superscriptGeV2Q2=mpi2approx 0.175GeV2. Results for the matrix elements with 𝐪≠𝟎𝐪0qneq0, which are of paramount importance for the analysis of the neutrino oscillation data and more, will be presented in a separate publication.
content_sections.5 Conclusions: Elastic nucleon form factors N→N→𝑁𝑁Nto N, as well as transition form factors for N→Δ→𝑁ΔNtoDelta, N→N∗→𝑁superscript𝑁Nto N* and N→N​π→𝑁𝑁𝜋Nto Npi are of paramount importance for analysis of neutrino-nucleus scattering data in the resonance region production. In this study, we used a variational analysis to explicitly remove the N→N​π→𝑁𝑁𝜋Nto Npi matrix elements from the standard nucleon-like three-point functions. As a by-product, we present some preliminary results for the transition matrix elements N→N​π→𝑁𝑁𝜋Nto Npi at vanishing spatial momentum transfer for the isospin transition 1/2→1/2→12121/2to 1/2. Results for the elastic nucleon form factors were presented in citebib.bib23. We find that with the GEVP analysis the ratios tend to be surprisingly flat at source-sink separations ≳0.8​fmgreater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent0.8fmgtrsim 0.8fm, which allowed a simple constant fit to the data at the largest available source-sink separation. The results suffer from 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a) effects as the currents are not 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)-improved. The ratio of 𝒫𝒫P and 𝒜4subscript𝒜4A4 matrix elements is found to be within ∼10%similar-toabsentpercent10sim 10% of the ChPT prediction. Other results at non-vanishing momentum transfer and with other currents will be presented in a separate publication. Our lattice simulations are performed on a single Nf=3subscript𝑁𝑓3Nf=3 ensemble with lattice spacing a=0.1​fm𝑎0.1fma=0.1fm, mπ=420​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜋420MeVmpi=420MeV and T=2​L=4.8​fm𝑇2𝐿4.8fmT=2L=4.8fm. The S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU symmetry introduces another small complication as the N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states are degenerate to Λ​KΛ𝐾Lambda K states. Although we expect this to not have any large effect in the three-point functions, they might have some few percent effect in the nucleon two-point functions and therefore they can introduce some small systematic effects in the GEVP eigensolutions. This work represents only the first step towards the calculation of transition form factors with a more complete variational analysis, where single-hadron and multi-hadron operators must be included in a Lüscher formalism to reconstruct the spectra. The results confirm the ChPT prediction of very large N​π𝑁𝜋Npi states contamination in some nucleon-like three-point functions and we emphasize that one particular lattice diagram (D) is the most important one.

all_papers.title:
Searching for the first radial excitation of the $\Delta(1232)$ in
  lattice QCD
all_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice QCD analysis of the $ \Delta $-baryon spectrum, with the
goal of finding the position of the $ 2s $ radial excitation of the $
\Delta(1232) $ ground state. Using smeared three-quark operators in a
correlation matrix analysis, we report masses for the ground, first and second
excited states of the $ J^P = 3/2^+ $ spectrum across a broad range of $
m_\pi^2 $. We identify the lowest lying state as being a $ 1s $ state,
consistent with the well known $ \Delta(1232) $. The first excitation is
identified as a $ 2s $ state, but is found to have a mass of approximately
2.15~GeV on our $ \sim3 $ fm lattice, which does not appear to be associated
with the $ \Delta(1600) $ resonance in a significant manner. We also report on
the spin-$ 1/2 $ and odd-parity states accessible via our methods. The large
excitation energies of the radial excitations provide a potential resolution to
the long-standing missing baryon resonances problem.

sections.2: Gell-Mann, The Eightfold Way: A Theory of strong interaction symmetry 10.2172/4008239 (1961).
sections.4: Luscher, Volume Dependence of the Energy Spectrum in Massive Quantum Field Theories
sections.8: Michael, Adjoint Sources in Lattice Gauge Theory, Nucl
sections.13: Michael, Adjoint Sources in Lattice Gauge Theory, Nucl
sections.16: Parisi, The Strategy for Computing the Hadronic Mass Spectrum, Phys
